### Step 3 — Compute $\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial \theta_j}$

Let $z = \theta^T x$, so $h = \sigma(z)$, and using the sigmoid derivative identity:

$$\frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta_j} = \sigma'(z) \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial \theta_j} = \sigma(z)\bigl(1-\sigma(z)\bigr) \cdot x_j = h(1-h)\, x_j$$

### Step 4 — Substitute back and simplify

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \left( -\frac{y}{h} + \frac{1-y}{1-h} \right) \cdot h(1-h)\, x_j$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \bigl( -y(1-h) + (1-y)h \bigr) x_j = (h - y) x_j$$

### Step 5 — Average over all $m$ examples

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \bigl( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \bigr) x_j^{(i)}$$

### Step 6 — Apply the gradient descent update rule

$$\boxed{\;\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \bigl( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \bigr) x_j^{(i)}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remarkable Insight**: The gradient formula for logistic regression has *exactly the same form* as for linear regression, but $h_\theta(x)$ differs (sigmoid vs identity). Examiners love to highlight this elegant parallel.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Vectorized Form for Batch Implementation

Define the design matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (n+1)}$ and label vector $y \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$. The vectorized operations are:

$$Z = X \theta \quad \Longrightarrow \quad H = \sigma(Z)$$

$$\nabla J = \frac{1}{m}\, X^T (H - y)$$

$$\theta := \theta - \alpha\, \nabla J$$

> This avoids explicit loops and enables efficient computation on GPUs.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Probability Bounds of Log-Loss

To understand why log-loss works, examine its two cases:

- When $y = 1$: cost $= -\log(h)$. If $h \to 0$, cost $\to +\infty$ (heavy penalty for confident wrong prediction).
- When $y = 0$: cost $= -\log(1-h)$. If $h \to 1$, cost $\to +\infty$.

This **infinite penalty for confident misclassifications** is what makes the log-loss so well-suited for classification.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Logistic Regression Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Feature Vector x] --> B[Linear Combination z = theta transpose x]
    B --> C[Apply Sigmoid Function sigma z]
    C --> D[Predicted Probability h theta x]
    D --> E{Threshold at 0.5}
    E -->|h greater than 0.5| F[Predict Class 1]
    E -->|h less than 0.5| G[Predict Class 0]
    H[True Label y] --> I[Compute Log Loss]
    D --> I
    I --> J[Compute Gradient]
    J --> K[Update theta via Gradient Descent]
    K --> B
```

## 4.2 Sigmoid Function Shape (Conceptual Mermaid Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Large Negative z] --> B[h approaches 0]
    C[z equals 0] --> D[h equals 0.5]
    E[Large Positive z] --> F[h approaches 1]
    G[Sigmoid curve smoothly transitions] --> B
    G --> D
    G --> F
```

## 4.3 Decision Boundary Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feature Space 2D] --> B{Linear Features}
    A --> C{Polynomial Features}
    B --> D[Straight Line Boundary]
    C --> E[Curved Non Linear Boundary]
    D --> F[Class 1 Region]
    D --> G[Class 0 Region]
    E --> F
    E --> G
```

## 4.4 Training Loop (Iterative Optimization Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize theta randomly to 0] --> B[Forward Pass compute h theta x]
    B --> C[Compute Cost J theta]
    C --> D[Backward Pass compute gradients]
    D --> E[Update Parameters]
    E --> F{Converged or Max Iter}
    F -->|No| B
    F -->|Yes| G[Output Trained Model]
```

## 4.5 Multiclass Extension (One-vs-Rest Strategy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Multiclass Dataset with K classes] --> B[Train Classifier 1 vs Rest]
    A --> C[Train Classifier 2 vs Rest]
    A --> D[Train Classifier K vs Rest]
    B --> E[Probability for Class 1]
    C --> F[Probability for Class 2]
    D --> G[Probability for Class K]
    E --> H[Argmax Selection]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Final Predicted Class]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**Q1. Why is the sigmoid function used in logistic regression instead of the identity function?**

**Model Answer:**
The identity function used in linear regression outputs values in $(-\infty, +\infty)$, which cannot represent a probability bounded in $[0, 1]$. The sigmoid function $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ squashes any real number into the range $(0, 1)$, making the output interpretable as a probability. Additionally, the sigmoid has a smooth, easily differentiable form: $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$, which enables efficient gradient-based optimization. It also has the property that its output represents the log-odds linearly: $\log\bigl(\frac{P(y=1)}{1-P(y=1)}\bigr) = \theta^T x$. **(3 marks)**

---

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**Q2. What is the cost function used in logistic regression? Why is Mean Squared Error (MSE) not used?**

**Model Answer:**
Logistic regression uses the **Binary Cross-Entropy (Log-Loss)** cost function:

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \Bigl[ y^{(i)} \log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1-h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \Bigr]$$

MSE is not used because when combined with the sigmoid, it produces a **non-convex** cost surface, leading to multiple local minima and unreliable convergence. Log-loss is **convex**, guaranteeing convergence to the global optimum. It also penalizes confident misclassifications heavily (penalty $\to \infty$), which aligns with the goals of classification. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Derive the cost function for logistic regression from the Maximum Likelihood Estimation principle. Also derive the gradient descent update rule.**

**(a) Derivation of cost function from MLE (7 marks)**

**Step 1**: Model the probability of a single training example:

$$P(y \mid x; \theta) = h^y (1-h)^{(1-y)} \quad \text{where } h = h_\theta(x)$$

**Step 2**: Likelihood over $m$ i.i.d. samples:

$$L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} h^{(i)y^{(i)}} (1-h^{(i)})^{1-y^{(i)}}$$

**Step 3**: Take the log-likelihood (monotonic transformation):

$$\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \Bigl[ y^{(i)} \log h^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1-h^{(i)}) \Bigr]$$

**Step 4**: Define cost as negative mean log-likelihood:

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \ell(\theta)$$

**Justification**: MLE maximizes likelihood. The negative sign converts it to a minimization problem, and dividing by $m$ makes it independent of dataset size. The resulting function is convex in $\theta$, ensuring gradient descent converges to the global minimum. **(7 marks — full working must be shown)**

**(b) Derivation of gradient descent update rule (7 marks)**

**Step 1**: Differentiate cost for one example with respect to $\theta_j$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = -\left[ \frac{y}{h} - \frac{1-y}{1-h} \right] \frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta_j}$$

**Step 2**: Use sigmoid derivative $\frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta_j} = h(1-h)x_j$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = -\left[ \frac{y - h}{h(1-h)} \right] h(1-h) x_j = (h - y) x_j$$

**Step 3**: Average across $m$ examples:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 4**: Apply gradient descent update:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$$

**Remark**: The gradient has the *same form* as linear regression, but with sigmoid-based $h$. **(7 marks — final boxed equation is mandatory for full marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Explain the logistic regression algorithm in detail. Discuss the sigmoid function, hypothesis representation, decision boundary, and the role of regularization.**

**(a) Algorithm explanation with sigmoid, hypothesis, and decision boundary (7 marks)**

**Sigmoid Function**: $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ squashes any real number into $(0,1)$. It has a characteristic S-shape, is differentiable, and satisfies $\sigma(-z) = 1 - \sigma(z)$.

**Hypothesis Representation**: $h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x) = P(y=1 \mid x; \theta)$. Output is interpreted as the probability that the sample belongs to class 1.

**Decision Boundary**: Defined by $\theta^T x = 0$ (when threshold = 0.5). For linear features, this is a straight line/hyperplane. By engineering polynomial features ($x_1^2, x_1 x_2$, etc.), the boundary becomes non-linear. The class is predicted as 1 if $h_\theta(x) \geq 0.5$, else 0. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Regularization and parameter optimization (7 marks)**

**L2 Regularized Cost**:

$$J_R(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \Bigl[ y^{(i)} \log h^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1-h^{(i)}) \Bigr] + \frac{\lambda}{2m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \theta_j^2$$

Note: The bias term $\theta_0$ is typically *not* regularized.

**Why Regularize?**
- Prevents overfitting when features are highly correlated or numerous
- Shrinks weights toward zero, reducing model variance
- $\lambda$ controls the bias-variance trade-off:
  - Large $\lambda$ → underfitting (high bias)
  - Small $\lambda$ → overfitting (high variance)

**L1 Regularization** uses $|\theta_j|$ instead of $\theta_j^2$ and produces sparse models (feature selection).

**Optimization Methods**:
1. **Batch Gradient Descent** — uses entire dataset per update (stable, slow for large $m$)
2. **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** — uses one example per update (fast, noisy)
3. **Mini-Batch GD** — compromise between batch and SGD
4. **Newton-Raphson / IRLS** — second-order, faster convergence

**Convergence Criteria**: Stop when $|J^{(t)} - J^{(t-1)}| < \epsilon$ or after a maximum number of iterations. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the cost function — it MUST be negative log-likelihood, not positive.
> 2. **Using MSE** with logistic regression — this is a guaranteed 0–1 mark hit; always justify using cross-entropy.
> 3. **Not averaging by $m$** in the gradient formula — KTU strictly checks this.
> 4. **Confusing $\theta^T x$ with $\theta x$** — they are not the same when $\theta$ and $x$ are vectors.
> 5. **Skipping the derivation steps** in 14-mark questions — show EVERY algebraic step; no shortcuts.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention that the cost is convex** in logistic regression — this is a key justification for gradient descent.
> 7. **Mixing up L1 and L2 regularization** — L1 gives sparsity, L2 gives shrinkage; remember the difference.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this in your brain before the exam):**

- ☐ Logistic Regression is a **classification**, not regression, algorithm.
- ☐ Sigmoid: $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$; derivative: $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$.
- ☐ Hypothesis: $h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x) \in (0, 1)$.
- ☐ Cost function: **Binary Cross-Entropy / Log-Loss** (NOT MSE).
- ☐ Cost is **convex** in $\theta$ — guarantees global minimum via gradient descent.
- ☐ Gradient formula: $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum (h - y) x_j$ — same form as linear regression!
- ☐ Update rule: $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$.
- ☐ Decision boundary: $\theta^T x = 0$ at threshold 0.5; can be made non-linear via feature engineering.
- ☐ MLE derivation: $P(y\mid x) = h^y(1-h)^{1-y}$ → log-likelihood → negative mean = cost.
- ☐ **L1 regularization** (Lasso) → sparsity; **L2 regularization** (Ridge) → shrinkage.
- ☐ Regularization term: $\frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum \theta_j^2$ (exclude $\theta_0$ from regularization).
- ☐ Multiclass extension: **One-vs-Rest (OvR)** or **Softmax Regression**.
- ☐ **Odds** = $\frac{P}{1-P} = e^{\theta^T x}$ → log-odds = $\theta^T x$ (linear in features).
- ☐ Three optimization variants: **Batch GD**, **Stochastic GD**, **Mini-Batch GD**.
- ☐ Heavy penalty for confident misclassifications — log-loss punishes $h \to 0$ when $y=1$ with infinite cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner Tip**: In KTU 14-mark derivations, ALWAYS box the final equation. Examiners use boxed answers as visual anchors during valuation — an unboxed answer may receive partial credit even if the derivation is correct.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support Vector Machines (SVM), Unsupervised Learning: Basics of Unsupervised Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST412 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Support Vector Machines (SVM), Unsupervised Learning: Basics of Unsupervised Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Support Vector Machines (SVM)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a **supervised binary (and extendable to multi-class) discriminative classifier** that constructs an **optimal separating hyperplane** in a (possibly high- or infinite-dimensional) feature space by **maximizing the geometric margin** between two classes. The hyperplane is defined solely by the critical training samples lying closest to the decision boundary — these are called **Support Vectors**.

Mathematically, for a training set $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, SVM finds the hyperplane:

$$w^T x + b = 0$$

such that the geometric margin $\frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$ is **maximized**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Widest Road Between Two Villages"**
>
> Imagine two neighboring villages **Class A** (+1) and **Class B** (−1) separated by a narrow strip of land. The SVM's job is to draw a road (the **decision hyperplane**) exactly midway between the two villages, but not just *any* road — it must be the **widest possible road** so that future travelers (new test points) are confidently assigned to the correct village.
>
> The **fence posts at the edge of the road** (closest points from each class) are the **Support Vectors**. Move any other village house, and the road does NOT change. Move a fence post, and the entire road shifts. This sensitivity to only a few critical points makes SVM both **robust** and **sparse**.

### Why SVM Matters for KTU Pattern Recognition

- It is a **maximum-margin classifier** — strong generalization guarantee via **Structural Risk Minimization (SRM)**.
- **Kernel trick** allows learning **non-linear decision boundaries** without explicit feature mapping.
- Forms the foundation for advanced topics: **SVR (Regression)**, **One-Class SVM**, **SVDD**.

---

## 1.2 Unsupervised Learning — Basics

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unsupervised Learning** is a machine learning paradigm where the algorithm is given a dataset $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ with **no associated target labels** $y_i$. The goal is to discover **intrinsic structure**, **patterns**, **clusters**, or **low-dimensional representations** directly from the feature distribution $P(X)$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Sorting Laundry Without Instructions"**
>
> You are given a giant pile of clothes with no labels (no "shirt", "trousers" tags). You naturally **group similar items together** — socks with socks, shirts with shirts. You just performed **unsupervised clustering** based on intrinsic visual features (color, shape, texture).
>
> The key difference from supervised learning: there is **no teacher** telling you the correct group. The algorithm must infer groupings from the data's own geometry.

### Core Tasks Under Unsupervised Learning

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Principal Sub-Tasks:**
> 1. **Clustering** — Partition $N$ samples into $K$ groups (e.g., $K$-Means, Hierarchical, DBSCAN).
> 2. **Dimensionality Reduction** — Project $d$-dimensional data to $d' \ll d$ dimensions while preserving structure (e.g., **PCA**, LDA-supervised, t-SNE).

### Quick Contrast Table

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | $(x_i, y_i)$ pairs | $x_i$ only (no labels) |
| **Goal** | Learn $f: X \to Y$ | Discover structure in $X$ |
| **Example** | SVM, Perceptron | $K$-Means, PCA |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, F1, Confusion Matrix | Silhouette, Davies-Bouldin, Reconstruction Error |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Linear SVM — Hard Margin (Linearly Separable Case)

### Setup
- Training data: $\{(x_i, y_i)\}, \; y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$
- Two classes are **linearly separable** if $\exists (w, b)$ such that:
$$y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i \in \{1, \ldots, N\}$$

### Optimization Problem (Primal Form)

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2} \Vert w \Vert^2$$

subject to:
$$y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

- The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is convention for cleaner gradient.
- $\Vert w \Vert^2$ minimization $\Leftrightarrow$ margin maximization.

### Geometric Margin

$$\text{Margin} = \frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$?**
> Distance from any point $x_0$ to hyperplane $w^T x + b = 0$ is $\frac{\vert w^T x_0 + b \vert}{\Vert w \Vert}$. The two supporting hyperplanes are $w^T x + b = +1$ and $w^T x + b = -1$, separated by total distance $\frac{1}{\Vert w \Vert} - \frac{-1}{\Vert w \Vert} = \frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$.

---

## 2.2 Soft Margin SVM (Non-Separable Case)

Introduce **slack variables** $\xi_i \geq 0$ to allow misclassification:

$$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2} \Vert w \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

subject to:
$$y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$

- $C > 0$ is the **regularization (penalty) parameter** balancing margin width vs. misclassification.
- Large $C$ → harder margin (less tolerance for violations).
- Small $C$ → softer margin (wider margin, more violations tolerated).

---

## 2.3 Dual Formulation & KKT Conditions

Using Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$, the **dual problem** is:

$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(x_i, x_j)$$

subject to:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$$

- $K(x_i, x_j)$ is the **kernel function** (replaces inner product for non-linear SVM).
- **KKT Complementary Slackness:**
$$\alpha_i \big[ y_i (w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \big] = 0$$

- Samples with $\alpha_i > 0$ are the **Support Vectors**.

---

## 2.4 The Kernel Trick

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kernel Trick:** Replace the inner product $\langle x_i, x_j \rangle$ with a kernel $K(x_i, x_j) = \langle \phi(x_i), \phi(x_j) \rangle$, where $\phi(\cdot)$ maps data to a higher-dimensional space — **without explicitly computing $\phi$**.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Kernel | Formula $K(x_i, x_j)$ | Hyperparameters | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $x_i^T x_j$ | None | Linearly separable data |
| **Polynomial** | $(\gamma x_i^T x_j + r)^d$ | $\gamma, r, d$ | Curved boundaries |
| **RBF (Gaussian)** | $\exp(-\gamma \Vert x_i - x_j \Vert^2)$ | $\gamma > 0$ | Most common default; infinite-dim feature space |
| **Sigmoid** | $\tanh(\gamma x_i^T x_j + r)$ | $\gamma, r$ | Historically similar to neural nets |
| **Cosine** | $\frac{x_i^T x_j}{\Vert x_i \Vert \Vert x_j \Vert}$ | None | Text/NLP similarity |

### Mercer's Condition

> [!NOTE]
> A function $K$ is a valid kernel iff the **kernel matrix** (Gram matrix) $K_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ is **symmetric positive semi-definite** for all training samples.

---

## 2.5 Unsupervised Learning — Cluster Analysis

### $K$-Means Algorithm

$$\min_{C_1, \ldots, C_K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$$

where $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ is the centroid of cluster $k$.

**Steps:**
1. Initialize $K$ cluster centroids (randomly or via $K$-Means++).
2. **Assignment step:** Assign each $x_i$ to nearest centroid.
3. **Update step:** Recompute $\mu_k$ as mean of assigned points.
4. Repeat 2–3 until convergence.

---

## 2.6 Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

### Objective
Find orthogonal directions (principal components) $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{d'}$ that **maximize variance** of projected data.

### Maximization Form

$$\max_{w} w^T \Sigma w \quad \text{subject to} \quad w^T w = 1$$

- $\Sigma = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})(x_i - \bar{x})^T$ is the **covariance matrix**.
- Optimal $w$ = **eigenvector** of $\Sigma$ corresponding to the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda$.
- Variance captured by component $w$: $\lambda$.

### Reconstruction Error Formulation (Equivalent)

$$\min_{W, Z} \Vert X - W Z^T \Vert_F^2$$

subject to $W^T W = I_{d'}$.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Technique | Application Domain |
|---|---|
| SVM | Face detection, bioinformatics (cancer classification), text categorization, handwriting OCR |
| $K$-Means | Customer segmentation, image compression (vector quantization), anomaly detection |
| PCA | Face recognition (Eigenfaces), signal denoising, feature extraction, data visualization |
| Kernel SVM | Non-linear fault diagnosis in mechanical systems, gene expression analysis |

---

## 2.8 Distance / Similarity Metrics (Used in Clustering)

| Metric | Formula | Note |
|---|---|---|
| Euclidean | $\sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_{ij}-x_{kj})^2}$ | Default for $K$-Means |
| Manhattan | $\sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_{ij} - x_{kj} \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| Minkowski | $\left(\sum_j \vert x_{ij}-x_{kj} \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalization |
| Cosine | $1 - \frac{x_i^T x_k}{\Vert x_i \Vert \Vert x_k \Vert}$ | Direction-based; for text |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: From Primal SVM to Dual Form

### Step 1 — Construct the Lagrangian

For the hard-margin SVM, form the **primal Lagrangian** by adding constraints with multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(w, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2} \Vert w \Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \big[ y_i (w^T x_i + b) - 1 \big]
$$

### Step 2 — Set Partial Derivatives to Zero (KKT Stationarity)

Differentiate w.r.t. $w$ and $b$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = w - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Back into $\mathcal{L}$

Substitute $w$ back:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} w^T w - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i w^T x_i - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i b + \sum_i \alpha_i
$$

Using $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ (the $b$-term vanishes):

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} w^T w - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i w^T x_i + \sum_i \alpha_i
$$

But $w^T w = w^T \left(\sum_j \alpha_j y_j x_j\right) = \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i^T x_j)$

Substituting:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i^T x_j) - \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i^T x_j) + \sum_i \alpha_i
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathcal{L}_D(\alpha) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i^T x_j)}
$$

### Step 4 — State the Dual Problem

$$
\max_{\alpha} \quad \mathcal{L}_D(\alpha) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(x_i, x_j)
$$

subject to: $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad \alpha_i \geq 0$.

### Step 5 — Kernelization

Replace $x_i^T x_j$ with $K(x_i, x_j)$ to obtain the **kernelized dual**:

$$
\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(x_i, x_j)
$$

This is the **only change** needed to convert linear SVM into non-linear SVM — the **kernel trick**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Hyperplane Offset $b^*$

From KKT complementary slackness:

$$
\alpha_i \big[ y_i (w^T x_i + b) - 1 \big] = 0
$$

For any support vector with $0 < \alpha_i < C$ (margin support vector, where $\xi_i = 0$):

$$
y_i (w^T x_i + b) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = y_i - w^T x_i
$$

Averaging over all margin support vectors $S$ gives a stable estimate:

$$
\boxed{b^* = \frac{1}{\vert S \vert} \sum_{i \in S} \big( y_i - w^T x_i \big) = \frac{1}{\vert S \vert} \sum_{i \in S} \big( y_i - \sum_{j} \alpha_j y_j K(x_j, x_i) \big)}
$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Linear SVM on 2D Toy Data

**Data:** $x_1 = (3, 3)^T, y_1 = +1$; $x_2 = (1, 1)^T, y_2 = -1$.

### Step 1 — Write constraints

For class +1: $3 w_1 + 3 w_2 + b \geq 1$.

For class -1: $w_1 + w_2 + b \leq -1$.

### Step 2 — Assume both are support vectors (active constraints)

$$
3 w_1 + 3 w_2 + b = 1
$$

$$
w_1 + w_2 + b = -1
$$

Subtract the second from the first:

$$
2 w_1 + 2 w_2 = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w_1 + w_2 = 1
$$

From equation (2): $1 + b = -1 \Rightarrow b = -2$.

### Step 3 — Solve

The weight direction is proportional to $(x_1 - x_2) = (2, 2)^T$, so $w = (a, a)^T$.

Then $2a = 1 \Rightarrow a = 0.5$. Hence:

$$
\boxed{w = (0.5, 0.5)^T, \quad b = -2}
$$

### Step 4 — Decision function

$$
f(x) = 0.5 x_1 + 0.5 x_2 - 2
$$

Margin width:

$$
\frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{0.5^2 + 0.5^2}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{0.5}} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — $K$-Means on 2D Points

**Points:** $A(1,1), B(1,2), C(2,1), D(8,8), E(9,9), F(8,9)$.  
**Choose** $K = 2$, initial centroids $C_1 = (1,1), C_2 = (8,8)$.

### Iteration 1

Compute Euclidean distance of each point to both centroids:

| Point | Dist to $C_1$ | Dist to $C_2$ | Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| A(1,1) | 0.00 | 9.90 | 1 |
| B(1,2) | 1.00 | 9.90 | 1 |
| C(2,1) | 1.00 | 8.49 | 1 |
| D(8,8) | 9.90 | 0.00 | 2 |
| E(9,9) | 11.31 | 1.41 | 2 |
| F(8,9) | 10.63 | 1.00 | 2 |

### Update Centroids

$$
\mu_1 = \left( \tfrac{1+1+2}{3}, \tfrac{1+2+1}{3} \right) = (1.33, 1.33)
$$

$$
\mu_2 = \left( \tfrac{8+9+8}{3}, \tfrac{8+9+9}{3} \right) = (8.33, 8.67)
$$

### Iteration 2 (Re-assign)

All points still belong to their original clusters — **convergence reached**. The intra-cluster sum of squared distances:

$$
J = (0.89 + 0.45 + 0.45) + (0.11 + 0.45 + 0.11) \approx 2.46
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Boundaries Summary

| Method | Decision Boundary | Linear? | Learns From |
|---|---|---|---|
| Perceptron | $w^T x + b = 0$ | Yes | Any separating hyperplane |
| Hard-Margin SVM | $w^T x + b = 0$ | Yes | Max-margin hyperplane |
| Soft-Margin SVM | $w^T x + b = 0$ | Yes | Max-margin with slack |
| Kernel SVM | $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i K(x_i, x) + b = 0$ | No (in input space) | Max-margin in feature space |
| $K$-Means | Voronoi tessellation | Piecewise-linear | Cluster centroids |
| PCA | Subspace projection | Yes | Variance directions |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 SVM — Maximum Margin Classifier Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Training Dataset with Two Classes] --> B[Map Data to Feature Space]
    B --> C{Linearly Separable?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Hard-Margin SVM]
    C -- No --> E[Soft-Margin SVM with Slack Variables]
    D --> F[Construct Two Parallel Hyperplanes]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Maximize Margin 2 over norm w]
    G --> H[Identify Support Vectors alpha greater than zero]
    H --> I{Linear Boundary Needed?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Linear Hyperplane Decision Function]
    I -- No --> K[Apply Kernel Trick K of x_i x_j]
    K --> L[Non-Linear Decision Boundary in Input Space]
    J --> M[Classify New Test Points]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 SVM Optimization Pipeline — Primal to Dual to Kernel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Primal: Minimize 0.5 norm w squared plus C times sum xi] -->|Lagrangian| D1[Dual: Maximize sum alpha minus 0.5 sum alpha_i alpha_j y_i y_j K x_i x_j]
    D1 -->|Constraints| C1[Sum alpha_i y_i equals 0, alpha_i between 0 and C]
    C1 -->|Solve QP| SV[Extract Support Vectors where alpha greater than zero]
    SV -->|Compute w and b| HYP[Decision Hyperplane]
    HYP -->|Predict| OUT[Sign of w transpose x plus b]
```

## 4.3 Unsupervised Learning Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UN[Unsupervised Learning] --> CL[Clustering]
    UN --> DR[Dimension Reduction]
    UN --> AS[Association Rule Mining]
    UN --> AN[Anomaly Detection]
    CL --> KM[K-Means]
    CL --> HC[Hierarchical Agglomerative]
    CL --> DB[DBSCAN Density Based]
    CL --> GM[Gaussian Mixture Models]
    DR --> PCA[Principal Component Analysis]
    DR --> ICA[Independent Component Analysis]
    DR --> TSNE[t-SNE Visualization]
    AS --> APR[Apriori]
    AN --> ISO[Isolation Forest]
```

## 4.4 K-Means Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Input: N samples and K clusters] --> S2[Initialize K centroids KMeans plus plus preferred]
    S2 --> S3[Assignment: Assign each point to nearest centroid]
    S3 --> S4[Update: Recompute centroid as mean of cluster]
    S4 --> S5{Convergence or Max Iterations?}
    S5 -- No --> S3
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Output: K final clusters and centroids]
```

## 4.5 PCA Geometric Intuition Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original d-dimensional Data Cloud] --> B[Compute Mean Vector mu and Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    B --> C[Eigendecomposition: Sigma v equals lambda v]
    C --> D[Sort Eigenvalues lambda 1 greater than lambda 2 greater than ... greater than lambda d]
    D --> E[Select Top d prime Eigenvectors as Principal Axes]
    E --> F[Project Data: Z equals W transpose times X minus mu]
    F --> G[Reconstruct: X hat equals W Z plus mu]
    G --> H[Reconstruction Error equals norm X minus X hat squared]
```

## 4.6 Hard vs Soft Margin Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HM[Hard-Margin SVM] --> HM1[No slack variables]
    HM1 --> HM2[Strict separation required]
    HM2 --> HM3[Used only when data is perfectly linearly separable]
    SM[Soft-Margin SVM] --> SM1[Introduces slack variables xi greater than zero]
    SM1 --> SM2[Penalty parameter C controls trade-off]
    SM2 --> SM3[Robust to outliers and noise]
    SM2 --> SM4[Allows some misclassifications]
    HM3 --> OUT[Compare Performance on Validation Set]
    SM3 --> OUT
    SM4 --> OUT
```
