# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the EM Update Equations for GMM

We begin with the complete-data log-likelihood (assuming latent $\mathbf{z}$ is observed):

$$
\log p(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{Z} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} z_{nk} \left[ \log \pi_k + \log \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_k, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k) \right]
$$

The expected complete-data log-likelihood (Q-function) is obtained by taking the expectation of the above with respect to the posterior $p(\mathbf{Z} \mid \mathbf{X}, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}})$. Because $z_{nk}$ is binary, this expectation simply replaces $z_{nk}$ with its posterior mean $\gamma(z_{nk})$:

$$
Q(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}}) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{Z} \mid \mathbf{X}, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}}} \left[ \log p(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{Z} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) \right] = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \left[ \log \pi_k + \log \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_k, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k) \right]
$$

---

### 3.1.1 Derivation of $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\text{new}}$

We isolate the terms of $Q$ that depend on $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$:

$$
Q_{\boldsymbol{\mu}_k} = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) (\mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)^{\!\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{-1} (\mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k) + \text{const}
$$

Differentiate with respect to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial Q_{\boldsymbol{\mu}_k}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_k} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{-1} (\mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k) = \boldsymbol{0}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k$ (which is invertible):

$$
\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) (\mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k) = \boldsymbol{0}
$$

Expanding:

$$
\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) = \boldsymbol{0}
$$

Solving for $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$:

$$
\boxed{\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \mathbf{x}_n}{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \mathbf{x}_n}
$$

**Interpretation:** The new mean is the *responsibility-weighted sample mean* of the data.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{\text{new}}$

Isolate $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k$-dependent terms in $Q$:

$$
Q_{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k} = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \left[ \log \vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k \vert + (\mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)^{\!\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{-1} (\mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k) \right]
$$

Using standard matrix derivatives: $\frac{\partial \log \vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k \vert}{\partial \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k} = (\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{-1})^{\!\top} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{-1}$ and $\frac{\partial \, \mathbf{a}^{\!\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{-1} \mathbf{a}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k} = -(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{-1} \mathbf{a} \mathbf{a}^{\!\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{-1})^{\!\top}$.

Setting $\frac{\partial Q_{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k} = 0$ and simplifying:

$$
\boxed{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \, (\mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\text{new}})(\mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\text{new}})^{\!\top}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Responsibility-weighted sample covariance.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of $\pi_k^{\text{new}}$ (with Lagrange Multiplier)

The $\pi_k$-dependent part of $Q$ is:

$$
Q_{\pi} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \log \pi_k
$$

Subject to the constraint $\sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k = 1$, we form the Lagrangian:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \log \pi_k + \lambda \left( \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k - 1 \right)
$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $\pi_k$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \pi_k} = \frac{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})}{\pi_k} + \lambda = \frac{N_k}{\pi_k} + \lambda = 0
$$

Solving for $\pi_k$:

$$
\pi_k = -\frac{N_k}{\lambda}
$$

Summing over $k$ and using the constraint:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k = -\frac{1}{\lambda} \sum_{k=1}^{K} N_k = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \lambda = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} N_k = -N
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\boxed{\pi_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{N_k}{N}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The new mixing coefficient is the *fractional occupancy* of component $k$ in the dataset.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: 1-D GMM with $K=2$

**Problem Setup:** Suppose we have $N=5$ one-dimensional points: $\mathcal{X} = \{1, 2, 3, 7, 8\}$. We fit a 2-component GMM (i.e., a mixture of two univariate Gaussians).

**Initial parameters (iteration $t=0$):**
$\pi_1 = \pi_2 = 0.5$, $\mu_1 = 1.5$, $\mu_2 = 7.5$, $\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = 1$.

**E-step (compute responsibilities):** For each $\mathbf{x}_n$, evaluate $\gamma(z_{n1})$:

$$
\gamma(z_{n1}) = \frac{\pi_1 \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \mu_1, \sigma_1^2)}{\pi_1 \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \mu_1, \sigma_1^2) + \pi_2 \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \mu_2, \sigma_2^2)}
$$

For $\mathbf{x}_n = 1$:
$\mathcal{N}(1 \mid 1.5, 1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp(-0.5 \cdot (1-1.5)^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp(-0.125) \approx 0.3521$
$\mathcal{N}(1 \mid 7.5, 1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp(-0.5 \cdot (1-7.5)^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp(-21.125) \approx 6.87 \times 10^{-10}$

$\gamma(z_{11}) \approx \frac{0.5 \times 0.3521}{0.5 \times 0.3521 + 0.5 \times 6.87 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 1.0$

Similarly, points $\{1, 2, 3\}$ are essentially assigned to component 1, and points $\{7, 8\}$ to component 2.

**M-step (parameter updates):**
$N_1 = \sum_{n=1}^{5} \gamma(z_{n1}) \approx 3$, $N_2 \approx 2$.

$\mu_1^{\text{new}} = \frac{\gamma(z_{11})\cdot 1 + \gamma(z_{21})\cdot 2 + \gamma(z_{31})\cdot 3 + \gamma(z_{41})\cdot 7 + \gamma(z_{51})\cdot 8}{3} \approx \frac{1\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 3 + 0\cdot 7 + 0\cdot 8}{3} = 2$

$\mu_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{0\cdot 1 + 0\cdot 2 + 0\cdot 3 + 1\cdot 7 + 1\cdot 8}{2} = 7.5$

$\pi_1^{\text{new}} = 3/5 = 0.6$, $\pi_2^{\text{new}} = 0.4$.

The parameters converge in a few iterations, recovering the two well-separated clusters.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Numerical GMM problems in KTU typically use $D=1$ or $D=2$ and only **one or two EM iterations**. Practice computing responsibilities by hand for at least one such problem.

## 3.3 Proof of Monotonic Convergence (Sketch)

Define the auxiliary function:

$$
\mathcal{F}(q, \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{\mathbf{Z}} q(\mathbf{Z}) \log \frac{p(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{Z} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta})}{q(\mathbf{Z})}
$$

By Jensen's inequality, $\mathcal{F}(q, \boldsymbol{\theta}) \leq \ell(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ with equality iff $q(\mathbf{Z}) = p(\mathbf{Z} \mid \mathbf{X}, \boldsymbol{\theta})$.

- **E-step:** Choose $q^{\text{new}}(\mathbf{Z}) = p(\mathbf{Z} \mid \mathbf{X}, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}})$, so $\mathcal{F}(q^{\text{new}}, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}}) = \ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}})$.
- **M-step:** Maximize $\mathcal{F}(q^{\text{new}}, \boldsymbol{\theta})$ w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ to obtain $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{new}}$. Since the bound is tight at $\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}}$, we have $\mathcal{F}(q^{\text{new}}, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{new}}) \geq \mathcal{F}(q^{\text{new}}, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}}) = \ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}})$.
- Since $\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \geq \mathcal{F}(q, \boldsymbol{\theta})$ for all $q$, we conclude $\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{new}}) \geq \ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}})$. ∎

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of GMM-based Pattern Recognition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data X] --> B[Preprocessing and Feature Scaling]
    B --> C[Choose K Number of Components]
    C --> D[Initialize Parameters mu sigma pi]
    D --> E[E-Step Compute Responsibilities gamma]
    E --> F{Convergence Check}
    F -- Not Converged --> G[M-Step Re-estimate mu sigma pi]
    G --> E
    F -- Converged --> H[Compute Log-Likelihood]
    H --> I[Posterior Probabilities p zk given x]
    I --> J[Cluster Assignment via argmax gamma]
    J --> K[Final Output Pattern Labels or Density Estimates]
    style A fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    style K fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style E fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style G fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
```

## 4.2 The EM Algorithm as an Iterative Loop (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Initialize theta 0] --> B[Iter t: 0 1 2 ...]
    B --> C[E-Step: Compute gamma znk for all n and k]
    C --> D[Store Old Parameters theta old]
    D --> E[M-Step: Maximize Q w.r.t. theta]
    E --> F[Compute Delta Log-Likelihood]
    F --> G{Delta less than epsilon or max iter}
    G -- No --> B
    G -- Yes --> H[Output theta star]
    style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style H fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style C fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
    style E fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Architecture of a Gaussian Mixture Density

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LATENT[Latent Variable Layer]
        Z1[Z1 with prior pi 1]
        Z2[Z2 with prior pi 2]
        Z3[Z3 with prior pi 3]

        G2[Gaussian 2 mean mu 2 cov Sigma 2]

    G2 --> OUT
    G3 --> OUT
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KM[K Means Hard Clustering]
        KM1[Distance to centroid] --> KM2[argmin distance gives hard label 0 or 1]
    end
    subgraph GM[GMM Soft Clustering]

    end
```

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define a Gaussian Mixture Model. Write the expression for the joint and marginal distribution in terms of latent variables.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)** is a probabilistic model that represents a complex data distribution as a convex combination of $K$ multivariate Gaussian component densities. Using a 1-of-$K$ latent variable $\mathbf{z}$ with prior $p(z_k = 1) = \pi_k$, the joint distribution is:

$$
p(\mathbf{x}, z_k = 1) = \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x} \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_k, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k)
$$

and the marginal distribution (summing out the latent variable) is:

$$
p(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x} \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_k, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k)
$$

with the constraints $\pi_k \geq 0$ and $\sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k = 1$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the responsibility term in the EM algorithm for GMM? Give its expression and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **responsibility** $\gamma(z_{nk})$ is the posterior probability that the $k$-th Gaussian component was responsible for generating data point $\mathbf{x}_n$, given the current parameter estimates:

$$
\gamma(z_{nk}) = p(z_{nk} = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}_n, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}}) = \frac{\pi_k^{\text{old}} \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\text{old}}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{\text{old}})}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j^{\text{old}} \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{\text{old}}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\text{old}})}
$$

**Significance:** It serves as a *soft cluster assignment* for each data point. Unlike K-Means' hard 0/1 assignment, the responsibility captures uncertainty, allowing a point to partially belong to multiple components. The M-step re-estimates parameters using these responsibilities as weights, enabling the model to learn from uncertain assignments.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice Format

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is structured as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, consistent with the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) pattern. Two alternative question choices (Q-A and Q-B) are provided.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q: (a)** Derive the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm update equations for a Gaussian Mixture Model. Start from the expected complete-data log-likelihood (Q-function) and clearly show the E-step and M-step derivations for $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$, $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k$, and $\pi_k$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 1-D dataset with $N=4$ points: $\mathcal{X} = \{0, 1, 10, 11\}$. A 2-component GMM is to be fitted with initial parameters: $\pi_1 = \pi_2 = 0.5$, $\mu_1 = 0.5$, $\mu_2 = 10.5$, $\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = 1.0$.

Compute **(i)** the responsibilities $\gamma(z_{n1})$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, 4$ at the initial step, and **(ii)** the updated means $\mu_1^{\text{new}}$ and $\mu_2^{\text{new}}$ after one full EM iteration. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Complete-Data Log-Likelihood**

The complete data includes observed $\mathbf{X}$ and latent $\mathbf{Z}$. With $z_{nk} \in \{0,1\}$ indicating the component:

$$
\log p(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{Z} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} z_{nk} \Big[ \log \pi_k + \log \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_k, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k) \Big]
$$

**Step 2: E-Step — Computing the Q-Function**

Take the expectation over the posterior $p(\mathbf{Z} \mid \mathbf{X}, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}})$. Since $z_{nk}$ is binary, $\mathbb{E}[z_{nk}] = p(z_{nk}=1 \mid \mathbf{x}_n, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}}) \equiv \gamma(z_{nk})$:

$$
\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k^{\text{old}} \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\text{old}}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{\text{old}})}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j^{\text{old}} \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{\text{old}}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\text{old}})}
$$

The Q-function becomes:

$$
Q(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \Big[ \log \pi_k + \log \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_k, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k) \Big]
$$

**Step 3: M-Step — Maximize Q w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$**

Differentiate the $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$-dependent part, set to zero, multiply by $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \mathbf{x}_n, \quad \text{where } N_k = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})
$$

**Step 4: M-Step — Maximize Q w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k$**

Using matrix derivatives $\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\Sigma}} \log \vert \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \vert = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}$ and $\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\Sigma}} \mathbf{a}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{a} = -\boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} \mathbf{a} \mathbf{a}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) (\mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\text{new}})(\mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\text{new}})^{\top}
$$

**Step 5: M-Step — Maximize Q w.r.t. $\pi_k$ with Constraint**

Use a Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ for $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \pi_k} \left[ \sum_{n,k} \gamma(z_{nk}) \log \pi_k + \lambda(\sum_k \pi_k - 1) \right] = \frac{N_k}{\pi_k} + \lambda = 0
$$

Solving: $\pi_k = -N_k / \lambda$. Summing over $k$ and applying the constraint: $\lambda = -N$. Therefore:

$$
\pi_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{N_k}{N}
$$

**Step 6: Iterate** — Repeat E-step and M-step until $\vert \ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t+1)}) - \ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(t)}) \vert < \epsilon$.

*(Full marks require the E-step equation, the three M-step formulas, and a brief statement of the Lagrange multiplier technique.)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Set Up the E-Step for $N=4$, $K=2$**

The Gaussian density for 1-D is:

$$
\mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi} \, \sigma} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)
$$

With $\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = 1$ and $\pi_1 = \pi_2 = 0.5$, the responsibility simplifies to:

$$
\gamma(z_{n1}) = \frac{\exp\!\left( -0.5 (x_n - 0.5)^2 \right)}{\exp\!\left( -0.5 (x_n - 0.5)^2 \right) + \exp\!\left( -0.5 (x_n - 10.5)^2 \right)}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\gamma(z_{11})$ for $x_1 = 0$**

$(0 - 0.5)^2 = 0.25 \;\Rightarrow\; \exp(-0.125) \approx 0.8825$
$(0 - 10.5)^2 = 110.25 \;\Rightarrow\; \exp(-55.125) \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-23}$
$\gamma(z_{11}) = \frac{0.8825}{0.8825 + 1.05 \times 10^{-23}} \approx 1.0000$

**Step 3: Compute $\gamma(z_{21})$ for $x_2 = 1$**

$(1 - 0.5)^2 = 0.25 \;\Rightarrow\; \exp(-0.125) \approx 0.8825$
$(1 - 10.5)^2 = 90.25 \;\Rightarrow\; \exp(-45.125) \approx 2.49 \times 10^{-20}$
$\gamma(z_{21}) \approx 1.0000$

**Step 4: Compute $\gamma(z_{31})$ for $x_3 = 10$**

$(10 - 0.5)^2 = 90.25 \;\Rightarrow\; \exp(-45.125) \approx 2.49 \times 10^{-20}$
$(10 - 10.5)^2 = 0.25 \;\Rightarrow\; \exp(-0.125) \approx 0.8825$
$\gamma(z_{31}) \approx \frac{2.49 \times 10^{-20}}{2.49 \times 10^{-20} + 0.8825} \approx 0.0000$

**Step 5: Compute $\gamma(z_{41})$ for $x_4 = 11$**

$(11 - 0.5)^2 = 110.25 \;\Rightarrow\; \exp(-55.125) \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-23}$
$(11 - 10.5)^2 = 0.25 \;\Rightarrow\; \exp(-0.125) \approx 0.8825$
$\gamma(z_{41}) \approx 0.0000$

**Summary of Responsibilities:**
$\gamma(z_{11}) \approx 1.0,\; \gamma(z_{21}) \approx 1.0,\; \gamma(z_{31}) \approx 0.0,\; \gamma(z_{41}) \approx 0.0$

By symmetry: $\gamma(z_{n2}) = 1 - \gamma(z_{n1})$.

**Step 6: M-Step — Compute $N_1$ and $N_2$**

$N_1 = 1.0 + 1.0 + 0.0 + 0.0 = 2.0$
$N_2 = 0.0 + 0.0 + 1.0 + 1.0 = 2.0$

**Step 7: Update Means**

$$
\mu_1^{\text{new}} = \frac{1.0 \cdot 0 + 1.0 \cdot 1 + 0.0 \cdot 10 + 0.0 \cdot 11}{2.0} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5
$$

$$
\mu_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{0.0 \cdot 0 + 0.0 \cdot 1 + 1.0 \cdot 10 + 1.0 \cdot 11}{2.0} = \frac{21}{2} = 10.5
$$

**Step 8: Update Mixing Coefficients**

$\pi_1^{\text{new}} = N_1 / N = 2/4 = 0.5$, $\pi_2^{\text{new}} = 0.5$.

**Final Answer (b):**
- Responsibilities: $\{1.0,\; 1.0,\; 0.0,\; 0.0\}$
- Updated means: $\mu_1^{\text{new}} = 0.5$, $\mu_2^{\text{new}} = 10.5$
- Updated weights: $\pi_1^{\text{new}} = 0.5$, $\pi_2^{\text{new}} = 0.5$

*(The means remain stable because the two clusters $\{0,1\}$ and $\{10,11\}$ are perfectly separable given the initial standard deviations.)*

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the limitations of using Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) directly for a Gaussian Mixture Model. Show that the log-likelihood has no closed-form solution and motivate the use of the EM algorithm. Derive the posterior responsibility used in the E-step using Bayes' theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail how the optimal number of components $K$ can be selected for a GMM. Compare **AIC (Akaike Information Criterion)** and **BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion)** in terms of their penalty terms and asymptotic properties. Apply the BIC formula to a hypothetical scenario with $\ell = -1200$, $N = 500$, $d = 12$ and interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Write the Log-Likelihood**

For i.i.d. data $\mathcal{X} = \{\mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_N\}$:

$$
\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \log \left( \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_k, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k) \right)
$$

**Step 2: Differentiate and Set to Zero**

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_k} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \underbrace{\frac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_k, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k)}{\sum_{j} \pi_j \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_j, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j)}}_{\gamma(z_{nk})} \, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{-1} (\mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k) = \boldsymbol{0}
$$

The gradient depends on the responsibilities, which themselves depend on the means. This creates a **circular dependency** with no closed-form solution.

**Step 3: Explain the Difficulty**

- The $\log$ is applied to a **sum**, not a product. There is no algebraic simplification to push $\log$ inside the sum.
- Setting $\partial \ell / \partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_k = 0$ yields an equation that **contains the unknown $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ on both sides** (via $\gamma(z_{nk})$), making closed-form solution impossible.

**Step 4: The Singularity Problem**

If any data point $\mathbf{x}_n$ lies exactly on a component mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$, the likelihood for that component diverges (the variance shrinks to zero), and the MLE is **undefined**. EM avoids this through regularization and bounded responsibilities.

**Step 5: Justify EM**

EM handles the missing latent variable $z_{nk}$ by **alternating between**:
- Inferring $\gamma(z_{nk})$ (E-step),
- Optimizing weighted statistics (M-step).

This is guaranteed to monotonically increase $\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ and converge to a local maximum.

**Step 6: Derive the E-Step via Bayes' Theorem**

$$
\gamma(z_{nk}) = p(z_{nk} = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}_n, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}}) = \frac{p(z_{nk}=1) \, p(\mathbf{x}_n \mid z_{nk}=1, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}})}{p(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\text{old}})}
$$

Substituting the prior and likelihood:

$$
\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k^{\text{old}} \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\text{old}}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{\text{old}})}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j^{\text{old}} \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{\text{old}}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\text{old}})}
$$

The denominator is the marginal $p(\mathbf{x}_n) = \sum_j p(\mathbf{z}_j) p(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \mathbf{z}_j)$, which ensures $0 \leq \gamma(z_{nk}) \leq 1$ and $\sum_k \gamma(z_{nk}) = 1$. ∎

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: The Model Selection Problem**

Choosing $K$ in a GMM is a classic **model selection** problem. If $K$ is too small, the model underfits; if $K$ is too large, it overfits and may suffer from singularities or redundant components. The likelihood alone always increases with $K$, so a **penalized likelihood** criterion is required.

**Step 2: Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)**

$$
\text{AIC} = -2 \, \ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{\text{MLE}}) + 2 \, d
$$

where $d$ is the number of free parameters. The penalty is $2d$, which is *constant in $N$*. AIC is derived from an information-theoretic perspective and asymptotically selects the model that minimizes the expected Kullback–Leibler divergence from the true model.

**Step 3: Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC)**

$$
\text{BIC} = -2 \, \ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}_{\text{MLE}}) + d \, \log N
$$

The penalty is $d \log N$, which grows with $N$. BIC is derived from a Bayesian approximation to the model evidence and is **consistent**: as $N \to \infty$, BIC selects the true model with probability $\to 1$.

**Step 4: Comparison Table**

| Property | AIC | BIC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Penalty term | $2d$ | $d \log N$ |
| Growth with $N$ | Constant | Logarithmic |
| Asymptotic consistency | No (overfits) | Yes |
| Tendency | Favors larger models | Favors parsimonious models |
| Derived from | KL divergence | Bayesian marginal likelihood |

**Step 5: Numerical Application**

Given: $\ell = -1200$, $N = 500$, $d = 12$.

$$
\text{BIC} = -2(-1200) + 12 \log(500) = 2400 + 12 \times 6.2146 = 2400 + 74.575 = 2474.58
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation**

A BIC value of **2474.58** is a *relative* number — to choose $K$, one fits GMMs with $K = 1, 2, \ldots, K_{\max}$ and selects the $K$ that **minimizes BIC**. Since the penalty $d \log N = 12 \times 6.21 \approx 74.6$ is non-trivial, the model would need to improve the log-likelihood by at least $\sim 37$ units per added parameter to "beat" the simpler model. If a larger $K$ yields a smaller BIC, the more complex model is preferred; otherwise, the simpler one wins on parsimony.

*(To award full marks, the answer must include both formulas, the penalty comparison, the BIC computation with $\log(500)$, and a clear interpretation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Pushing $\log$ inside the sum** — A frequent error where students write $\log \sum_k \pi_k \mathcal{N} = \sum_k \pi_k \log \mathcal{N}$. This is **mathematically false** and loses 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the constraint $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$** when deriving the weight update. The Lagrange multiplier is *required*. Marks are lost if the constraint is silently dropped.
> 3. **Confusing prior $p(z_k=1) = \pi_k$ with posterior $\gamma(z_{nk})$** — they are different quantities. The E-step *computes* the posterior from the prior.
> 4. **Reporting the *initial* parameters as the *updated* ones** in numerical problems. You must show the E-step calculations first, then plug the resulting $\gamma$ values into the M-step formula.
> 5. **Ignoring numerical underflow in responsibilities** — in KTU numericals, exponential terms often vanish. State explicitly that the smaller term is negligible (e.g., $e^{-55} \approx 10^{-24}$).
> 6. **Forgetting to transpose in covariance update** — the term $(\mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)(\mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k)^{\top}$ requires an explicit transpose; students often omit the transpose, giving a scalar instead of a matrix.
> 7. **Not stating the convergence criterion** — Always finish EM derivations with "iterate until $\vert \Delta \ell \vert < \epsilon$ or maximum iterations reached."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (last-minute exam prep):**

- A **GMM** is a weighted sum of $K$ multivariate Gaussians with weights $\pi_k$, means $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$, and covariances $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k$.
- The mixing coefficients satisfy $\pi_k \geq 0$ and $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$.
- Direct MLE has **no closed form** because the $\log$ is outside the sum over components.
- The **EM algorithm** is an iterative two-step procedure: E-step computes responsibilities, M-step re-estimates parameters.
- The **responsibility** $\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_k, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k)}{\sum_j \pi_j \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_n \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_j, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j)}$ is the posterior $p(z_{nk}=1 \mid \mathbf{x}_n)$.
- The **M-step mean** is the responsibility-weighted sample mean: $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_n \gamma(z_{nk}) \mathbf{x}_n$.
- The **M-step covariance** is the responsibility-weighted sample covariance: $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_n \gamma(z_{nk}) (\mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\text{new}})(\mathbf{x}_n - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{\text{new}})^{\top}$.
- The **M-step weight** is the fractional occupancy: $\pi_k^{\text{new}} = N_k / N$, where $N_k = \sum_n \gamma(z_{nk})$.
- The mixing coefficient update is derived using a **Lagrange multiplier** for the constraint $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$.
- EM guarantees **monotonic increase** in the observed-data log-likelihood: $\ell^{(t+1)} \geq \ell^{(t)}$.
- Convergence is reached when $\vert \ell^{(t+1)} - \ell^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$ or $t > t_{\max}$.
- The number of free parameters is $d = (K-1) + K D + K \cdot \frac{D(D+1)}{2}$.
- **AIC** uses penalty $2d$; **BIC** uses penalty $d \log N$. BIC is asymptotically consistent.
- GMMs provide **soft (probabilistic)** cluster assignments, unlike K-Means' hard 0/1 assignments.
- GMMs can approximate **any continuous density** as $K \to \infty$ (universal approximator).
- GMMs are widely used in **speaker recognition, image segmentation, anomaly detection, and bioinformatics**.
- Initialization matters: K-Means clustering of $\mathbf{X}$ is a common way to seed $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(0)}\}$.
- **Covariance regularization**: Add $\epsilon \mathbf{I}$ to $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k$ to prevent singularity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST412 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Hidden Markov Models (HMMs)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Hidden Markov Model (HMM)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** is a doubly stochastic, discrete-time statistical finite-state Markov chain in which the **system being modelled is assumed to be a Markov process with unobserved (hidden) states**, but the output observations generated by each state are probabilistically dependent only on the current state. Mathematically, an HMM is fully parameterised by the compact quintuple $\lambda = (S, V, A, B, \pi)$, where each element rigorously quantifies the state space, observation alphabet, transition dynamics, emission dynamics, and initial state distribution respectively.

In a Hidden Markov Model, we have **two parallel stochastic processes running simultaneously**:

| Process Layer | Visibility to the Observer | Markov Property |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hidden State Process** $\{q_t\}$ | ❌ Hidden / Latent | First-order Markov on states |
| **Observable Output Process** $\{O_t\}$ | ✅ Visible / Emitted | Conditioned only on current hidden state |

The **hidden** nature of the state sequence is precisely what distinguishes a *Hidden* Markov Model from a pure (observable) Markov chain, and is the source of virtually all computational challenges we will study in this module.

---

### 1.2 The Five Canonical Elements of an HMM

An HMM is fully specified by the following five mathematical components:

$$ \lambda = (N,\; M,\; \mathbf{A},\; \mathbf{B},\; \boldsymbol{\pi}) $$

1. **$N$ — Number of Hidden States**  
   The cardinality of the latent state space:  
   $S = \{S_1, S_2, \dots, S_N\}$  
   Although the state set itself is fixed, the *index* of the actual state at time $t$, denoted $q_t \in S$, is hidden from the observer.

2. **$M$ — Number of Distinct Observation Symbols**  
   The size of the output alphabet:  
   $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_M\}$  
   Each observation $O_t \in V$ is the *visible* emission produced by the current hidden state.

3. **$\mathbf{A}$ — State Transition Probability Matrix** ($N \times N$)  
   $A = [a_{ij}]$, where  
   $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = S_j \mid q_t = S_i),\quad 1 \leq i,j \leq N$  
   Subject to the stochastic constraints: $a_{ij} \geq 0$ and $\sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} = 1$ for all $i$.

4. **$\mathbf{B}$ — Observation Emission Probability Matrix** ($N \times M$)  
   $B = [b_j(k)]$, where  
   $b_j(k) = P(O_t = v_k \mid q_t = S_j),\quad 1 \leq j \leq N,\; 1 \leq k \leq M$  
   Subject to: $b_j(k) \geq 0$ and $\sum_{k=1}^{M} b_j(k) = 1$ for all $j$.

5. **$\boldsymbol{\pi}$ — Initial State Distribution Vector** ($1 \times N$)  
   $\pi_i = P(q_1 = S_i),\quad 1 \leq i \leq N$  
   Subject to: $\pi_i \geq 0$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_i = 1$.

> [!NOTE] **Compact Notation**
> The compact description $\lambda = (\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}, \boldsymbol{\pi})$ is used once $N$ and $M$ are understood from context. This is the standard shorthand in the foundational HMM literature.

---

### 1.3 Two Critical Independence Assumptions (Markov + Output Independence)

> [!IMPORTANT] **The Two Pillars of Every HMM**
> 1. **Markov (First-Order) Assumption**: The future hidden state depends only on the present hidden state, not on the entire past history.  
>    $P(q_{t+1} \mid q_t, q_{t-1}, \dots, q_1) = P(q_{t+1} \mid q_t)$
> 2. **Output (Emission) Independence Assumption**: The current observation depends only on the current hidden state, not on the states or observations at any earlier time.  
>    $P(O_t \mid q_t, q_{t-1}, \dots, q_1, O_{t-1}, \dots, O_1) = P(O_t \mid q_t)$

These two assumptions jointly reduce the joint probability of a complete state-observation sequence to a tractable product form and make the rich forward–backward, Viterbi, and Baum–Welch machinery feasible.

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Weather–Clothes Conundrum"

Imagine you are an **alien visiting Earth**. You cannot see the sky, but you can see what your human friend wears each day.

| Hidden Reality | Observable Evidence |
| :--- | :--- |
| Actual weather ($S \in \{\text{Sunny, Rainy}\}$) — **hidden** | Clothing worn ($O \in \{\text{Coat, T-shirt}\}$) — **visible** |

- The actual weather follows a **Markov chain** (tomorrow's weather depends only on today's weather, not on last week's).
- On each day, your friend probabilistically chooses clothing **conditioned only on that day's weather** (output independence).
- You, as the alien observer, must **infer the hidden weather** from the observed clothing.

This is exactly the HMM problem! The weather is the *hidden state sequence*, the clothing is the *observation sequence*, and your job is to reason backwards through a doubly stochastic process.

---

### 1.5 Why HMMs Matter in Pattern Recognition (KTU Industrial Relevance)

> [!NOTE] **High-Yield Application Domains**
> - **Speech Recognition** (acoustic modelling of phonemes)
> - **Handwritten Character / Cursive Script Recognition**
> - **Bioinformatics** (gene prediction, protein profile alignment, CpG island detection)
> - **Gesture and Activity Recognition** in video surveillance
> - **Financial Time-Series Modelling** and regime detection
> - **Natural Language Processing** (POS tagging, named-entity recognition)
> - **Medical Diagnostics** (EEG/ECG signal segmentation)

The unifying theme: all of these domains exhibit **temporal sequence data** where the *true generative state* is latent but emits *noisy observations* that we can measure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Three Fundamental Problems of HMMs

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rabiner's Canonical Framework (Must Memorise for KTU)**
> Lawrence Rabiner's 1989 tutorial formally decomposed HMM computation into exactly **three well-posed problems**. The algorithms used to solve them constitute the bulk of the syllabus and the bulk of the marks.

| Problem # | Question Asked | Standard Algorithm | Use Case |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Problem 1 (Evaluation)** | Given $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ and $O = O_1 O_2 \dots O_T$, find $P(O \mid \lambda)$. | **Forward–Backward Algorithm** | Scoring how well a model fits data |
| **Problem 2 (Decoding)** | Given $\lambda$ and $O$, find the optimal hidden state sequence $Q^* = q_1^* q_2^* \dots q_T^*$. | **Viterbi Algorithm** | Discovering the underlying path |
| **Problem 3 (Learning)** | Given $O$, find $\lambda^* = \arg\max_{\lambda} P(O \mid \lambda)$. | **Baum–Welch (EM) Algorithm** | Training the model parameters |

---

## 2.2 Problem 1: The Evaluation Problem — Forward Variable

### 2.2.1 Why Direct Computation is Infeasible

A naive evaluation would enumerate **every possible state sequence** of length $T$, compute its probability jointly with $O$, and sum:

$$ P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{\text{all } Q} P(O, Q \mid \lambda) = \sum_{q_1 \dots q_T} \pi_{q_1} \, b_{q_1}(O_1) \, a_{q_1 q_2} \, b_{q_2}(O_2) \cdots a_{q_{T-1} q_T} \, b_{q_T}(O_T) $$

This summation contains $2T \cdot N^T$ operations (since each path has $T$ states with 2 factors per state). For moderate $N = 5$ and $T = 100$, this is approximately $10^{72}$ — **astronomically infeasible**. Hence the need for dynamic programming.

### 2.2.2 The Forward Variable $\alpha_t(i)$

> [!NOTE] **Definition of Forward Variable**
> $$ \alpha_t(i) = P(O_1, O_2, \dots, O_t,\; q_t = S_i \mid \lambda) $$
> Interpretation: the joint probability of observing the **first $t$ symbols** and being in state $S_i$ at time $t$.

### 2.2.3 Forward Algorithm (Inductive Recursion)

- **Initialisation** ($t = 1$):  
$$ \alpha_1(i) = \pi_i \, b_i(O_1),\quad 1 \leq i \leq N $$
- **Induction** ($t = 1, 2, \dots, T-1$):  
$$ \alpha_{t+1}(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) \, a_{ij} \right] b_j(O_{t+1}),\quad 1 \leq j \leq N $$
- **Termination**:  
$$ P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i) $$

**Complexity**: $O(N^2 T)$ — a massive saving from $O(N^T)$.

---

## 2.3 The Backward Variable $\beta_t(i)$

> [!NOTE] **Definition of Backward Variable**
> $$ \beta_t(i) = P(O_{t+1}, O_{t+2}, \dots, O_T \mid q_t = S_i,\; \lambda) $$
> Interpretation: the conditional probability of the **future observations from $t+1$ onward**, given the system is in state $S_i$ at time $t$.

- **Initialisation** ($t = T$): $\beta_T(i) = 1$ for all $i$.
- **Induction** ($t = T-1, \dots, 1$):  
$$ \beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} \, b_j(O_{t+1}) \, \beta_{t+1}(j) $$
- **Useful identity**:  
$$ P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) \, \beta_t(i) $$

---

## 2.4 Problem 2: The Decoding Problem — Viterbi Algorithm

### 2.4.1 Concept

We seek the *single best* (most probable) state sequence. Using **max-product** instead of **sum-product**:

$$ Q^* = \arg\max_Q P(Q \mid O, \lambda) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \arg\max_Q P(Q, O \mid \lambda) $$

### 2.4.2 Viterbi Recursion

Define $\delta_t(i) = \max_{q_1 \dots q_{t-1}} P(q_1 \dots q_{t-1}, q_t = S_i, O_1 \dots O_t \mid \lambda)$.

- **Initialisation**: $\delta_1(i) = \pi_i \, b_i(O_1)$, and store $\psi_1(i) = 0$.
- **Recursion** ($t = 2 \dots T$):  
$$ \delta_t(j) = \max_{1 \leq i \leq N} \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \, a_{ij} \right] \, b_j(O_t) $$  
$$ \psi_t(j) = \arg\max_{1 \leq i \leq N} \left[ \delta_{t-1}(i) \, a_{ij} \right] $$
- **Termination**: $P^* = \max_i \delta_T(i)$ and $q_T^* = \arg\max_i \delta_T(i)$.
- **Backtracking**: $q_t^* = \psi_{t+1}(q_{t+1}^*)$ for $t = T-1, \dots, 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Numerical Stability Tip (Frequently Asked in KTU)**  
> In practice, all Viterbi probabilities are computed in the **log-domain** to avoid floating-point underflow:  
$$ \log \delta_t(j) = \max_i \bigl[ \log \delta_{t-1}(i) + \log a_{ij} \bigr] + \log b_j(O_t) $$

---

## 2.5 Problem 3: The Learning Problem — Baum–Welch Algorithm

Since there is **no closed-form solution** for the MLE of HMM parameters, we resort to the **Expectation–Maximisation (EM)** iterative procedure:

### 2.5.1 Two Auxiliary Quantities

> [!NOTE] **$\xi_t(i, j)$ — Probability of Transitioning from $S_i$ to $S_j$ at time $t$**
> $$ \xi_t(i, j) = P(q_t = S_i, q_{t+1} = S_j \mid O, \lambda) = \frac{\alpha_t(i) \, a_{ij} \, b_j(O_{t+1}) \, \beta_{t+1}(j)}{P(O \mid \lambda)} $$

> [!NOTE] **$\gamma_t(i)$ — Probability of Being in $S_i$ at time $t$**
> $$ \gamma_t(i) = P(q_t = S_i \mid O, \lambda) = \frac{\alpha_t(i) \, \beta_t(i)}{P(O \mid \lambda)} = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \xi_t(i, j) $$

### 2.5.2 Re-estimation Formulas

$$ \bar{\pi}_i = \gamma_1(i) $$

$$ \bar{a}_{ij} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i, j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i)} $$

$$ \bar{b}_j(k) = \frac{\sum_{t:\,O_t = v_k} \gamma_t(j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} \gamma_t(j)} $$

The Baum–Welch algorithm guarantees **monotonic non-decrease**: $P(O \mid \bar{\lambda}) \geq P(O \mid \lambda)$ until a local maximum is reached. Convergence to the *global* maximum is **not** guaranteed.

---

## 2.6 Types of HMM Architectures

| Architecture | Topology | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ergodic (Fully Connected)** | Every state reachable from every state | $a_{ij} > 0$ for all $i, j$ |
| **Left-to-Right (Bakis)** | $a_{ij} = 0$ for $j < i$ | Dominant in speech recognition |
| **Parallel Path Left-to-Right** | Multiple parallel left-to-right chains | Models sub-state acoustic events |

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!NOTE] All formulas required for **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** questions on HMMs are consolidated below.

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Constraints / Notes |
| :- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | HMM parameter set | $\lambda = (N, M, \mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}, \boldsymbol{\pi})$ | $\mathbf{A}$ is $N \times N$, $\mathbf{B}$ is $N \times M$, $\boldsymbol{\pi}$ is $1 \times N$ |
| 2 | Row-stochasticity of $\mathbf{A}$ | $\sum_{j} a_{ij} = 1$ | $\forall i \in \{1 \dots N\}$ |
| 3 | Row-stochasticity of $\mathbf{B}$ | $\sum_{k} b_j(k) = 1$ | $\forall j \in \{1 \dots N\}$ |
| 4 | Sum of initial distribution | $\sum_{i} \pi_i = 1$ | $\pi_i \geq 0$ |
| 5 | Forward initialisation | $\alpha_1(i) = \pi_i \, b_i(O_1)$ | $1 \leq i \leq N$ |
| 6 | Forward induction | $\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \left[\sum_i \alpha_t(i) a_{ij}\right] b_j(O_{t+1})$ | Complexity $\mathcal{O}(N^2 T)$ |
| 7 | Backward initialisation | $\beta_T(i) = 1$ | $\forall i$ |
| 8 | Backward induction | $\beta_t(i) = \sum_j a_{ij} b_j(O_{t+1}) \beta_{t+1}(j)$ | — |
| 9 | Total likelihood | $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_i \alpha_T(i) = \sum_i \alpha_t(i) \beta_t(i)$ | Valid for any $t$ |
| 10 | Viterbi max | $\delta_t(j) = \max_i [\delta_{t-1}(i) a_{ij}] \, b_j(O_t)$ | Replaces sum by max |
| 11 | Two-state posterior | $\xi_t(i,j) = \dfrac{\alpha_t(i) a_{ij} b_j(O_{t+1}) \beta_{t+1}(j)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$ | E-step |
| 12 | Single-state posterior | $\gamma_t(i) = \sum_j \xi_t(i, j) = \dfrac{\alpha_t(i) \beta_t(i)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$ | — |
| 13 | Re-estimate $\pi$ | $\bar{\pi}_i = \gamma_1(i)$ | — |
| 14 | Re-estimate $a_{ij}$ | $\bar{a}_{ij} = \dfrac{\sum_t \xi_t(i,j)}{\sum_t \gamma_t(i)}$ | Expected transitions / expected time in $S_i$ |
| 15 | Re-estimate $b_j(k)$ | $\bar{b}_j(k) = \dfrac{\sum_{t: O_t = v_k} \gamma_t(j)}{\sum_t \gamma_t(j)}$ | Discrete alphabet only |
| 16 | Baum–Welch guarantee | $P(O \mid \lambda^{(n+1)}) \geq P(O \mid \lambda^{(n)})$ | Local, not global optimum |
| 17 | Markov property | $P(q_{t+1} \mid q_t, q_{t-1} \dots) = P(q_{t+1} \mid q_t)$ | First-order |
| 18 | Output independence | $P(O_t \mid q_t, O_{<t}, q_{<t}) = P(O_t \mid q_t)$ | — |
| 19 | Scaling factor | $c_t = \left[\sum_i \alpha_t(i)\right]^{-1}$ | Avoids underflow |
| 20 | Log-domain Viterbi | $\log \delta_t(j) = \max_i \left[\log \delta_{t-1}(i) + \log a_{ij}\right] + \log b_j(O_t)$ | Numerical stability |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility of HMMs in Engineering

- **Speech Recognition (e.g., HTK, Kaldi)**: Each word is modelled as a left-to-right HMM whose states correspond to sub-phonetic units. The Viterbi algorithm finds the most probable word sequence.
- **DNA Sequence Analysis**: Gene-finding algorithms (GENSCAN) treat coding vs non-coding regions as hidden states; nucleotide emissions are observed.
- **Fault Diagnosis in Machines**: Hidden states = "healthy" or "faulty"; emissions = noisy vibration sensor readings. Forward algorithm yields real-time probability of failure.
- **Gesture Recognition**: Hidden kinematic states emit observed accelerometer trajectories.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Forward Algorithm Recursion

### 3.1.1 Setup

We wish to evaluate $P(O \mid \lambda)$ without enumerating all $N^T$ paths. By the **law of total probability** conditioning on the state at time $t$:

$$ P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P(O,\; q_t = S_i \mid \lambda) $$

Define the forward variable:

$$ \alpha_t(i) = P(O_1, O_2, \dots, O_t,\; q_t = S_i \mid \lambda) $$

### 3.1.2 Recurrence Relation

Now decompose $\alpha_{t+1}(j)$ by conditioning on the state at time $t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{t+1}(j) &= P(O_1, \dots, O_{t+1},\; q_{t+1} = S_j \mid \lambda) \\[6pt]
&= P(O_1, \dots, O_t,\; q_t = S_i) \cdot P(q_{t+1} = S_j \mid q_t = S_i) \\
&\quad \cdot P(O_{t+1} \mid q_{t+1} = S_j) \quad \text{(summed over $i$)} \\[6pt]
&= \left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i)\, a_{ij}\right] \cdot b_j(O_{t+1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of each line:**
- **Line 1 → 2**: Apply the **chain rule** of probability, splitting the joint event into "everything up to time $t$ being in $S_i$" then "transitioning to $S_j$" then "emitting $O_{t+1}$".
- **Line 2 → 3**: Apply the **Markov assumption** (future state depends only on present state) and the **output independence assumption** (emission depends only on the current state). The summation over $i$ is the law of total probability.

### 3.1.3 Termination

After filling the trellis up to $t = T$, the marginal probability of the entire observation sequence is obtained by marginalising the final state:

$$ P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i) $$

> [!NOTE] **Complexity Check**
> At each time step, we perform $N$ multiplications and $N-1$ additions for each of $N$ destination states. Total: $\mathcal{O}(N^2 T)$ — a quadratic–linear polynomial instead of the prohibitive $\mathcal{O}(N^T)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Backward Algorithm Recursion

Define $\beta_t(i) = P(O_{t+1}, \dots, O_T \mid q_t = S_i, \lambda)$. By conditioning on the *next* state $S_j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta_t(i) &= \sum_{j=1}^{N} P(q_{t+1} = S_j \mid q_t = S_i) \cdot P(O_{t+1} \mid q_{t+1} = S_j) \\
&\quad \cdot P(O_{t+2}, \dots, O_T \mid q_{t+1} = S_j, \lambda) \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} \, b_j(O_{t+1}) \, \beta_{t+1}(j)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Justification of each term:**
- $a_{ij}$: probability of going from $S_i$ to $S_j$ (Markov assumption).
- $b_j(O_{t+1})$: probability that $S_j$ emits $O_{t+1}$ (output independence).
- $\beta_{t+1}(j)$: probability of all subsequent observations given we are now in $S_j$.

The base case $\beta_T(i) = 1$ corresponds to the convention that an empty suffix has probability 1.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Viterbi Recursion

Define $\delta_t(i) = \max_{q_1, \dots, q_{t-1}} P(q_1, \dots, q_{t-1}, q_t = S_i, O_1, \dots, O_t \mid \lambda)$. By dynamic programming:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{t+1}(j) &= \max_{q_1 \dots q_t} P(q_1 \dots q_t, q_{t+1} = S_j, O_1 \dots O_{t+1} \mid \lambda) \\[4pt]
&= \max_{1 \leq i \leq N} \left\{ \max_{q_1 \dots q_{t-1}} P(q_1 \dots q_{t-1}, q_t = S_i, O_1 \dots O_t \mid \lambda) \right. \\
&\quad \left. \cdot P(q_{t+1} = S_j \mid q_t = S_i) \cdot P(O_{t+1} \mid q_{t+1} = S_j) \right\} \\[4pt]
&= \max_{1 \leq i \leq N} \bigl[ \delta_t(i) \, a_{ij} \bigr] \, b_j(O_{t+1})
\end{aligned}
$$

The crucial conceptual point is that **$\max$ replaces $\sum$**. The back-pointer $\psi_t(j) = \arg\max_i \delta_{t-1}(i) a_{ij}$ allows reconstruction of the path.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of $\xi_t(i, j)$ (Posterior Transition Probability)

By Bayes' theorem and the definition of forward/backward variables:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\xi_t(i, j) &= P(q_t = S_i, q_{t+1} = S_j \mid O, \lambda) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{P(q_t = S_i, q_{t+1} = S_j, O \mid \lambda)}{P(O \mid \lambda)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{P(O_1 \dots O_t, q_t = S_i \mid \lambda) \cdot P(q_{t+1} = S_j \mid q_t = S_i)}{P(O \mid \lambda)} \\
&\quad \cdot P(O_{t+1} \mid q_{t+1} = S_j) \cdot P(O_{t+2} \dots O_T \mid q_{t+1} = S_j) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\alpha_t(i) \, a_{ij} \, b_j(O_{t+1}) \, \beta_{t+1}(j)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing over $j$ immediately yields $\gamma_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \xi_t(i, j) = \dfrac{\alpha_t(i) \beta_t(i)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Mini-Supervisor)

> [!NOTE] **Toy Problem Setup** (frequently given as sub-part in KTU 14-mark questions)  
> Two hidden states: $S_1$ (Rainy), $S_2$ (Sunny). Two observations: $v_1$ = Coat, $v_2$ = T-shirt.  
> $\pi = (0.6,\; 0.4)$,  
> $\mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}$,  
> $\mathbf{B} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.9 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{pmatrix}$.  
> Observation: $O = (v_1, v_2)$ i.e., Coat then T-shirt, so $T = 2$.

#### Step 1 — Initialise Forward Variables ($t = 1$, $O_1 = v_1$)

$$ \alpha_1(1) = \pi_1 \, b_1(v_1) = 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.54 $$

$$ \alpha_1(2) = \pi_2 \, b_2(v_1) = 0.4 \times 0.2 = 0.08 $$

#### Step 2 — Induction at $t = 2$ ($O_2 = v_2$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_2(1) &= \left[ \alpha_1(1)\, a_{11} + \alpha_1(2)\, a_{21} \right] b_1(v_2) \\
&= [\,0.54 \times 0.7 \;+\; 0.08 \times 0.4\,] \times 0.1 \\
&= [\,0.378 + 0.032\,] \times 0.1 = 0.410 \times 0.1 = 0.041
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_2(2) &= \left[ \alpha_1(1)\, a_{12} + \alpha_1(2)\, a_{22} \right] b_2(v_2) \\
&= [\,0.54 \times 0.3 \;+\; 0.08 \times 0.6\,] \times 0.8 \\
&= [\,0.162 + 0.048\,] \times 0.8 = 0.210 \times 0.8 = 0.168
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Total Likelihood

$$ P(O \mid \lambda) = \alpha_2(1) + \alpha_2(2) = 0.041 + 0.168 = 0.209 $$

#### Step 4 — Viterbi Best Path

$\delta_1(1) = 0.54$, $\delta_1(2) = 0.08$. At $t = 2$:

$$ \delta_2(1) = \max(0.54 \cdot 0.7,\; 0.08 \cdot 0.4) \cdot 0.1 = 0.378 \cdot 0.1 = 0.0378,\quad \psi_2(1) = 1 $$

$$ \delta_2(2) = \max(0.54 \cdot 0.3,\; 0.08 \cdot 0.6) \cdot 0.8 = 0.162 \cdot 0.8 = 0.1296,\quad \psi_2(2) = 1 $$

Best ending state: $q_2^* = \arg\max(\delta_2(1), \delta_2(2)) = S_2$. Backtrack: $\psi_2(2) = 1 \Rightarrow q_1^* = S_1$.  
**Optimal hidden sequence**: $Q^* = (S_1, S_2) = (\text{Rainy}, \text{Sunny})$.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Trade-offs

| Algorithm | Problem Solved | Time Complexity | Space | Optimality | Deterministic? |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Brute Force | 1 | $\mathcal{O}(N^T \cdot T)$ | $\mathcal{O}(T)$ | Exact | Yes |
| Forward | 1 | $\mathcal{O}(N^2 T)$ | $\mathcal{O}(N T)$ | Exact | Yes |
| Backward | 1 (auxiliary) | $\mathcal{O}(N^2 T)$ | $\mathcal{O}(N T)$ | Exact | Yes |
| Viterbi | 2 | $\mathcal{O}(N^2 T)$ | $\mathcal{O}(N T)$ | Exact (MAP) | Yes |
| Baum–Welch | 3 | $\mathcal{O}(N^2 T)$ per iteration | $\mathcal{O}(N T)$ | Local optimum | No (stochastic-free but starts depend on initialisation) |

---

## 3.7 Continuous-Density HMMs (Extension Note)

> [!NOTE] **Beyond Discrete $\mathbf{B}$**
> When observations are continuous (e.g., MFCC speech features), the emission matrix is replaced by a **probability density function** per state. Common choices:
> - **Gaussian HMM**: $b_j(o) = \mathcal{N}(o \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)$
> - **GMM-HMM**: $b_j(o) = \sum_{m=1}^{M} c_{jm}\, \mathcal{N}(o \mid \mu_{jm}, \Sigma_{jm})$
> - **HMM with Neural Emissions** (modern deep learning): emission modelled by an RNN/LSTM.

```mermaid
    t1q2 -. b2 O1 .-> t1o1
```

**Reading guide:** Solid arrows are *state transitions* ($a_{ij}$); dotted arrows are *emissions* ($b_j$). Anything in pink is hidden from the observer; green circles are visible.

> When physical drawings are infeasible, this functional-architecture flow is used. Each block represents one engineering stage. The output of each stage is the input to the next, exactly mirroring the data flow in a deployed HMM-based speech recogniser.

---

## 4.4 Viterbi Trellis (Best Path Visualisation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1t1["delta_1 of S1"]:::node
    s2t1["delta_1 of S2"]:::node
    s1t2["delta_2 of S1"]:::node
    s2t2["delta_2 of S2"]:::node
    s1t3["delta_3 of S1"]:::node
    s2t3["delta_3 of S2"]:::node

    s1t1 -- max a_ij --> s1t2
    s1t1 -- max a_ij --> s2t2
    s2t1 -- max a_ij --> s1t2
    s2t1 -- max a_ij --> s2t2
    s1t2 -- max a_ij --> s1t3
    s1t2 -- max a_ij --> s2t3
    s2t2 -- max a_ij --> s1t3
    s2t2 -- max a_ij --> s2t3

    WIN((Best Final State)):::win
    s1t3 --> WIN
    s2t3 --> WIN

    classDef node fill:#e2d6f3,stroke:#5a3d8a,color:#2a1a44
    classDef win fill:#ffd166,stroke:#a3791f,color:#3d2c0a,stroke-width:2.5px
```

**Reading guide:** Each grey node holds $\delta_t(i)$. Edges show the *max* taken over previous states. The orange node is the terminating best state from which backtracking yields $Q^*$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT] **Mark Distribution Pattern (PECST412 Pattern Recognition)**  
> - Part A: $2 \times 3 = 6$ marks (short answer, one or two sentences + diagram).  
> - Part B (Module Internal Choice): $1 \times 14 = 14$ marks; the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B from each module. Each 14-marker is split into 7 + 7 sub-parts.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a Hidden Markov Model. List its five essential parameters.**

**Model Answer:**
> A Hidden Markov Model (HMM) is a doubly stochastic finite-state Markov process in which the state sequence is unobservable (hidden), but a sequence of observations generated by the states is observable.  
> Its five essential parameters are:
> 1. $N$ — number of hidden states
> 2. $M$ — number of distinct observation symbols
> 3. $\mathbf{A} = [a_{ij}]$ — state transition probability matrix
> 4. $\mathbf{B} = [b_j(k)]$ — observation emission probability matrix
> 5. $\boldsymbol{\pi} = [\pi_i]$ — initial state distribution vector
> 
> Compactly written as $\lambda = (N, M, A, B, \pi)$ or simply $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and briefly explain the three fundamental problems of HMMs.**

**Model Answer:**
> Rabiner's tutorial identifies three canonical problems:
> 1. **Evaluation (Likelihood)**: Given $\lambda$ and observation $O$, compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$. Solved by the Forward–Backward algorithm.
> 2. **Decoding**: Given $\lambda$ and $O$, find the optimal hidden state sequence $Q^*$ that best explains $O$. Solved by the Viterbi algorithm.
> 3. **Learning**: Given only $O$, find $\lambda^*$ that maximises $P(O \mid \lambda)$. Solved by the Baum–Welch (EM) algorithm.
> **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **INSTRUCTION (KTU style)**: *Answer ANY ONE of the following Question A or Question B.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the architecture of a Hidden Markov Model. Discuss the Markov assumption and the output independence assumption in detail. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Derive the forward algorithm recursion step-by-step. Show how it solves the evaluation problem with reduced complexity. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

An HMM is represented as a trellis over time $t = 1 \dots T$ with $N$ hidden states per time slice. At each time step:

- The **hidden process** transitions according to $\mathbf{A}$.
- The **visible process** emits a symbol according to $\mathbf{B}$, conditioned on the current hidden state.

**Diagram** (already shown in Section 4.1): hidden states in the upper row, observations in the lower row, solid arrows = transitions, dotted arrows = emissions.

**Markov Assumption** (first order):  
$$ P(q_{t+1} \mid q_1, q_2, \dots, q_t) = P(q_{t+1} \mid q_t) $$

This means the next state depends only on the present state — *memoryless* across the past.

**Output Independence Assumption**:  
$$ P(O_t \mid q_1, \dots, q_t, O_1, \dots, O_{t-1}) = P(O_t \mid q_t) $$

The current observation depends only on the current hidden state, not on any past states or observations.

**Justification**: Without these assumptions, the joint distribution $P(O, Q \mid \lambda)$ would involve exponentially many terms. The two assumptions enable the *factored product form* used in the forward algorithm.

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Step 1**: Define the forward variable $\alpha_t(i) = P(O_1, \dots, O_t, q_t = S_i \mid \lambda)$.

**Step 2**: Initialisation at $t = 1$: $\alpha_1(i) = \pi_i \, b_i(O_1)$.

**Step 3**: Induction (already derived in Section 3.1.2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{t+1}(j) &= P(O_1 \dots O_{t+1}, q_{t+1} = S_j \mid \lambda) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{N} P(O_1 \dots O_t, q_t = S_i \mid \lambda) \cdot P(q_{t+1} = S_j \mid q_t = S_i) \cdot P(O_{t+1} \mid q_{t+1} = S_j) \\
&= \left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) \, a_{ij}\right] \, b_j(O_{t+1})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4**: Termination: $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i)$.

**Step 5: Complexity argument**:  
- Naive enumeration: $2T \cdot N^T$ operations.  
- Forward algorithm: At each of $T$ time steps, compute $N$ partial sums each over $N$ terms $\Rightarrow \mathcal{O}(N^2 T)$.  
- For $N = 5, T = 100$: $5^{100} \approx 7.8 \times 10^{69}$ vs $5^2 \cdot 100 = 2500$. A massive speedup.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the Viterbi algorithm with all the mathematical steps. How does it differ from the forward algorithm? **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Explain the Baum–Welch algorithm for parameter re-estimation. Derive the re-estimation formula for the transition probability $a_{ij}$. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Viterbi Algorithm — Step-by-Step:**

Define $\delta_t(i) = \max_{q_1 \dots q_{t-1}} P(q_1 \dots q_{t-1}, q_t = S_i, O_1 \dots O_t \mid \lambda)$.

1. **Initialisation** ($t = 1$):  
$$ \delta_1(i) = \pi_i \, b_i(O_1),\quad \psi_1(i) = 0 $$

2. **Recursion** ($t = 2 \dots T$):  
$$ \delta_t(j) = \max_{1 \leq i \leq N} \bigl[\delta_{t-1}(i) \, a_{ij}\bigr] \, b_j(O_t) $$
$$ \psi_t(j) = \arg\max_{1 \leq i \leq N} \bigl[\delta_{t-1}(i) \, a_{ij}\bigr] $$

3. **Termination**: $P^* = \max_i \delta_T(i)$ and $q_T^* = \arg\max_i \delta_T(i)$.

4. **Backtracking** ($t = T-1 \dots 1$): $q_t^* = \psi_{t+1}(q_{t+1}^*)$.

**Difference from Forward Algorithm:**

| Forward | Viterbi |
| :--- | :--- |
| Uses **sum** over previous states | Uses **max** over previous states |
| Computes total likelihood $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | Computes *most probable single path* |
| **No back-pointers** stored | Stores $\psi_t(j)$ for path recovery |
| Solves Problem 1 (Evaluation) | Solves Problem 2 (Decoding) |

Both have $\mathcal{O}(N^2 T)$ complexity.

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Baum–Welch Algorithm (EM for HMMs):**

The Baum–Welch algorithm iteratively re-estimates $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ to maximise $P(O \mid \lambda)$. It is an instance of the **Expectation–Maximisation** algorithm.

**E-step**: Compute the posterior probabilities using the current $\lambda$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_t(i) &= \frac{\alpha_t(i) \, \beta_t(i)}{\sum_{k=1}^{N} \alpha_t(k) \, \beta_t(k)} \\
\xi_t(i, j) &= \frac{\alpha_t(i) \, a_{ij} \, b_j(O_{t+1}) \, \beta_{t+1}(j)}{\sum_{k=1}^{N} \sum_{l=1}^{N} \alpha_t(k) \, a_{kl} \, b_l(O_{t+1}) \, \beta_{t+1}(l)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**M-step**: Re-estimate the parameters by maximising the auxiliary Q-function.

**Derivation of $\bar{a}_{ij}$**:

Expected number of transitions from $S_i$ to $S_j$ across all time steps:

$$ \sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i, j) = \sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \frac{\alpha_t(i) \, a_{ij} \, b_j(O_{t+1}) \, \beta_{t+1}(j)}{P(O \mid \lambda)} $$

Expected total number of transitions from $S_i$:

$$ \sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i) $$

Dividing the two (as the M-step maximiser under row-normalisation) yields:

$$ \bar{a}_{ij} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i, j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i)} $$

The algorithm guarantees $P(O \mid \bar{\lambda}) \geq P(O \mid \lambda)$ but convergence is **only to a local maximum**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing forward variable with backward variable**: $\alpha_t(i)$ is the probability of *the prefix up to $t$* ending in $S_i$, while $\beta_t(i)$ is the probability of *the suffix from $t+1$ onwards* given $S_i$. Mixing these leads to wrong formulas and at least 3-mark deductions.
> 2. **Forgetting the emission factor $b_j(O_{t+1})$** in the forward induction: students often write $\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \sum_i \alpha_t(i) a_{ij}$, missing the multiplication by $b_j(O_{t+1})$. This is a classic 1-mark killer.
> 3. **Termination confusion**: Termination of forward algorithm is $\sum_i \alpha_T(i)$, **not** just $\alpha_T(\text{some }i)$. The forward variable at the final time still needs to be summed over all possible end-states.
> 4. **Viterbi vs Forward**: Both have $\mathcal{O}(N^2 T)$ complexity — students often claim Viterbi is $\mathcal{O}(N^T)$ thinking it's exhaustive. It is *not*; it uses dynamic programming just like the forward algorithm.
> 5. **Baum–Welch guarantee**: It guarantees only a **local** optimum. Writing "global maximum" loses a mark.
> 6. **Stochastic constraints**: Always verify $\sum_j a_{ij} = 1$, $\sum_k b_j(k) = 1$, $\sum_i \pi_i = 1$ when working numerical problems.
> 7. **First-order Markov**: If a question asks "what if the model is second-order?", answer $P(q_{t+1} \mid q_t, q_{t-1})$, not the same first-order formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Box Before Exam)**

- [ ] HMM = **doubly stochastic** process with **hidden** states and **visible** emissions.
- [ ] Parameter quintuple: $\lambda = (N, M, A, B, \pi)$; shorthand $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$.
- [ ] **$A$** is $N \times N$ transitions, **$B$** is $N \times M$ emissions, **$\pi$** is $1 \times N$ initial.
- [ ] All rows of $A$ and $B$ and the vector $\pi$ are probability distributions (non-negative, sum to 1).
- [ ] **Two assumptions**: (i) Markov (first-order) on states, (ii) Output independence.
- [ ] **Three problems**: Evaluation, Decoding, Learning — solved by Forward–Backward, Viterbi, Baum–Welch respectively.
- [ ] **Forward recursion**: $\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \bigl[\sum_i \alpha_t(i) a_{ij}\bigr] b_j(O_{t+1})$.
- [ ] **Backward recursion**: $\beta_t(i) = \sum_j a_{ij} b_j(O_{t+1}) \beta_{t+1}(j)$.
- [ ] **Termination**: $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_i \alpha_T(i) = \sum_i \alpha_t(i) \beta_t(i)$ for any $t$.
- [ ] **Viterbi recursion**: replace $\sum$ by $\max$ in the forward recursion and store back-pointer $\psi_t$.
- [ ] **$\xi_t(i, j)$**: posterior probability of the transition $S_i \to S_j$ at time $t$.
- [ ] **$\gamma_t(i)$**: posterior probability of being in $S_i$ at time $t = \sum_j \xi_t(i, j)$.
- [ ] **Re-estimate** $\pi_i = \gamma_1(i)$, $a_{ij}$, $b_j(k)$ using ratios of expected counts.
- [ ] **Baum–Welch** is EM; convergence is monotonic but only to a local optimum.
- [ ] All three algorithms are $\mathcal{O}(N^2 T)$.
- [ ] Numerical stability: use **scaling** (Forward) and **log-domain** (Viterbi).
- [ ] Common architectures: Ergodic (fully connected) and Left-to-Right (Bakis).
- [ ] Continuous observations: replace discrete $\mathbf{B}$ with GMMs or deep nets.
- [ ] Industrial applications: speech, bioinformatics, finance, fault diagnosis, NLP.

---

**End of Module 4 — Basics of Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) Notes**

topics.title: HMM for Sequence Modeling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST412 - PATTERN RECOGNITION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** HMM for Sequence Modeling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** is a statistical Markov model in which the system being modeled is assumed to be a **Markov process with unobserved (hidden) states**. It is formally defined as a quintuple $\lambda = (S, V, A, B, \pi)$, where:

- $S = \{S_1, S_2, \dots, S_N\}$ — the finite set of **N hidden states**.
- $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_M\}$ — the finite set of **M possible observation symbols**.
- $A = \{a_{ij}\}$ — the **state transition probability matrix**, where $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = S_j \mid q_t = S_i)$, with $\sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} = 1$.
- $B = \{b_j(k)\}$ — the **emission (observation) probability matrix**, where $b_j(k) = P(o_t = v_k \mid q_t = S_j)$, with $\sum_{k=1}^{M} b_j(k) = 1$.
- $\pi = \{\pi_i\}$ — the **initial state distribution**, where $\pi_i = P(q_1 = S_i)$.

For compactness, the HMM is often abbreviated as $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Markov Property (First-Order):** The future state depends only on the present state, and not on the sequence of states that preceded it. Formally: $P(q_{t+1} \mid q_1, q_2, \dots, q_t) = P(q_{t+1} \mid q_t)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **tourist trapped inside a cave** in Kerala during monsoon. You cannot see the weather outside, but you have a friend who lets you observe only one thing: whether the **lotus flowers** in a nearby pond are **open or closed** every morning.

- The **hidden state** is the actual weather: **{Sunny, Rainy}** — you cannot see it directly.
- The **observation** is the lotus state: **{Open, Closed}** — this is what you perceive.
- There is a **transition probability** of weather changing from one day to the next (e.g., Rainy → Rainy is 0.7, Rainy → Sunny is 0.3).
- There is an **emission probability** of the lotus being open given the weather (e.g., if Sunny, lotus is open 90% of the time).

Your goal: based on a sequence of *observations* (lotus open/closed), **infer the most likely sequence of hidden states** (the actual weather) or compute the **likelihood** of the observation sequence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** HMM is fundamental in **Speech Recognition, Bioinformatics (gene prediction, protein modeling), Gesture Recognition, Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging**, and **Time-Series Anomaly Detection**.

## 1.3 Why "Hidden"?

The states are never directly observed. We only observe stochastic outputs (emissions) generated by the hidden states. This **double stochastic process** distinguishes HMM from a plain Markov Chain.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Problems of HMM

Given an HMM $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ and an observation sequence $O = o_1, o_2, \dots, o_T$, HMM theory revolves around solving three canonical problems.

### Problem 1 — The Evaluation Problem
**Question:** Given $\lambda$, compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ — the probability of the observation sequence.

**Use Case:** Pattern classification; determining which model most likely generated the observation.

**Algorithm:** **Forward-Backward Procedure**.

### Problem 2 — The Decoding Problem
**Question:** Given $\lambda$ and $O$, find the optimal state sequence $Q^* = q_1^*, q_2^*, \dots, q_T^*$ that best explains the observations.

**Use Case:** Speech recognition (phoneme → word alignment), POS tagging.

**Algorithm:** **Viterbi Algorithm** (Dynamic Programming).

### Problem 3 — The Learning Problem
**Question:** Adjust model parameters $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ to maximize $P(O \mid \lambda)$.

**Use Case:** Training HMMs from unlabeled data.

**Algorithm:** **Baum-Welch Algorithm** (a special case of Expectation-Maximization).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description / Constraints |
|---|---|---|
| **HMM Tuple** | $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ | Compact representation of an HMM |
| **Transition Prob.** | $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1}=S_j \mid q_t=S_i)$ | $\sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} = 1$, $a_{ij} \geq 0$ |
| **Emission Prob.** | $b_j(k) = P(o_t=v_k \mid q_t=S_j)$ | $\sum_{k=1}^{M} b_j(k) = 1$, $b_j(k) \geq 0$ |
| **Initial Prob.** | $\pi_i = P(q_1 = S_i)$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_i = 1$ |
| **Forward Variable** | $\alpha_t(i) = P(o_1,\dots,o_t, q_t=S_i \mid \lambda)$ | Joint probability of partial obs. and state |
| **Backward Variable** | $\beta_t(i) = P(o_{t+1},\dots,o_T \mid q_t=S_i, \lambda)$ | Probability of future obs. given current state |
| **Forward Recursion** | $\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) a_{ij} \right] b_j(o_{t+1})$ | Computes forward variables |
| **Total Likelihood** | $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \pi_i b_i(o_1) \beta_1(i)$ | Sum over terminal forward variables |
| **Viterbi Recursion** | $\delta_{t+1}(j) = \max_{i} \left[ \delta_t(i) a_{ij} \right] b_j(o_{t+1})$ | Max-prob path score |
| **State Posterior** | $\gamma_t(i) = \dfrac{\alpha_t(i)\beta_t(i)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$ | Probability of being in state $i$ at time $t$ |
| **Transition Posterior** | $\xi_t(i,j) = \dfrac{\alpha_t(i) a_{ij} b_j(o_{t+1}) \beta_{t+1}(j)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$ | Probability of transition $i \to j$ at time $t$ |
| **Baum-Welch: $\pi$** | $\bar{\pi}_i = \gamma_1(i)$ | Re-estimated initial distribution |
| **Baum-Welch: $A$** | $\bar{a}_{ij} = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i,j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i)}$ | Re-estimated transition matrix |
| **Baum-Welch: $B$** | $\bar{b}_j(k) = \dfrac{\sum_{t: o_t = v_k} \gamma_t(j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} \gamma_t(j)}$ | Re-estimated emission matrix |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Speech recognition systems (HTK, Kaldi), gene-finders (GENSCAN), and stock-market regime detectors all rely on these exact formulas.

## 2.3 Why HMM Outperforms Naïve Markov Chains

| Feature | Markov Chain | Hidden Markov Model |
|---|---|---|
| State Visibility | Directly visible | Hidden (inferred via emissions) |
| Memory | First-order | First-order + observation dependence |
| Realism | Limited | Captures noisy/lossy sensor readings |
| Computation | Direct count | Requires Forward-Viterbi-Baum-Welch |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Forward Algorithm (Problem 1)

### 3.1.1 Motivation
The naïve computation of $P(O \mid \lambda)$ requires enumerating **all possible state sequences** of length $T$, which has $N^T$ possibilities. For $N = 5$ and $T = 100$, this is $5^{100} \approx 7.8 \times 10^{69}$ — computationally infeasible. We need a polynomial-time algorithm.

### 3.1.2 Definition of the Forward Variable

Define $\alpha_t(i)$ as the probability of observing the **partial sequence** $o_1, o_2, \dots, o_t$ AND being in state $S_i$ at time $t$:

$$\alpha_t(i) = P(o_1, o_2, \dots, o_t, \, q_t = S_i \mid \lambda)$$

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Initialization ($t = 1$):**
The probability of starting in state $S_i$ AND observing $o_1$ is the product of the initial probability and the emission probability for $o_1$:

$$\alpha_1(i) = \pi_i \cdot b_i(o_1), \quad 1 \leq i \leq N$$

**Step 2 — Induction ($1 \leq t \leq T-1$):**
We derive the forward variable at time $t+1$ by summing over all predecessor states $S_i$. The state $S_j$ at time $t+1$ can be reached from **any** state $S_i$ at time $t$, hence the sum:

$$\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) \, a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(o_{t+1})$$

**Justification of every factor:**

- $\alpha_t(i)$: probability of being at state $S_i$ after seeing $o_1, \dots, o_t$.
- $a_{ij}$: probability of transitioning from $S_i$ to $S_j$.
- The product $\alpha_t(i) \cdot a_{ij}$: probability of the path up to $S_i$ at time $t$ followed by transition to $S_j$ at $t+1$.
- Summing over $i$: marginalizes (sums out) the unknown predecessor — uses the **law of total probability**.
- $b_j(o_{t+1})$: probability that state $S_j$ emits the observed symbol $o_{t+1}$.

**Step 3 — Termination ($t = T$):**
The total likelihood is obtained by summing the forward variable over all possible terminal states:

$$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i)$$

**Complexity:** $O(N^2 \cdot T)$ instead of $O(N^T)$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Backward Variable

By symmetry, define the backward variable:

$$\beta_t(i) = P(o_{t+1}, o_{t+2}, \dots, o_T \mid q_t = S_i, \lambda)$$

**Initialization:**

$$\beta_T(i) = 1, \quad 1 \leq i \leq N$$

**Induction:**

$$\beta_t(i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} \, b_j(o_{t+1}) \, \beta_{t+1}(j), \quad 1 \leq t \leq T-1, \; 1 \leq i \leq N$$

**Justification:** Given we are in $S_i$ at time $t$, we transition to some $S_j$ at $t+1$ (probability $a_{ij}$), emit $o_{t+1}$ (probability $b_j(o_{t+1})$), and then continue producing the rest of the sequence (probability $\beta_{t+1}(j)$). The sum marginalizes over all possible $S_j$.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Viterbi Algorithm (Problem 2)

### 3.3.1 Objective
Find the **single best state sequence** $Q^* = (q_1^*, \dots, q_T^*)$ that maximizes $P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$. Using Bayes' rule, since $P(O \mid \lambda)$ is constant for a given $O$, this is equivalent to:

$$Q^* = \arg\max_Q P(Q, O \mid \lambda)$$

### 3.3.2 The Viterbi Variable
Define $\delta_t(i)$ as the **maximum probability** of any partial path ending in state $S_i$ at time $t$ that produces observations $o_1, \dots, o_t$:

$$\delta_t(i) = \max_{q_1, \dots, q_{t-1}} P(q_1, \dots, q_{t-1}, q_t = S_i, o_1, \dots, o_t \mid \lambda)$$

### 3.3.3 Step-by-Step Recursion

**Step 1 — Initialization:**

$$\delta_1(i) = \pi_i \, b_i(o_1), \quad 1 \leq i \leq N$$

$$\psi_1(i) = 0 \quad \text{(backpointer, not used at t=1)}$$

**Step 2 — Recursion:**

$$\delta_{t+1}(j) = \max_{1 \leq i \leq N} \left[ \delta_t(i) \, a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(o_{t+1})$$

$$\psi_{t+1}(j) = \arg\max_{1 \leq i \leq N} \left[ \delta_t(i) \, a_{ij} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Termination:**

$$P^* = \max_{1 \leq i \leq N} \delta_T(i)$$

$$q_T^* = \arg\max_{1 \leq i \leq N} \delta_T(i)$$

**Step 4 — Path Backtracking:**

$$q_t^* = \psi_{t+1}(q_{t+1}^*), \quad t = T-1, T-2, \dots, 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference from Forward Algorithm:** Viterbi uses **max** over predecessors (instead of **sum**), and stores backpointers $\psi$ to reconstruct the optimal path. This is pure dynamic programming.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Baum-Welch Algorithm (Problem 3)

### 3.4.1 EM-Based Re-estimation

Baum-Welch is a direct application of the **Expectation-Maximization (EM)** principle. We define two posterior quantities.

**The state posterior (probability of being in state $S_i$ at time $t$):**

$$\gamma_t(i) = P(q_t = S_i \mid O, \lambda) = \frac{\alpha_t(i) \, \beta_t(i)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$$

Derivation: $\alpha_t(i)$ gives the joint probability of the "left" half of the sequence AND $q_t = S_i$, while $\beta_t(i)$ gives the conditional probability of the "right" half. Their product is the joint probability of the whole sequence with $q_t = S_i$, and dividing by $P(O \mid \lambda)$ normalizes it to a posterior.

**The transition posterior (probability of transition $S_i \to S_j$ at time $t$):**

$$\xi_t(i, j) = P(q_t = S_i, q_{t+1} = S_j \mid O, \lambda)$$

$$\xi_t(i, j) = \frac{\alpha_t(i) \, a_{ij} \, b_j(o_{t+1}) \, \beta_{t+1}(j)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$$

Derivation: $\alpha_t(i)$ = probability of left half ending at $S_i$. Then transition $S_i \to S_j$ contributes $a_{ij}$, emission at $t+1$ contributes $b_j(o_{t+1})$, and the right half is $\beta_{t+1}(j)$.

### 3.4.2 The M-Step (Re-estimation Formulas)

The re-estimated parameters that maximize the expected log-likelihood are:

$$\bar{\pi}_i = \gamma_1(i) = \frac{\alpha_1(i) \, \beta_1(i)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$$

This makes sense: the expected number of times we start in $S_i$ equals the posterior probability of being in $S_i$ at $t=1$.

$$\bar{a}_{ij} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i, j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i)}$$

**Justification:** The numerator sums the expected number of transitions from $S_i$ to $S_j$ across all time steps; the denominator sums the expected number of times we are in $S_i$ (i.e., the total "leaving" events from $S_i$). The ratio is the expected transition probability.

$$\bar{b}_j(k) = \frac{\sum_{t \,:\, o_t = v_k} \gamma_t(j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} \gamma_t(j)}$$

**Justification:** The numerator sums the posterior probability of being in $S_j$ whenever the observation was $v_k$; the denominator is the total expected time in $S_j$.

### 3.4.3 Convergence
The Baum-Welch algorithm is guaranteed to **monotonically increase** $P(O \mid \lambda)$ (or leave it unchanged) at every iteration, converging to a **local maximum** of the likelihood — not necessarily the global optimum.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HMM Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram illustrates the HMM as a probabilistic graphical model with hidden states, observed emissions, and the three inference pipelines.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S["LATENT LAYER (Hidden States)"]
        q1["q1 at t=1"]:::hiddenNode
        q2["q2 at t=2"]:::hiddenNode
        q3["q3 at t=3"]:::hiddenNode
        qT["qT at t=T"]:::hiddenNode
    end
    subgraph O["OBSERVATION LAYER"]
        o1["o1"]:::obsNode
        o2["o2"]:::obsNode
        o3["o3"]:::obsNode
        oT["oT"]:::obsNode
    end
    pi0["Initial Dist pi"]:::paramNode
    AMat["Transition Matrix A"]:::paramNode
    BMat["Emission Matrix B"]:::paramNode
    pi0 -.-> q1
    q1 -->|aij| q2
    q2 -->|aij| q3
    q3 -.-> qT
    q1 -->|bjko| o1
    q2 -->|bjko| o2
    q3 -->|bjko| o3
    qT -->|bjko| oT
    AMat -.-> q2
    AMat -.-> q3
    BMat -.-> o1
    BMat -.-> o2
    BMat -.-> o3
    classDef hiddenNode fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef obsNode fill:#e07a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef paramNode fill:#81b29a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 The Three HMM Inference Pipelines

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["INPUT: HMM lambda and Obs Sequence O"]:::inputNode
    P1["PROBLEM 1: Evaluation"]:::p1Node
    P2["PROBLEM 2: Decoding"]:::p2Node
    P3["PROBLEM 3: Learning"]:::p3Node
    A1["Compute P(O|lambda)"]:::opNode
    A2["Find best state sequence Q*"]:::opNode
    A3["Re-estimate A, B, pi"]:::opNode
    M1["Forward-Backward Algorithm"]:::methodNode
    M2["Viterbi Algorithm DP"]:::methodNode
    M3["Baum-Welch EM Algorithm"]:::methodNode
    R1["Used for pattern classification"]:::resultNode
    R2["Used for sequence alignment"]:::resultNode
    R3["Used for parameter training"]:::resultNode
    A0 --> P1
    A0 --> P2
    A0 --> P3
    P1 --> A1 --> M1 --> R1
    P2 --> A2 --> M2 --> R2
    P3 --> A3 --> M3 --> R3
    classDef inputNode fill:#f2cc8f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef p1Node fill:#3d405b,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef p2Node fill:#3d405b,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef p3Node fill:#3d405b,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef opNode fill:#81b29a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef methodNode fill:#e07a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef resultNode fill:#f4f1de,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Viterbi Trellis Diagram (Toy Example, N=2, T=4)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1t1["S1 t=1"]:::tNode
    s2t1["S2 t=1"]:::tNode
    s1t2["S1 t=2"]:::tNode
    s2t2["S2 t=2"]:::tNode
    s1t3["S1 t=3"]:::tNode
    s2t3["S2 t=3"]:::tNode
    s1t4["S1 t=4"]:::tNode
    s2t4["S2 t=4"]:::tNode
    bp["argmax backpointers psi"]:::bpNode
    s1t1 -->|a11| s1t2
    s1t1 -->|a12| s2t2
    s2t1 -->|a21| s1t2
    s2t1 -->|a22| s2t2
    s1t2 -->|a11| s1t3
    s1t2 -->|a12| s2t3
    s2t2 -->|a21| s1t3
    s2t2 -->|a22| s2t3
    s1t3 -->|a11| s1t4
    s1t3 -->|a12| s2t4
    s2t3 -->|a21| s1t4
    s2t3 -->|a22| s2t4
    s2t4 -.-> bp
    s1t4 -.-> bp
    classDef tNode fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bpNode fill:#e63946,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

The trellis has $N \times T$ nodes. At each column, only the **best score** is retained, and a backpointer stores which predecessor was chosen.
