# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define Hidden Markov Model. List its five essential elements.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

A Hidden Markov Model is a doubly stochastic process in which the underlying state sequence is **not directly observable** (hidden), but generates an observable output sequence through known emission probabilities.

**Five Essential Elements** $\lambda = (S, V, A, B, \pi)$:

1. **$N$** — Number of hidden states in $S = \{S_1, S_2, \dots, S_N\}$.
2. **$M$** — Number of distinct observation symbols per state, $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_M\}$.
3. **$A = \{a_{ij}\}$** — State transition probability distribution, $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = S_j \mid q_t = S_i)$.
4. **$B = \{b_j(k)\}$** — Observation symbol probability distribution, $b_j(k) = P(o_t = v_k \mid q_t = S_j)$.
5. **$\pi = \{\pi_i\}$** — Initial state distribution, $\pi_i = P(q_1 = S_i)$.

---

### **Q2.** Distinguish between the Evaluation Problem and the Decoding Problem of HMM. Name the algorithm used for each.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Evaluation Problem | Decoding Problem |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ | Find the optimal state sequence $Q^*$ |
| **Input** | $\lambda$, $O$ | $\lambda$, $O$ |
| **Output** | A scalar probability | A state sequence |
| **Method Used** | **Forward-Backward Algorithm** | **Viterbi Algorithm** |
| **Aggregation** | **Sum** over all paths | **Maximum** over all paths |
| **Application** | Model selection, classification | POS tagging, speech alignment |

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Read Carefully!**
> - **Always** show the full forward-variable / Viterbi computation **tabulated** with all intermediate values; missing tables cost 3–4 marks.
> - **Sum must equal 1** along rows of $A$ and columns of $B$ — examiners verify this.
> - For Viterbi, **do not forget the backpointer $\psi$**; the path backtracking step carries 2 marks.
> - In Baum-Welch derivations, **clearly distinguish** between $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \xi$ — confusing them is the #1 mark-deductor.
> - Log-space computation is recommended to avoid underflow; examiners often give bonus credit for this remark.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Viterbi-Focused**

#### (a) Construct and solve the Viterbi algorithm for the following HMM. **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Modified Past Paper]**

**Given:**
- States: $S = \{S_1, S_2\}$
- Observations: $V = \{A, B\}$
- Initial: $\pi = (0.6, 0.4)$
- Transition: $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}$
- Emission: $B = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{pmatrix}$
- Observation sequence: $O = (A, B, A)$

**Step 1 — Initialization ($t = 1$, $o_1 = A$):**

$$\delta_1(1) = \pi_1 \cdot b_1(A) = 0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.30, \quad \psi_1(1) = 0$$

$$\delta_1(2) = \pi_2 \cdot b_2(A) = 0.4 \times 0.3 = 0.12, \quad \psi_1(2) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Recursion at $t = 2$, $o_2 = B$:**

$$\delta_2(1) = \max_i \left[ \delta_1(i) \cdot a_{i1} \right] \cdot b_1(B)$$

- From $S_1$: $0.30 \times 0.7 = 0.21$
- From $S_2$: $0.12 \times 0.4 = 0.048$

Maximum = $0.21$, from $S_1$. So $\delta_2(1) = 0.21 \times 0.5 = 0.105$, $\psi_2(1) = 1$.

$$\delta_2(2) = \max_i \left[ \delta_1(i) \cdot a_{i2} \right] \cdot b_2(B)$$

- From $S_1$: $0.30 \times 0.3 = 0.09$
- From $S_2$: $0.12 \times 0.6 = 0.072$

Maximum = $0.09$, from $S_1$. So $\delta_2(2) = 0.09 \times 0.7 = 0.063$, $\psi_2(2) = 1$.

**Step 3 — Recursion at $t = 3$, $o_3 = A$:**

$$\delta_3(1) = \max_i \left[ \delta_2(i) \cdot a_{i1} \right] \cdot b_1(A)$$

- From $S_1$: $0.105 \times 0.7 = 0.0735$
- From $S_2$: $0.063 \times 0.4 = 0.0252$

Maximum = $0.0735$, $\psi_3(1) = 1$. So $\delta_3(1) = 0.0735 \times 0.5 = 0.03675$.

$$\delta_3(2) = \max_i \left[ \delta_2(i) \cdot a_{i2} \right] \cdot b_2(A)$$

- From $S_1$: $0.105 \times 0.3 = 0.0315$
- From $S_2$: $0.063 \times 0.6 = 0.0378$

Maximum = $0.0378$, $\psi_3(2) = 2$. So $\delta_3(2) = 0.0378 \times 0.3 = 0.01134$.

**Step 4 — Termination:**

$$P^* = \max(0.03675, 0.01134) = 0.03675, \quad q_3^* = S_1$$

**Step 5 — Backtracking:**

$$q_2^* = \psi_3(q_3^*) = \psi_3(1) = 1 \implies S_1$$

$$q_1^* = \psi_2(q_2^*) = \psi_2(1) = 1 \implies S_1$$

**Final Answer:** $Q^* = (S_1, S_1, S_1)$ with $P^* = 0.03675$.

---

#### (b) Explain the three fundamental problems of HMM with examples. **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer (Structured):**

**Problem 1 — Evaluation:**
- **Statement:** Compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ — the probability of an observation sequence given the model.
- **Algorithm:** Forward-Backward procedure.
- **Example:** Given speech models for digits 0–9, compute the likelihood of an unknown audio utterance under each model and pick the one with the highest score.

**Problem 2 — Decoding:**
- **Statement:** Find the optimal hidden state sequence $Q^*$ that maximizes $P(Q \mid O, \lambda)$.
- **Algorithm:** Viterbi algorithm (dynamic programming with backpointers).
- **Example:** Given an audio signal, find the most likely sequence of phonemes that produced it.

**Problem 3 — Learning:**
- **Statement:** Given only observation sequences, estimate the model parameters $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ that maximize $P(O \mid \lambda)$.
- **Algorithm:** Baum-Welch (EM-based, no closed-form solution).
- **Example:** Train a gene-finding HMM on a corpus of unlabelled DNA sequences.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Forward-Backward & Baum-Welch Focused**

#### (a) Compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ for the observation sequence $O = (B, A)$ using the Forward Algorithm. **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Given:** Same HMM as Question A.

**Step 1 — Initialization ($t = 1$, $o_1 = B$):**

$$\alpha_1(1) = \pi_1 \cdot b_1(B) = 0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.30$$

$$\alpha_1(2) = \pi_2 \cdot b_2(B) = 0.4 \times 0.7 = 0.28$$

**Step 2 — Induction at $t = 2$, $o_2 = A$:**

$$\alpha_2(1) = \left[ \alpha_1(1) a_{11} + \alpha_1(2) a_{21} \right] b_1(A)$$

$$\alpha_2(1) = \left[ (0.30)(0.7) + (0.28)(0.4) \right] (0.5) = (0.21 + 0.112)(0.5) = 0.161$$

$$\alpha_2(2) = \left[ \alpha_1(1) a_{12} + \alpha_1(2) a_{22} \right] b_2(A)$$

$$\alpha_2(2) = \left[ (0.30)(0.3) + (0.28)(0.6) \right] (0.3) = (0.09 + 0.168)(0.3) = 0.0774$$

**Step 3 — Termination:**

$$P(O = B, A \mid \lambda) = \alpha_2(1) + \alpha_2(2) = 0.161 + 0.0774 = 0.2384$$

---

#### (b) Derive the Baum-Welch re-estimation formula for the transition matrix $A$. **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Define Expected Transition Count:**
The expected number of transitions from state $S_i$ to $S_j$ given observation $O$ and current model $\lambda$ is:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i, j)$$

where

$$\xi_t(i, j) = \frac{\alpha_t(i) \, a_{ij} \, b_j(o_{t+1}) \, \beta_{t+1}(j)}{\sum_{k=1}^{N} \sum_{l=1}^{N} \alpha_t(k) \, a_{kl} \, b_l(o_{t+1}) \, \beta_{t+1}(l)}$$

**Step 2 — Define Expected Total Time in $S_i$:**

$$\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i) = \sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \frac{\alpha_t(i) \, \beta_t(i)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$$

**Step 3 — M-Step Maximization:**
The Baum-Welch re-estimated transition probability is the ratio of the expected transitions out of $S_i$ that went to $S_j$ over the total expected transitions out of $S_i$:

$$\bar{a}_{ij} = \frac{\text{Expected transitions from } S_i \text{ to } S_j}{\text{Expected total transitions out of } S_i} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i, j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i)}$$

**Step 4 — Justification:**
The numerator counts, in expectation, how often the path used the edge $S_i \to S_j$ across all $T-1$ possible time steps. The denominator counts, in expectation, how often the path was at $S_i$ about to leave. Dividing yields a proper probability (always non-negative, sums to 1 along $j$ for fixed $i$).

**Convergence Note:** The algorithm monotonically increases $P(O \mid \bar{\lambda}) \geq P(O \mid \lambda)$ until a fixed point is reached, but convergence is only to a **local maximum**.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 4 — HMM for Sequence Modeling):**

- **HMM Quintuple:** $\lambda = (S, V, A, B, \pi)$ — memorize and be able to draw the table.
- **First-order Markov Property:** Future $\perp$ Past $\mid$ Present.
- **Three Canonical Problems:** Evaluation (Forward-Backward), Decoding (Viterbi), Learning (Baum-Welch).
- **Forward Variable $\alpha_t(i)$** — joint probability of partial observations AND current state.
- **Backward Variable $\beta_t(i)$** — conditional probability of future observations given current state.
- **Viterbi vs Forward:** Replace $\sum$ with $\max$ and **store backpointers** $\psi$.
- **Time Complexity:** All three algorithms are $O(N^2 T)$ — efficient.
- **Baum-Welch** is **EM-based**; converges monotonically to a **local** optimum.
- **Sum Constraints:** $\sum_j a_{ij} = 1$, $\sum_k b_j(k) = 1$, $\sum_i \pi_i = 1$.
- **Real-World Applications:** Speech recognition (HTK, CMU Sphinx), bioinformatics (GENSCAN, profile HMMs), POS tagging, anomaly detection.
- **Numerical Stability:** Always use **log-probabilities** in real implementations; examiners reward awareness of underflow.
- **Viterbi Path Recovery:** Trace back from $q_T^*$ using $\psi_{t+1}(q_{t+1}^*)$ — a 2-mark question by itself.
- **Limitation of HMM:** Assumes observations are conditionally independent given the state — a strong assumption that motivates **CRF (Conditional Random Fields)** as a successor model.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) — The Foundation Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **Hidden Markov Model (HMM)** is a doubly stochastic process in which the system being modeled is assumed to be a **Markov process with unobserved (hidden) states**, and the observations are probabilistic functions of these hidden states. Formally, an HMM is defined by the **5-tuple** $\lambda = (S, V, A, B, \pi)$:
> 1. $S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_N\}$ — finite set of **N hidden states**
> 2. $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_M\}$ — finite set of **M observation symbols** (vocabulary)
> 3. $A = [a_{ij}]$ — **state transition probability matrix**, where $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = s_j \mid q_t = s_i)$
> 4. $B = [b_j(k)]$ — **emission probability matrix**, where $b_j(k) = P(o_t = v_k \mid q_t = s_j)$
> 5. $\pi = [\pi_i]$ — **initial state distribution**, where $\pi_i = P(q_1 = s_i)$

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Weather-Umbrella" Story
Imagine you are a **prisoner in a dungeon** and cannot see the sky. Every morning, your friend walks in with either a *dry* or *rain-soaked* umbrella. From the sequence of umbrellas, you must **infer the hidden weather** (Sunny, Rainy, Cloudy). The umbrellas are *visible*; the weather is *hidden*. HMMs solve exactly this kind of **inference from observable sequences generated by invisible processes** — and this is precisely why they dominate **speech** and **language** modeling.

---

## 1.2 Applications of HMMs in Speech Processing

> [!NOTE]
> **Speech Recognition (ASR — Automatic Speech Recognition)** is the single most commercially successful application of HMMs. Systems like the classical Sphinx system, HTK, and Kaldi all rely on HMMs as their core statistical engine.

### Why HMMs for Speech?
| Speech Property | How HMM Handles It |
|---|---|
| **Temporal sequentiality** | Markov chain naturally models left-to-right time progression |
| **Variable duration of phonemes** | Self-loop probabilities $a_{ii}$ allow staying in a state |
| **Spectral variability** | Emission probabilities $b_j(k)$ model GMM/MFCC distributions |
| **Unobservable linguistic intent** | Hidden states = phonemes/words; observations = acoustic frames |

### The Speech Recognition Pipeline
1. **Feature Extraction:** Convert raw audio into **MFCC** (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients) or **PLP** vectors every 10–25 ms.
2. **Acoustic Model:** Each **phoneme** is modeled by a 3-state left-to-right HMM.
3. **Language Model:** Predicts the next word given previous words (often $n$-gram or RNN).
4. **Decoder:** Uses the **Viterbi algorithm** to find the most probable word sequence $W^*$:

$$W^* = \underset{W}{\arg\max}\; P(W) \cdot P(O \mid W)$$

where $P(O \mid W)$ is computed via the Forward algorithm and $P(W)$ via the language model.

### Other Speech Applications
- **Speaker Identification / Verification** — Hidden states model speaker traits; observations are MFCCs.
- **Speech Synthesis (TTS)** — HMM-based parametric synthesis (e.g., HTS, used in Festival).
- **Keyword Spotting** — Detecting predefined keywords in continuous audio.
- **Audio Segmentation** — Identifying phoneme/digit boundaries.

---

## 1.3 Applications of HMMs in Language Processing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** tasks that involve **sequential structure** and **latent linguistic categories** are classic HMM playgrounds. The hidden states represent **linguistic labels**; the observations are **words**.

### Key NLP Applications
| Application | Hidden States | Observations |
|---|---|---|
| **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** | Noun, Verb, Adjective, etc. | Words of a sentence |
| **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** | Person, Location, Organization, O | Words in context |
| **Word Sense Disambiguation** | Sense of a polysemous word | Context words |
| **Information Extraction** | Slot labels (e.g., Date, Time) | Tokens |
| **Bioinformatics** | Gene regions (exon/intron) | DNA nucleotides A,C,G,T |

### POS Tagging — A Canonical Example
**Sentence:** *"The cat sat on the mat."*
- **Hidden states:** POS tags $\rightarrow$ `DET NOUN VERB PREP DET NOUN`
- **Observations:** the words $o = \langle$The, cat, sat, on, the, mat, .$\rangle$
- HMM computes: $T^* = \underset{T}{\arg\max} P(T) P(O \mid T)$

where $P(T)$ is captured by the transition matrix $A$ and $P(O \mid T)$ by emission matrix $B$.

---

## 1.4 Ensemble Methods — The Core Idea

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Dietterich, 2000):** An **ensemble method** is a machine learning paradigm where **multiple individual learners (base models)** are **strategically combined** to solve a particular problem. The principle is that a *committee* of models, each capturing different aspects of the data, can outperform any single constituent model — a concept known as the **"Wisdom of the Crowd."**

### Conceptual Analogy — The Medical Council
Imagine you have a **serious medical diagnosis** to make. You have two options:
1. Consult **one doctor** (single model).
2. Consult a **panel of 10 specialists** from different fields (ensemble).

The **panel** is more likely to be correct because:
- Each specialist makes **independent errors**.
- Errors get **outvoted** by correct opinions.
- The panel **averages out noise** in any single opinion.

This is the **essence of ensemble learning** — combining diverse, moderately accurate learners to produce a **highly accurate and robust** final predictor.

### Why Do Ensembles Work? — Three Pillars
1. **Statistical Reason:** A learning algorithm may find multiple hypotheses equally good given limited data. Averaging reduces the risk of choosing the *wrong* one.
2. **Computational Reason:** Many algorithms (e.g., neural networks) can get stuck in local optima. Ensembles explore different starting points.
3. **Representational Reason:** The true function $f(x)$ may not be representable by any single model in the hypothesis space $\mathcal{H}$. A weighted sum of hypotheses can **expand the effective hypothesis space**.

### Mathematical Justification — Condorcet's Jury Theorem
If each classifier has accuracy $p > 0.5$ and they are **independent**, then the probability that the **majority vote is correct** is:

$$P(\text{ensemble correct}) = \sum_{k=0}^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} \binom{n}{k} p^{n-k} (1-p)^k$$

As $n \to \infty$, this probability approaches **1**, even though each individual $p < 1$.

---

## 1.5 Types of Ensemble Methods — The Big Three

| Method | Strategy | Key Algorithm | Year |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bagging** (Bootstrap AGGregatING) | Reduce **variance**; train on resamples | Random Forest | 1996 |
| **Boosting** | Reduce **bias**; train sequentially on errors | AdaBoost, Gradient Boost, XGBoost | 1990–2016 |
| **Stacking** (Stacked Generalization) | Learn a meta-learner over base learners | Stacked Generalization | 1992 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bayesian Model Averaging (BMA)** and **Voting** (hard/soft) are also widely-used ensemble techniques, often considered foundational building blocks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HMM — The Three Fundamental Problems

Rabiner (1989) formalized HMMs through **three canonical problems** that must be solved for any application:

### Problem 1 — Evaluation
**Given** $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ and observation sequence $O = o_1, o_2, \dots, o_T$.
**Find** $P(O \mid \lambda)$ — probability that the model generated $O$.

- **Solution:** **Forward Algorithm** (dynamic programming).

$$\alpha_t(j) = P(o_1, o_2, \dots, o_t, q_t = s_j \mid \lambda)$$

with initialization $\alpha_1(j) = \pi_j b_j(o_1)$ and recursion:

$$\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_t(i) a_{ij} \right] b_j(o_{t+1})$$

Final answer: $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i)$.

- **Why needed in speech/language:** To score how well a candidate word/tag sequence matches the model.

### Problem 2 — Decoding
**Given** $\lambda$ and $O$. **Find** the optimal hidden state sequence $Q^* = q_1^*, q_2^*, \dots, q_T^*$.

- **Solution:** **Viterbi Algorithm** (DP on log-probabilities for numerical stability).

Define the Viterbi variable:

$$\delta_t(j) = \max_{q_1,\dots,q_{t-1}} P(q_1,\dots,q_{t-1}, q_t = s_j, o_1,\dots,o_t \mid \lambda)$$

Recursion:

$$\delta_{t+1}(j) = \max_{i} \left[ \delta_t(i) a_{ij} \right] b_j(o_{t+1})$$

with back-pointer $\psi_{t+1}(j) = \arg\max_i \left[ \delta_t(i) a_{ij} \right]$.

- **Why needed:** The decoder in ASR uses Viterbi to find the most probable word sequence. POS taggers use it to find the most probable tag sequence.

### Problem 3 — Learning
**Given** observation sequence(s) $O$. **Find** $\lambda^* = (A^*, B^*, \pi^*)$ that **maximizes** $P(O \mid \lambda)$.

- **Solution:** **Baum–Welch Algorithm** (a special case of the **EM algorithm**).

E-step: compute forward $\alpha_t(i)$ and backward $\beta_t(i)$ variables.

$$\beta_t(i) = P(o_{t+1}, \dots, o_T \mid q_t = s_i, \lambda)$$

M-step re-estimates:

$$\pi_i^* = \gamma_1(i) = \frac{\alpha_1(i)\beta_1(i)}{P(O \mid \lambda)}$$

$$a_{ij}^* = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \xi_t(i,j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T-1} \gamma_t(i)}$$

$$b_j^*(k) = \frac{\sum_{t=1,\,o_t = v_k}^{T} \gamma_t(j)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} \gamma_t(j)}$$

where $\gamma_t(i) = P(q_t = s_i \mid O, \lambda)$ and $\xi_t(i,j) = P(q_t = s_i, q_{t+1} = s_j \mid O, \lambda)$.

---

## 2.2 HMM Topology for Speech Recognition

For speech, a **3-state left-to-right (Bakis) HMM** is used per phoneme:

```mermaid
graph LR
    s1((s1)) -- a11 self-loop --> s1
    s1 -- a12 --> s2((s2))
    s2 -- a22 self-loop --> s2
    s2 -- a23 --> s3((s3))
    s3 -- a33 self-loop --> s3
```

- $s_1$ = beginning of phoneme (transient)
- $s_2$ = middle of phoneme (stable, has highest self-loop $a_{22}$)
- $s_3$ = end of phoneme (transient)

This topology enforces **left-to-right temporal flow** and allows **variable duration** (more self-loops $\Rightarrow$ longer phoneme).

---

## 2.3 Ensemble Methods — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.3.1 Bias-Variance Decomposition

For a regression problem with target $y$ and prediction $f(x)$, the expected squared error decomposes as:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[ (y - \hat{f}(x))^2 \right] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(x)]}_{\text{systematic error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)]}_{\text{variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible noise}}$$

- **Bagging** primarily **reduces variance** without increasing bias.
- **Boosting** primarily **reduces bias** by iteratively reweighting hard examples.
- **Stacking** can reduce both, depending on the meta-learner.

### 2.3.2 Ensemble Error Bound (Regression)

If we average $M$ independent base predictors $f_1, \dots, f_M$ with variance $\sigma^2$ each:

$$\text{Var}\left( \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} f_i \right) = \frac{\sigma^2}{M}$$

So averaging $M$ independent models **reduces variance by a factor of M** — a powerful theoretical guarantee.

For **classification**, with independent errors and majority voting, accuracy improves superlinearly with $M$ (Condorcet).

### 2.3.3 Diversity — The Key to a Good Ensemble

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diversity** among base learners is essential. If all base learners make the **same errors**, ensembling offers no benefit. Diversity is measured by:
> - **Disagreement measure** (for classification)
> - **Pearson correlation** of predictions
> - **Q-statistic**, **Kappa statistic**, **double-fault measure**

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every key formula** tested in KTU Module 4 examinations. Memorize the symbols, not the derivations, for the 3-mark questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | HMM definition | $\lambda = (S, V, A, B, \pi)$ | 5-tuple |
| 2 | Transition prob. | $a_{ij} = P(q_{t+1} = s_j \mid q_t = s_i)$ | $A$ matrix |
| 3 | Emission prob. | $b_j(k) = P(o_t = v_k \mid q_t = s_j)$ | $B$ matrix |
| 4 | Initial prob. | $\pi_i = P(q_1 = s_i)$ | Starting state |
| 5 | Markov assumption | $P(q_{t+1} \mid q_t, \dots, q_1) = P(q_{t+1} \mid q_t)$ | First-order |
| 6 | Forward variable | $\alpha_t(j) = P(o_1,\dots,o_t, q_t = s_j \mid \lambda)$ | DP forward |
| 7 | Backward variable | $\beta_t(j) = P(o_{t+1},\dots,o_T \mid q_t = s_j, \lambda)$ | DP backward |
| 8 | Forward recursion | $\alpha_{t+1}(j) = \left[ \sum_i \alpha_t(i) a_{ij} \right] b_j(o_{t+1})$ | Evaluation |
| 9 | Viterbi recursion | $\delta_{t+1}(j) = \max_i \left[ \delta_t(i) a_{ij} \right] b_j(o_{t+1})$ | Decoding |
| 10 | Baum–Welch $\pi$ | $\pi_i^* = \gamma_1(i)$ | Initial re-est. |
| 11 | Baum–Welch $A$ | $a_{ij}^* = \frac{\sum_t \xi_t(i,j)}{\sum_t \gamma_t(i)}$ | Transition re-est. |
| 12 | Baum–Welch $B$ | $b_j^*(k) = \frac{\sum_{t: o_t = v_k} \gamma_t(j)}{\sum_t \gamma_t(j)}$ | Emission re-est. |
| 13 | Bias-Variance | $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Decomposition |
| 14 | Ensemble variance | $\text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{M}\sum_i f_i\right) = \frac{\sigma^2}{M}$ | Averaging effect |
| 15 | Condorcet jury | $P_{\text{correct}} = \sum_{k=0}^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} \binom{n}{k} p^{n-k} (1-p)^k$ | Majority vote |
| 16 | Bagging output | $f_{\text{bag}}(x) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} f_m(x)$ | Regression avg. |
| 17 | Hard voting | $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{f_1(x), \dots, f_M(x)\}$ | Classification |
| 18 | Soft voting | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_c \sum_{m=1}^{M} w_m P_m(y = c \mid x)$ | Prob. average |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Forward Algorithm in HMM

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic 2-state, 2-observation** toy problem. It is the most common computation question in KTU exams.

**Given:**
- States: $S = \{s_1, s_2\}$ (Sunny, Rainy)
- Observations: $V = \{v_1, v_2\}$ (Walk, Shop)
- Initial: $\pi = [\pi_1, \pi_2] = [0.6, 0.4]$
- Transition matrix:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

- Emission matrix:

$$B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 & 0.1 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$$

- Observation sequence: $O = \langle v_1, v_2, v_1 \rangle$ (Walk, Shop, Walk)

**Goal:** Compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ using the Forward algorithm.

### Step 1 — Initialization ($t = 1$)

We need $\alpha_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(o_1) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(\text{Walk})$.

**State $s_1$ (Sunny):**
- $o_1 = v_1 = $ Walk
- $b_1(\text{Walk}) = B[1,1] = 0.9$
- $\alpha_1(1) = \pi_1 \cdot b_1(v_1) = 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.54$

**State $s_2$ (Rainy):**
- $b_2(\text{Walk}) = B[2,1] = 0.2$
- $\alpha_1(2) = \pi_2 \cdot b_2(v_1) = 0.4 \times 0.2 = 0.08$

### Step 2 — Induction ($t = 2$, observe Shop = $v_2$)

$$\alpha_2(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{2} \alpha_1(i) a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(v_2)$$

**State $s_1$:**
- Inner sum: $\alpha_1(1) a_{11} + \alpha_1(2) a_{21} = (0.54)(0.7) + (0.08)(0.4) = 0.378 + 0.032 = 0.410$
- $b_1(\text{Shop}) = B[1,2] = 0.1$
- $\alpha_2(1) = 0.410 \times 0.1 = 0.0410$

**State $s_2$:**
- Inner sum: $\alpha_1(1) a_{12} + \alpha_1(2) a_{22} = (0.54)(0.3) + (0.08)(0.6) = 0.162 + 0.048 = 0.210$
- $b_2(\text{Shop}) = B[2,2] = 0.8$
- $\alpha_2(2) = 0.210 \times 0.8 = 0.1680$

### Step 3 — Induction ($t = 3$, observe Walk = $v_1$)

$$\alpha_3(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{2} \alpha_2(i) a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(v_1)$$

**State $s_1$:**
- Inner sum: $\alpha_2(1) a_{11} + \alpha_2(2) a_{21} = (0.0410)(0.7) + (0.1680)(0.4) = 0.02870 + 0.06720 = 0.09590$
- $b_1(\text{Walk}) = 0.9$
- $\alpha_3(1) = 0.09590 \times 0.9 = 0.08631$

**State $s_2$:**
- Inner sum: $\alpha_2(1) a_{12} + \alpha_2(2) a_{22} = (0.0410)(0.3) + (0.1680)(0.6) = 0.01230 + 0.10080 = 0.11310$
- $b_2(\text{Walk}) = 0.2$
- $\alpha_3(2) = 0.11310 \times 0.2 = 0.02262$

### Step 4 — Termination

$$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{2} \alpha_3(i) = 0.08631 + 0.02262 = 0.10893$$

$$\boxed{P(O \mid \lambda) \approx 0.1089}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Numerical Underflow:** In real systems with $T > 100$, multiplying many small probabilities causes underflow. The solution is to compute in **log-space**: $\log P(O \mid \lambda) = \log \sum_i \alpha_T(i)$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Viterbi Decoding

Same HMM $\lambda$ and observation $O = \langle v_1, v_2, v_1 \rangle$.
**Goal:** Find the most likely hidden state sequence $Q^*$.

### Step 1 — Initialization

$$\delta_1(j) = \pi_j \cdot b_j(v_1)$$

- $\delta_1(1) = 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.54$
- $\delta_1(2) = 0.4 \times 0.2 = 0.08$
- $\psi_1(j) = 0$ (no predecessor at $t=1$)

### Step 2 — Recursion $t = 2$ (observe $v_2 = $ Shop)

For each $j$, compute $\delta_2(j) = \max_i \left[ \delta_1(i) a_{ij} \right] \cdot b_j(v_2)$.

**State $s_1$:**
- $\delta_1(1) a_{11} = 0.54 \times 0.7 = 0.378$
- $\delta_1(2) a_{21} = 0.08 \times 0.4 = 0.032$
- $\max = 0.378$, so $\psi_2(1) = 1$
- $\delta_2(1) = 0.378 \times 0.1 = 0.0378$

**State $s_2$:**
- $\delta_1(1) a_{12} = 0.54 \times 0.3 = 0.162$
- $\delta_1(2) a_{22} = 0.08 \times 0.6 = 0.048$
- $\max = 0.162$, so $\psi_2(2) = 1$
- $\delta_2(2) = 0.162 \times 0.8 = 0.1296$

### Step 3 — Recursion $t = 3$ (observe $v_1 = $ Walk)

**State $s_1$:**
- $\delta_2(1) a_{11} = 0.0378 \times 0.7 = 0.02646$
- $\delta_2(2) a_{21} = 0.1296 \times 0.4 = 0.05184$
- $\max = 0.05184$, so $\psi_3(1) = 2$
- $\delta_3(1) = 0.05184 \times 0.9 = 0.046656$

**State $s_2$:**
- $\delta_2(1) a_{12} = 0.0378 \times 0.3 = 0.01134$
- $\delta_2(2) a_{22} = 0.1296 \times 0.6 = 0.07776$
- $\max = 0.07776$, so $\psi_3(2) = 2$
- $\delta_3(2) = 0.07776 \times 0.2 = 0.015552$

### Step 4 — Termination and Backtracking

- Best final probability: $P^* = \max(\delta_3(1), \delta_3(2)) = \max(0.046656, 0.015552) = 0.046656$
- $q_3^* = \arg\max \delta_3 = 1$ (Sunny)
- $q_2^* = \psi_3(1) = 2$ (Rainy)
- $q_1^* = \psi_2(2) = 1$ (Sunny)

$$\boxed{Q^* = \langle s_1, s_2, s_1 \rangle = \langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Rainy}, \text{Sunny} \rangle}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Bagging vs Boosting vs Stacking

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often ask a **"Compare and contrast"** question (7 or 14 marks). The following analytical matrix provides a complete answer framework.

| Property | **Bagging** | **Boosting** | **Stacking** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Reduce variance | Reduce bias (and variance) | Improve any metric |
| **Training style** | Parallel, independent | Sequential, dependent | Two-level (base + meta) |
| **Data sampling** | Bootstrap (with replacement) | Weighted re-sampling | No re-sampling |
| **Weight handling** | Equal weights | Adaptive weights $\alpha_m$ | Meta-learner decides |
| **Base learners** | High-variance (deep trees) | High-bias (shallow trees/stumps) | Heterogeneous (any type) |
| **Output combining** | Average / majority vote | Weighted majority vote | Meta-learner function |
| **Sensitive to noise** | No | **Yes** (overfits noisy data) | Depends on meta-learner |
| **Interpretability** | Moderate | Low | Very low |
| **Computational cost** | Parallelizable, fast | Sequential, slower | High (two levels of training) |
| **Canonical algorithm** | Random Forest | AdaBoost, Gradient Boost, XGBoost | Stacked Generalization |
| **Final output (regression)** | $\frac{1}{M}\sum_m f_m(x)$ | $\sum_m \alpha_m f_m(x)$ | $f_{\text{meta}}(\mathbf{f}(x))$ |
| **When to use** | Unstable base learners, large variance | Underfitting, weak base learners | Mix of strong, diverse models |

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) with HMMs

```mermaid
graph TD
    audio["Raw Audio Signal x t"] --> feat["Feature Extraction MFCC / PLP / Filterbank"]
    feat --> am["Acoustic Model HMM per phoneme GMM emission"]
    lm["Language Model N-gram probabilities"] --> decoder
    lex["Lexicon / Pronunciation Dictionary"] --> decoder
    am --> decoder["Decoder Viterbi Search Beam Search"]
    decoder --> text["Output Text Sentence"]
    
    style audio fill:#e1f5ff
    style text fill:#d4edda
    style decoder fill:#fff3cd
    style am fill:#f8d7da
```

**Description of blocks:**
- **Raw Audio:** 16 kHz PCM waveform.
- **Feature Extraction:** 25 ms window, 10 ms hop, 13 MFCCs + deltas.
- **Acoustic Model:** 3-state HMM per phoneme, GMM emissions, trained on labeled audio.
- **Language Model:** Probabilistic $n$-gram (e.g., bigram, trigram).
- **Decoder:** Viterbi over a **word lattice** combining acoustic scores, LM scores, and pronunciation probabilities.
- **Output:** Best hypothesis word sequence.

---

```mermaid

    x["Input Features x"] --> l0["Level 0 Base Learners"]
    l0 --> l1["Model A Decision Tree"]
    l0 --> l2["Model B SVM"]
```

**Key insight:** Stacking uses **out-of-fold predictions** from base learners as input to the meta-learner to prevent data leakage.
