<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Decision Trees and Random Forests

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Decision Trees — Formal Definition

A **Decision Tree** is a non-parametric, supervised machine learning model used for both **classification** and **regression** tasks. It recursively partitions the feature space into a hierarchical tree-like structure of decision nodes and leaf nodes, where each internal node represents a test on a feature, each branch represents the outcome of that test, and each leaf node represents a class label (classification) or a continuous value (regression).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:** The 2024 scheme specifically mandates study of **CART (Classification and Regression Trees)**, **ID3**, and **C4.5** algorithms. Students must be able to derive impurity measures and compute splits by hand.

Mathematically, a decision tree approximates a function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ by partitioning the input space $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ into axis-aligned rectangular regions $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_M$, with prediction for region $R_m$ given as:

$$\hat{y}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{m=1}^{M} c_m \cdot \mathbb{I}(\mathbf{x} \in R_m)$$

where $c_m$ is the predicted class/value in region $R_m$ and $\mathbb{I}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Twenty Questions" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** A decision tree works exactly like the game of **"Twenty Questions"** you play with a friend. With each question, you eliminate a large chunk of possibilities. *"Is the animal warm-blooded?"* — Yes? Then it's not a reptile. *"Does it have feathers?"* — Yes? Then it's a bird. Each question splits your remaining possibilities. By the end, you've narrowed down to one specific answer through a series of clever yes/no questions chosen to give the **most information** at each step.

### 1.3 Random Forests — Formal Definition

A **Random Forest** is an **ensemble learning** method that constructs a multitude of decision trees ($B$ trees, typically $B \in [100, 1000]$) at training time and outputs the class that is the **mode** (majority vote) of the classes (classification) or **mean prediction** (regression) of the individual trees. It combines the principles of **bagging (Bootstrap AGGregatING)** and **random feature selection** to produce a model with significantly reduced variance compared to a single decision tree.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Sources of Randomness in Random Forests:**
> 1. **Bootstrap Sampling** — Each tree is trained on a random sample of training data drawn *with replacement*.
> 2. **Random Feature Subspace** — At each split, only a random subset $m \approx \sqrt{d}$ of features is considered (for classification), introducing **decorrelation** between trees.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wisdom of Crowds" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you want to predict tomorrow's weather. Asking **one** expert (a single deep decision tree) might lead to overfitting — that expert memorizes too many idiosyncratic patterns. Instead, ask **100 meteorologists** (the forest) from different regions, each with slightly different training data and slightly different focus areas. The **majority opinion** is almost always more accurate than any single expert. Random Forest is the mathematical formalization of this democratic voting principle.

### 1.5 Key Engineering Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Number of trees in forest | $B$ | **100 – 1000** |
| Features considered at each split (classification) | $m_{try}$ | $\sqrt{d}$ |
| Features considered at each split (regression) | $m_{try}$ | $d/3$ |
| Bootstrap sample size | $N$ | Equal to original dataset size $N$ |
| Out-of-bag fraction | OOB | $\approx 1 - (1 - 1/N)^N \approx \mathbf{0.368}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The constant $\mathbf{0.368}$ ($\approx 36.8\%$) is a consequence of the **Poisson limit** and represents the asymptotic probability that any single training sample is **not** selected in a bootstrap sample. These unused samples form the **OOB (Out-of-Bag) set** used for unbiased error estimation — a "free" cross-validation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Decision Tree

A decision tree is built through a **top-down, greedy recursive partitioning** strategy known as **recursive binary splitting**. The procedure is:

1. Start at the **root node** containing all training samples.
2. For every possible feature $X_j$ and every possible split point $s$, evaluate the **impurity reduction** $\Delta I$.
3. Choose the split $(j^*, s^*)$ that **maximizes** the impurity reduction.
4. Partition the data into two child nodes.
5. Recurse on each child until a **stopping criterion** is met (max depth, min samples, pure node).
6. Apply **pruning** to reduce overfitting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greedy Choice Justification:** The greedy choice is *not* guaranteed to be globally optimal (finding the optimal tree is NP-complete), but it produces models with excellent empirical performance and O($N \log N$) complexity in practice.

### 2.2 Impurity Measures — The Core of Splits

For a node $t$ containing $N_t$ samples with class proportions $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_c$ (where $p_i = N_i^{(t)}/N_t$), the three standard impurity measures are:

#### 2.2.1 Entropy (used by ID3, C4.5)

$$H(t) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

- Range: $0 \leq H(t) \leq \log_2 c$
- $H(t) = 0$ when node is **pure** (all samples in one class)
- $H(t) = \log_2 c$ when classes are **uniformly distributed**

#### 2.2.2 Gini Impurity (used by CART)

$$G(t) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2 = \sum_{i \neq j} p_i p_j$$

- Range: $0 \leq G(t) \leq 1 - 1/c$
- $G(t) = 0$ when node is **pure**
- $G(t)$ is the probability of misclassifying a randomly chosen sample if labeled according to the class distribution

#### 2.2.3 Classification Error (used as a stopping criterion)

$$E(t) = 1 - \max_i p_i$$

### 2.3 Information Gain and Splitting Criterion

The **Information Gain (IG)** from splitting node $t$ on feature $X_j$ at threshold $s$ is:

$$IG(t, j, s) = I(t) - \left[ \frac{N_{t_L}}{N_t} I(t_L) + \frac{N_{t_R}}{N_t} I(t_R) \right]$$

where $I(\cdot)$ is any impurity measure (Entropy, Gini), and $t_L, t_R$ are the left and right child nodes.

For **C4.5**, the **Gain Ratio** normalizes IG by the **intrinsic split information** to penalize high-cardinality features:

$$GR(t, j, s) = \frac{IG(t, j, s)}{SI(t, j, s)}$$

$$SI(t, j, s) = -\sum_{v=1}^{V} \frac{N_{t,v}}{N_t} \log_2 \frac{N_{t,v}}{N_t}$$

### 2.4 Regression Trees

For regression, the impurity measure is the **residual sum of squares (RSS)** within a node:

$$RSS(t) = \sum_{i \in t} (y_i - \bar{y}_t)^2$$

The predicted value at leaf $t$ is the **mean** of training targets: $\hat{y}_t = \bar{y}_t = \frac{1}{N_t} \sum_{i \in t} y_i$.

### 2.5 Pruning — Controlling Overfitting

**Pre-pruning (Early Stopping):** Halt tree growth when:
- Maximum depth exceeded
- Minimum samples at a node below threshold
- Impurity reduction falls below $\epsilon$

**Post-pruning (Cost-Complexity Pruning in CART):** Replace a subtree with a leaf if the cost-complexity criterion improves:

$$C_{\alpha}(T) = \sum_{t=1}^{\vert T \vert} N_t \cdot I(t) + \alpha \vert T \vert$$

where $\vert T \vert$ is the number of terminal nodes and $\alpha \geq 0$ is a regularization parameter trading off fit vs. complexity.

### 2.6 Random Forest — Mathematical Formulation

Given training data $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$:

1. For $b = 1, 2, \ldots, B$:
   - Draw a **bootstrap sample** $\mathcal{D}_b$ of size $N$ from $\mathcal{D}$ (with replacement).
   - Grow an unpruned decision tree $T_b$ on $\mathcal{D}_b$ with the modification: at each node, consider only a random subset of $m_{try}$ features for splitting.
2. Output final prediction:
   - **Classification (majority vote):** $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{T_1(\mathbf{x}), T_2(\mathbf{x}), \ldots, T_B(\mathbf{x})\}$
   - **Regression (averaging):** $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} T_b(\mathbf{x})$

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Below is the complete formula reference. Note the use of `\vert` instead of $\vert$ for absolute value to maintain markdown table integrity.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Range / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Entropy | $H(t) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $0 \leq H \leq \log_2 c$ |
| 2 | Gini Impurity | $G(t) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$ | $0 \leq G \leq 1 - 1/c$ |
| 3 | Classification Error | $E(t) = 1 - \max_i p_i$ | $0 \leq E \leq 1 - 1/c$ |
| 4 | Information Gain | $IG = H(t) - \frac{N_{t_L}}{N_t} H(t_L) - \frac{N_{t_R}}{N_t} H(t_R)$ | Higher is better |
| 5 | Split Information | $SI = -\sum_{v} \frac{N_v}{N_t} \log_2 \frac{N_v}{N_t}$ | Used in Gain Ratio |
| 6 | Gain Ratio | $GR = IG / SI$ | Used by C4.5 |
| 7 | RSS (Regression) | $RSS = \sum_{i \in t} (y_i - \bar{y}_t)^2$ | Minimized at each split |
| 8 | Cost-Complexity | $C_{\alpha}(T) = \sum N_t I(t) + \alpha \vert T \vert$ | $\alpha \geq 0$ |
| 9 | OOB Probability | $P(\text{OOB}) = (1 - 1/N)^N \approx e^{-1} \approx 0.368$ | As $N \to \infty$ |
| 10 | RF Classification | $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{T_b(\mathbf{x})\}_{b=1}^{B}$ | Majority vote |
| 11 | RF Regression | $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} T_b(\mathbf{x})$ | Mean prediction |
| 12 | Feature subset (class.) | $m_{try} = \sqrt{d}$ | Default in sklearn |
| 13 | Feature subset (reg.) | $m_{try} = d/3$ | Default in sklearn |
| 14 | Gini Importance | $FI_j = \sum_{t \in T} \Delta G(t) \cdot \mathbb{I}(X_j \text{ splits at } t)$ | Mean decrease in Gini |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Applications of Decision Trees & Random Forests:**
> - **Medical Diagnosis:** Predicting disease presence from patient symptoms (interpretable rules crucial for doctor trust).
> - **Credit Risk Scoring:** Banks use Random Forest to predict loan default probability from applicant features.
> - **Remote Sensing:** Land-cover classification from satellite imagery (Random Forest is the de-facto standard in Earth observation).
> - **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression classification, biomarker discovery.
> - **Anomaly Detection in IoT:** Intrusion detection in network traffic.
> - **Feature Selection:** Gini importance from Random Forest is a benchmark for ranking features.

### 2.9 Why Random Forest Works — The Variance Reduction Argument

A single decision tree has **low bias but high variance** — small perturbations in training data can lead to very different tree structures. Let $\hat{f}_b$ be trees trained on bootstrap samples. The aggregation gives:

$$\text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_b\right) = \rho \sigma^2 + \frac{1 - \rho}{B} \sigma^2$$

where $\rho$ is the **pairwise correlation** between trees and $\sigma^2$ is the variance of a single tree. By **decorrelating** trees (via random feature selection), $\rho$ is reduced, dramatically lowering the ensemble's variance.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of Gini and Entropy Impurity

We demonstrate that for small $p$ (near-pure nodes), Gini and Entropy are approximately equivalent, explaining why CART (Gini) and ID3 (Entropy) often produce similar splits.

The Taylor expansion of $\log_2(1 - x) \approx -x/\ln 2$ around $x = 0$ gives:

$$\log_2 p_i = \log_2 \left[ (1 - (1 - p_i)) \right] \approx \frac{(1 - p_i)}{\ln 2} \quad \text{(first order)}$$

Wait — a cleaner approach. Use the identity $p_i \log_2 p_i = p_i \cdot \frac{\ln p_i}{\ln 2}$. For two classes, set $p_1 = p$, $p_2 = 1 - p$:

$$H(p) = -p \log_2 p - (1-p) \log_2 (1-p)$$

$$G(p) = 1 - p^2 - (1-p)^2 = 2p(1-p)$$

Both vanish at $p = 0$ and $p = 1$ and are symmetric about $p = 0.5$. At $p = 0.5$:

$$H(0.5) = -0.5 \log_2 0.5 - 0.5 \log_2 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ bit}$$

$$G(0.5) = 2(0.5)(0.5) = 0.5$$

The relative shape is preserved, hence both measures rank splits similarly.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Building a Decision Tree (ID3) by Hand

**Dataset (PlayTennis — classic KTU problem):**

| Day | Outlook | Temp | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | No |
| 2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | No |
| 3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | Yes |
| 4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | Yes |
| 5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | No |
| 7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 8 | Sunny | Mild | High | Weak | No |
| 9 | Sunny | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 10 | Rain | Mild | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 11 | Sunny | Mild | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 12 | Overcast | Mild | High | Strong | Yes |
| 13 | Overcast | Hot | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 14 | Rain | Mild | High | Strong | No |

Counts: Yes = 9, No = 5, Total = 14.

**Step 1: Entropy of root node $S$:**

$$H(S) = -\frac{9}{14} \log_2 \frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14} \log_2 \frac{5}{14}$$

$$= -0.6429 \times (-0.6374) - 0.3571 \times (-1.4854)$$

$$= 0.4098 + 0.5304 = 0.9402 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2: Compute IG for each feature.**

**Feature: Wind** (values: Weak, Strong)

- Weak: 8 samples → 6 Yes, 2 No
- Strong: 6 samples → 3 Yes, 3 No

$$H(\text{Weak}) = -\frac{6}{8} \log_2 \frac{6}{8} - \frac{2}{8} \log_2 \frac{2}{8}$$

$$= -0.75 \log_2 0.75 - 0.25 \log_2 0.25 = 0.75 \times 0.4150 + 0.25 \times 2 = 0.3113 + 0.5 = 0.8113 \text{ bits}$$

$$H(\text{Strong}) = -\frac{3}{6} \log_2 \frac{3}{6} - \frac{3}{6} \log_2 \frac{3}{6} = 1.0 \text{ bit}$$

$$IG(\text{Wind}) = 0.9402 - \left(\frac{8}{14} \times 0.8113 + \frac{6}{14} \times 1.0\right)$$

$$= 0.9402 - (0.4636 + 0.4286) = 0.9402 - 0.8922 = 0.0480 \text{ bits}$$

**Feature: Outlook** (values: Sunny, Overcast, Rain)

- Sunny: 5 samples → 2 Yes, 3 No → $H = -\frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} - \frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5} = 0.4 \times 1.3219 + 0.6 \times 0.7370 = 0.5288 + 0.4422 = 0.9710$
- Overcast: 4 samples → 4 Yes, 0 No → $H = 0$ (pure!)
- Rain: 5 samples → 3 Yes, 2 No → $H = -\frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5} - \frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} = 0.6 \times 0.7370 + 0.4 \times 1.3219 = 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.9710$

$$IG(\text{Outlook}) = 0.9402 - \left(\frac{5}{14}(0.9710) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.9710)\right)$$

$$= 0.9402 - (0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468) = 0.9402 - 0.6936 = 0.2466 \text{ bits}$$

**Feature: Humidity** (values: High, Normal)

- High: 7 samples → 3 Yes, 4 No → $H = -\frac{3}{7}\log_2\frac{3}{7} - \frac{4}{7}\log_2\frac{4}{7} = 0.4286 \times 1.2224 + 0.5714 \times 0.8074 = 0.5239 + 0.4614 = 0.9853$
- Normal: 7 samples → 6 Yes, 1 No → $H = -\frac{6}{7}\log_2\frac{6}{7} - \frac{1}{7}\log_2\frac{1}{7} = 0.8571 \times 0.2224 + 0.1429 \times 2.8074 = 0.1906 + 0.4011 = 0.5917$

$$IG(\text{Humidity}) = 0.9402 - \left(\frac{7}{14}(0.9853) + \frac{7}{14}(0.5917)\right)$$

$$= 0.9402 - (0.4927 + 0.2959) = 0.9402 - 0.7886 = 0.1516 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3: Choose root split.** Compare $IG$ values:

| Feature | Information Gain |
|---|---|
| Outlook | **0.2466** ← Maximum |
| Humidity | 0.1516 |
| Wind | 0.0480 |

**Root node = Outlook.** The "Overcast" branch is pure (all Yes). The algorithm recurses on the "Sunny" and "Rain" subsets, which is the **full ID3 procedure**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Out-of-Bag Error Estimate

Let $N$ be the number of training samples. The probability that a given sample is **not** selected in a single bootstrap draw of size $N$ (with replacement) is:

$$P(\text{not selected in one draw}) = 1 - \frac{1}{N}$$

For $N$ independent draws, the probability of never being selected is:

$$P(\text{OOB}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^N$$

Taking the limit as $N \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right)^N = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$$

This is why approximately **36.79%** of samples are OOB for any single tree — and these samples can be used as a built-in validation set, eliminating the need for explicit cross-validation.

### 3.4 Derivation: Mean Decrease in Gini (Feature Importance)

For a feature $X_j$, the importance is computed by summing the Gini impurity reductions $\Delta G(t)$ at every node $t$ in tree $T$ where $X_j$ is used as the splitting feature, then averaging across all $B$ trees in the forest:

$$FI(X_j) = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \sum_{t \in T_b : v(t) = j} N_t \cdot \Delta G(t)$$

where $v(t) = j$ means feature $X_j$ is the split variable at node $t$, and:

$$\Delta G(t) = G(t) - \frac{N_{t_L}}{N_t} G(t_L) - \frac{N_{t_R}}{N_t} G(t_R)$$

This is normalized to sum to 1 over all features: $\sum_{j=1}^{d} FI(X_j) = 1$.

### 3.5 Mathematical Insight: Bias-Variance Decomposition of Random Forest

A single fully-grown decision tree has **zero bias** (it can fit any function) but **high variance**. Bagging reduces variance without increasing bias by averaging many identically-distributed trees. The ensemble variance is:

$$\text{Var}_{\text{ensemble}} = \rho \cdot \sigma^2 + \frac{1 - \rho}{B} \cdot \sigma^2$$

where $\rho$ is the average correlation between any two trees in the forest. As $B \to \infty$:

$$\text{Var}_{\text{ensemble}} \to \rho \cdot \sigma^2 \quad \text{(for correlated trees)}$$

The clever insight: by reducing $m_{try}$ (the number of features considered at each split), we **decorrelate** the trees, reducing $\rho$, and thereby reducing ensemble variance. This is the central engineering lever in Random Forest — there is an optimal $m_{try}$ that minimizes $\text{Var}_{\text{ensemble}}$.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Tree Structure (Block Diagram)

The following block diagram illustrates the hierarchical decision flow of a single decision tree, with internal nodes performing tests and leaf nodes producing predictions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootNode["ROOT NODE: Outlook?"]
    branchSunny["Outlook = Sunny"]
    branchOvercast["Outlook = Overcast"]
    branchRain["Outlook = Rain"]
    sunnyHumid["Humidity Test"]
    sunnyHigh["Humidity = High"]
    sunnyNorm["Humidity = Normal"]
    rainWind["Wind Test"]
    rainWeak["Wind = Weak"]
    rainStrong["Wind = Strong"]
    leafNo1["LEAF: No"]
    leafYes1["LEAF: Yes"]
    leafYes2["LEAF: Yes"]
    leafNo2["LEAF: No"]
    leafYes3["LEAF: Yes"]

    rootNode --> branchSunny
    rootNode --> branchOvercast
    rootNode --> branchRain
    branchOvercast --> leafYes1
    branchSunny --> sunnyHumid
    sunnyHumid --> sunnyHigh
    sunnyHumid --> sunnyNorm
    sunnyHigh --> leafNo1
    sunnyNorm --> leafYes2
    branchRain --> rainWind
    rainWind --> rainWeak
    rainWind --> rainStrong
    rainWeak --> leafYes3
    rainStrong --> leafNo2
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each internal node (rectangle) is a test on a feature; each leaf node (rectangle with class label) is a final prediction. The tree is traversed top-down at inference time.

### 4.2 Random Forest Architecture — Functional Block Flow

The following functional architecture diagram shows the parallel training and aggregation pipeline of a Random Forest ensemble.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dataset["Original Training Dataset D of size N"]
    boot1["Bootstrap Sample 1 of size N"]
    boot2["Bootstrap Sample 2 of size N"]
    boot3["Bootstrap Sample 3 of size N"]
    bootB["Bootstrap Sample B of size N"]
    featSub["Random Feature Subspace m_try"]
    tree1["Decision Tree T1 unpruned"]
    tree2["Decision Tree T2 unpruned"]
    tree3["Decision Tree T3 unpruned"]
    treeB["Decision Tree TB unpruned"]
    oob1["OOB Set 1 ~ 0.368 of N"]
    oob2["OOB Set 2 ~ 0.368 of N"]
    oob3["OOB Set 3 ~ 0.368 of N"]
    oobB["OOB Set B ~ 0.368 of N"]
    oobErr["Aggregated OOB Error Estimate"]
    vote["Voting Mechanism"]
    final["Final Prediction: Mode or Mean"]

    dataset --> boot1
    dataset --> boot2
    dataset --> boot3
    dataset --> bootB
    featSub --> tree1
    featSub --> tree2
    featSub --> tree3
    featSub --> treeB
    boot1 --> tree1
    boot2 --> tree2
    boot3 --> tree3
    bootB --> treeB
    oob1 --> oobErr
    oob2 --> oobErr
    oob3 --> oobErr
    oobB --> oobErr
    tree1 --> vote
    tree2 --> vote
    tree3 --> vote
    treeB --> vote
    vote --> final
```

**Architecture components:**
- **Bootstrap sampling** creates diversity in training data.
- **Random feature subspace** (broadcasted to all trees) decorrelates trees.
- **Parallel tree training** allows massive scalability.
- **OOB aggregation** provides a free validation metric.
- **Voting/averaging** produces the final ensemble prediction.

### 4.3 Information Gain Computation — Algorithm Flow

The following flow diagram outlines the recursive greedy algorithm for ID3 decision tree construction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: Receive dataset S at node"]
    checkPure["Check: Is S pure or max depth reached?"]
    pureYes["Create leaf with majority class"]
    pureNo["Iterate over all features Xj and split points s"]
    calcIG["Compute Information Gain IG for each candidate split"]
    selectBest["Select split j* s* that maximizes IG"]
    splitData["Partition S into SL and SR"]
    recurseLeft["Recurse on SL"]
    recurseRight["Recurse on SR"]
    returnT["Return constructed subtree T"]

    start --> checkPure
    checkPure -->|Yes| pureYes
    checkPure -->|No| pureNo
    pureNo --> calcIG
    calcIG --> selectBest
    selectBest --> splitData
    splitData --> recurseLeft
    splitData --> recurseRight
    recurseLeft --> returnT
    recurseRight --> returnT
```
