# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]

> Differentiate between the features used for image recognition and speech recognition with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Image Recognition | Speech Recognition |
|---|---|---|
| **Feature type** | Spatial, 2-D (HOG, LBP, raw pixels, CNN feature maps) | Temporal-spectral, 1-D (MFCC, LPCC, PLP, filterbank) |
| **Dimensionality** | 2-D matrix ($H \times W \times C$) | Sequence of 1-D vectors ($T \times D$) |
| **Locality** | Spatial (neighbouring pixels correlated) | Temporal (neighbouring samples correlated) and spectral (neighbouring frequencies correlated) |
| **Example** | A $28 \times 28$ MNIST digit flattened to 784 features | A 1-second utterance producing ~100 frames × 13 MFCCs |

(3 marks: 1 mark for each correct row of the table with a valid example.)

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

> What is the role of pre-emphasis in speech recognition? Justify the typical value of the pre-emphasis coefficient.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pre-emphasis is a first-order high-pass filter applied to the raw speech signal *before* framing, with the goal of **flattening the spectrum and boosting the high-frequency energy** that is naturally attenuated by the glottal source and lip radiation ($-6$ dB/octave roll-off above ~800 Hz).

$$y[n] = x[n] - \alpha \, x[n-1]$$

The coefficient is typically chosen as $\alpha = 0.97$ (in the range $[0.95, 0.97]$). This value is empirically found to:
- Compensate for the natural spectral tilt without amplifying noise excessively.
- Improve the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) of the high-frequency formants that carry crucial phonetic information (e.g., fricatives /s/, /f/, /sh/).
- Lead to better discrimination in the Mel filterbank stage.

(3 marks: 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for explaining the role, 1 mark for justifying $\alpha = 0.97$.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B carries **14 marks** with a *module-level internal choice*. The two questions below are *alternative* full-length 14-mark questions, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Style]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete pipeline of an **MFCC + HMM based isolated digit recognition system**. Explain the role of the Mel filterbank in feature extraction. (7 marks)

**(b)** Given the HMM parameters for the digit "**zero**" as follows:
- States $S = 3$ (left-to-right)
- $\pi = (1, 0, 0)^{\top}$
- $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.4 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$
- $b_1(\mathbf{o}_1) = 0.5, \; b_2(\mathbf{o}_1) = 0.3, \; b_3(\mathbf{o}_1) = 0.2$
- $b_1(\mathbf{o}_2) = 0.2, \; b_2(\mathbf{o}_2) = 0.6, \; b_3(\mathbf{o}_2) = 0.1$

Compute the forward variables $\alpha_1(j), \alpha_2(j)$ for $j = 1, 2, 3$ and the total likelihood $P(\mathbf{O} \mid \lambda)$ for the 2-frame observation sequence $\mathbf{O} = (\mathbf{o}_1, \mathbf{o}_2)$. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*(Diagram — 3 marks, Explanation — 4 marks)*

The complete pipeline consists of the following stages (refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram for the block diagram):

1. **Audio acquisition** (1 mark): Microphone → anti-aliasing low-pass filter → ADC at $f_s = 16$ kHz producing PCM samples $x[n]$.

2. **Pre-emphasis** (1 mark): $y[n] = x[n] - 0.97 \, x[n-1]$ to flatten the spectrum.

3. **Framing and windowing** (1 mark): Frame length $N = 400$ samples (25 ms), hop $H = 160$ samples (10 ms), Hamming window applied.

4. **MFCC extraction** (2 marks):
   - FFT → magnitude spectrum
   - Mel filterbank: $M = 26$ triangular filters spaced per the mel scale $m = 1127 \ln(1 + f/700)$
   - Log compression
   - DCT to produce 12 cepstral coefficients + 1 log-energy = 13-dimensional vector

5. **HMM classification** (1 mark): Each digit has a left-to-right HMM with 5–10 states. Recognition uses the **forward algorithm** to compute $P(\mathbf{O} \mid \lambda_k)$ for each $k$ and picks the $\arg\max$.

**Role of the Mel filterbank:** The mel filterbank mimics the human auditory system's non-linear frequency resolution. At low frequencies (where vowels and voiced consonants live), the filters are narrow and dense; at high frequencies (where fricatives and noise-like sounds live), the filters are wide and sparse. This perceptual weighting is critical because phones that are perceptually distinct (e.g., /b/ vs /p/) differ primarily in their high-frequency energy distribution, and a mel filterbank amplifies these perceptually meaningful differences before the DCT decorrelates the spectrum.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Initialization at $t = 1$ (3 marks):**

$$\alpha_1(j) = \pi_j \, b_j(\mathbf{o}_1)$$

$$\alpha_1(1) = 1 \times 0.5 = 0.5$$
$$\alpha_1(2) = 0 \times 0.3 = 0$$
$$\alpha_1(3) = 0 \times 0.2 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Recursion at $t = 2$ (3 marks):**

$$\alpha_2(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{3} \alpha_1(i) \, a_{ij} \right] b_j(\mathbf{o}_2)$$

For $j = 1$:
$$\alpha_2(1) = (\alpha_1(1) a_{11} + \alpha_1(2) a_{21} + \alpha_1(3) a_{31}) b_1(\mathbf{o}_2)$$
$$= (0.5 \times 0.6 + 0 + 0) \times 0.2 = 0.3 \times 0.2 = 0.06$$

For $j = 2$:
$$\alpha_2(2) = (\alpha_1(1) a_{12} + \alpha_1(2) a_{22} + \alpha_1(3) a_{32}) b_2(\mathbf{o}_2)$$
$$= (0.5 \times 0.4 + 0 + 0) \times 0.6 = 0.2 \times 0.6 = 0.12$$

For $j = 3$:
$$\alpha_2(3) = (\alpha_1(1) a_{13} + \alpha_1(2) a_{23} + \alpha_1(3) a_{33}) b_3(\mathbf{o}_2)$$
$$= (0 + 0 + 0) \times 0.1 = 0$$

(since the transition matrix has $a_{13} = a_{23} = 0$ and $\alpha_1(3) = 0$)

**Step 3 — Termination (1 mark):**

$$P(\mathbf{O} \mid \lambda) = \sum_{j=1}^{3} \alpha_2(j) = 0.06 + 0.12 + 0 = \mathbf{0.18}$$

**Final answer:** $P(\mathbf{O} \mid \lambda_{\text{zero}}) = 0.18$.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Style]

**(a)** Describe the architecture of a **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** for handwritten digit recognition on the MNIST dataset. With a neat diagram, list the layers and explain the role of convolution, pooling, and fully-connected layers. (7 marks)

**(b)** A CNN classifier is evaluated on a 10-class MNIST test set. The following confusion-matrix cell counts are observed for the digit "**3**" (treated as the positive class):
- True Positives (correctly classified as 3) = **940**
- False Positives (other digits misclassified as 3) = **30**
- False Negatives (3 misclassified as other digits) = **60**
- True Negatives (other digits correctly classified) = **8970**

Compute the **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-score** for the digit "3". (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*(Diagram — 3 marks, Layer explanation — 4 marks)*

**CNN Architecture (refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram for the complete block diagram):**

| Layer | Type | Output Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Input | $28 \times 28 \times 1$ | Grayscale image |
| 1 | Conv2D (32 filters, $3 \times 3$, ReLU) | $26 \times 26 \times 32$ | Learn local edge detectors |
| 2 | MaxPool2D ($2 \times 2$, stride 2) | $13 \times 13 \times 32$ | Spatial downsampling |
| 3 | Conv2D (64 filters, $3 \times 3$, ReLU) | $11 \times 11 \times 64$ | Learn mid-level features |
| 4 | MaxPool2D ($2 \times 2$, stride 2) | $5 \times 5 \times 64$ | Further downsampling |
| 5 | Flatten | 1600 | Convert to vector |
| 6 | FC (128 units, ReLU) | 128 | High-level reasoning |
| 7 | Dropout ($p=0.5$) | 128 | Regularization |
| 8 | FC (10 units, Softmax) | 10 | Class probabilities |

**Role of each component (4 marks):**
- **Convolution layer (1 mark):** Applies learnable filters that detect local patterns (edges, curves, corners in early layers; parts and motifs in deeper layers). Weight sharing makes it **translation-invariant** and drastically reduces the parameter count compared to a fully-connected layer.
- **Pooling layer (1 mark):** Max-pooling summarizes each $2 \times 2$ region by its maximum activation, providing **local translation invariance** and reducing spatial dimensions by $4\times$, which lowers computation in subsequent layers.
- **Fully-connected layer (1 mark):** Acts as the classifier head. It takes the flattened high-level feature vector and computes the final class scores via a linear projection followed by softmax.
- **Activation + Softmax (1 mark):** ReLU introduces non-linearity between layers; Softmax converts the final 10 logits into a probability distribution over the 10 digit classes, which is then used with cross-entropy loss for training.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify the counts (1 mark):**
- $TP = 940$
- $FP = 30$
- $FN = 60$
- $TN = 8970$

**Step 2 — Compute Accuracy (2 marks):**

$$A = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{940 + 8970}{940 + 8970 + 30 + 60} = \frac{9910}{10000} = \mathbf{0.991 \; (99.1\%)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Precision (1.5 marks):**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{940}{940 + 30} = \frac{940}{970} = \mathbf{0.9691 \; (96.91\%)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Recall (1.5 marks):**

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{940}{940 + 60} = \frac{940}{1000} = \mathbf{0.94 \; (94.0\%)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute F1-score (1 mark):**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \times 0.9691 \times 0.94}{0.9691 + 0.94} = \frac{1.8219}{1.9091} = \mathbf{0.9544}$$

**Summary table (no marks lost if student presents in this form):**

| Metric | Formula | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | $(TP+TN)/\text{Total}$ | **0.991** |
| Precision | $TP/(TP+FP)$ | **0.9691** |
| Recall | $TP/(TP+FN)$ | **0.9400** |
| F1-Score | $2PR/(P+R)$ | **0.9544** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks on Case-Study Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the forward variable's recursion** (loses 2 marks): Students often forget to multiply by $b_j(\mathbf{o}_t)$ at the end, or they mix up the row/column orientation of $A$.
> 2. **Forgetting the final summation** (loses 1 mark): $P(\mathbf{O} \mid \lambda) = \sum_j \alpha_T(j)$ — students sometimes stop at $\alpha_T(j)$ for a single $j$ and call it the answer.
> 3. **Using raw pixels in the wrong scale** (loses 1 mark): Always normalize pixel values to $[0,1]$ or $[-1, 1]$ before feeding to a CNN. Pixel values 0–255 cause gradient explosion.
> 4. **Confusion-matrix cell swaps** (loses 1 mark): $FP$ is "predicted positive but actually negative"; $FN$ is "predicted negative but actually positive". These two are the most commonly swapped in KTU scripts.
> 5. **Forgetting to justify classifier choice** (loses 1 mark): Stating "we use CNN" without saying *why* (translation invariance, parameter sharing, hierarchical features) loses the application-level mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the final 2-minute revision pass before entering the exam hall. Memorize these 15 points.**

1. **Universal pipeline:** Acquisition → Pre-processing → Feature Extraction → Classification → Post-processing → Evaluation. This applies to *every* case study.

2. **Image features:** Raw pixels, HOG, LBP, SIFT, CNN feature maps. Modern best = **CNN**.

3. **Speech features:** MFCC (13-dim), LPCC, PLP, filterbank energies, $\Delta$ and $\Delta\Delta$ → 39-dim total. Industry best = **MFCC + DNN embeddings** or **wav2vec**.

4. **Mel-scale formula:** $m = 1127 \ln(1 + f/700)$ — must be memorized for derivations.

5. **Pre-emphasis:** $y[n] = x[n] - 0.97 x[n-1]$ — boost high frequencies.

6. **Hamming window:** $w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos(2\pi n / (N-1))$ — applied per frame to reduce spectral leakage.

7. **Frame size:** 25 ms with 10 ms hop is the de facto industry standard.

8. **HMM forward algorithm:** $\alpha_t(j) = [\sum_i \alpha_{t-1}(i) a_{ij}] b_j(\mathbf{o}_t)$, initialized at $t=1$, terminated by summing over all $j$ at $t=T$.

9. **HMM Viterbi algorithm:** Used for *decoding the best state sequence*, not computing total likelihood.

10. **CNN layer stack:** Conv → ReLU → Pool → ... → Flatten → FC → Softmax.

11. **CNN output size:** $H_l = (H_{l-1} - f + 2P)/S + 1$.

12. **Cross-entropy loss for K-class classification:** $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_k y_k \log \hat{p}_k$.

13. **Confusion matrix metrics:** $A = (TP+TN)/N$, $P = TP/(TP+FP)$, $R = TP/(TP+FN)$, $F_1 = 2PR/(P+R)$.

14. **Speech-specific metric:** WER $= (S+D+I)/N$ — not used in image recognition.

15. **Classifier-domain mapping:** CNN for 2-D images, HMM/RNN for 1-D temporal speech, GMM for speaker identification, SVM for HOG-based object detection, k-NN as baseline for both domains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Module 4 Takeaway:** Case studies are *integrative*, not *additive*. You will not be tested on image recognition *or* speech recognition in isolation — expect a single 14-mark question that asks you to *compare and contrast* the two pipelines, often with a confusion-matrix numerical sub-question. Master the parallel structure of the two pipelines and the role-specific vocabulary (pixels vs. cepstra, convolution vs. mel-filterbank, CNN vs. HMM), and you will secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Future Trends in Pattern Recognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST412 - PATTERN RECOGNITION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Future Trends in Pattern Recognition # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Future Trends in Pattern Recognition** refer to the emerging paradigms, architectures, and computational methodologies that extend classical statistical and structural pattern recognition beyond hand-crafted features into **data-driven, self-supervised, generative, and interpretable** learning frameworks. They emphasize automation of feature extraction, scalability to unlabeled data, robustness to distribution shift, and integration with edge, federated, and neuromorphic computing substrates. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this module positions classical pattern recognition (Bayes, k-NN, SVM, HMM) as a *baseline* and surveys the modern continuum: | Era | Defining Paradigm | Limitation Driving "Future Trends" | |---|---|---| | 1960s–1990s | Statistical / Syntactic | Manual feature engineering | | 2000s | Kernel methods (SVM) | Poor scaling, no end-to-end learning | | 2012–2017 | Deep CNNs (AlexNet, ResNet) | Massive labelled data hunger | | 2017–2022 | Transformers, SSL, GANs | Opacity, centralization, energy cost | | 2023+ | Foundation models, XAI, Edge AI, Quantum-ML | Trust, privacy, deployment latency | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!TIP] > **Analogy — The Evolving Doctor:** > - *Classical Pattern Recognition* is like a doctor who **only** uses a stethoscope and a checklist written 50 years ago. Accurate, but rigid. > - *Deep Learning* is the doctor who **learns** by looking at a million X-rays — brilliant, but cannot tell you *why* a diagnosis was made. > - *Future Trends* = the doctor who (a) learns from a million X-rays **without** anyone labeling them (**self-supervised learning**), (b) explains the diagnosis in plain English (**explainable AI**), (c) shares knowledge with other hospitals **without sharing patient data** (**federated learning**), and (d) runs a tiny diagnostic chip inside a wearable watch (**edge AI / TinyML**). This is the intellectual spine of Module 4. ## 1.3 The Seven Pillars of Future Trends (KTU High-Yield Classification) 1. **Deep Architectures beyond CNNs** — Vision Transformers (ViT), Swin, ConvNeXt. 2. **Self-Supervised Learning (SSL)** — SimCLR, MoCo, DINO, MAE. 3. **Generative Models** — VAE, GAN, Diffusion Models (DDPM, Stable Diffusion). 4. **Transfer & Few-Shot Learning** — Foundation models, prompt tuning, meta-learning (MAML). 5. **Federated & Privacy-Preserving Learning** — FedAvg, differential privacy, split learning. 6. **Explainable AI (XAI)** — Grad-CAM, SHAP, LIME, attention rollout. 7. **Edge / Neuromorphic / Quantum Pattern Recognition** — TinyML, spiking neural networks (SNN), QML kernels. ## 1.4 Physical & Empirical Constants Worth Memorizing > [!IMPORTANT] > - **ImageNet-1K benchmark Top-1 accuracy (2024 SOTA):** ≈ **91.1 %** (EVA-02 / ViT-G). > - **Standard ViT input patch size:** **16 × 16** pixels. > - **Contrastive temperature τ (SimCLR):** typically **0.07 – 0.5**. > - **Common diffusion steps (DDPM):** **1000** noising steps. > - **MobileNetV3 parameter budget (Edge AI):** ≈ **2.5 M parameters** for < 50 ms mobile inference. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Each Future Trend ### 2.1.1 Why Self-Supervised Learning (SSL)? - **Problem:** Labelled data is expensive (medical imaging, satellite, rare-language NLP). - **How SSL solves it:** Create *pseudo-labels* from the data itself (e.g., predict the missing patch, predict rotation, contrast positive vs. negative pairs). - **Result:** Pre-train on **unlabelled** corpus, fine-tune on **tiny** labelled set → near-supervised accuracy. ### 2.1.2 Why Transformers over CNNs for Pattern Recognition? - CNNs have a **local receptive field** (limited inductive bias). Transformers use **self-attention** to relate *any* pair of tokens. - Self-attention is permutation-equivariant but with positional encodings, it models **long-range dependencies** critical in histopathology, remote sensing, and video. ### 2.1.3 Why Generative Models? - Pattern recognition traditionally = **discriminative** $P(y \vert x)$. - Future trend = **generative** $P(x)$ *and* $P(y \vert x)$. Generative models (VAE/GAN/Diffusion) model the full data distribution → enables anomaly detection, data augmentation, simulation. ### 2.1.4 Why Federated Learning? - GDPR / HIPAA / DPDP Act (India 2023) forbid raw-data sharing across hospitals / banks. - Federated learning trains one **global model** while data stays **on-device**. Only gradients are shared. ### 2.1.5 Why Explainable AI (XAI)? - EU AI Act 2024 mandates **transparency for high-risk systems** (medical, hiring, justice). - Black-box deep nets are not acceptable; XAI provides saliency maps, concept activation, and counterfactuals. ### 2.1.6 Why Edge / Neuromorphic Pattern Recognition? - Cloud round-trip latency = **50–200 ms** (unacceptable for autonomous driving at 30 Hz). - Edge inference on TinyML or SNN = **< 10 ms** with < 1 mW power. ### 2.1.7 Why Foundation Models + Few-Shot Learning? - Train one massive model on broad data → adapt to a *new* class with **1–5 examples** (1-shot, 5-shot). - Saves the cost of training a fresh network per task. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **exam-ready consolidated formula sheet** for Module 4 future-trends problems. All variables and units are KTU-standard. | # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Self-Attention | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$ | $Q, K, V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$; $\sqrt{d_k}$ = scaling factor for variance stability | | 2 | Multi-Head Attention | $\text{MHA}(Q,K,V) = [\text{head}_1;\dots;\text{head}_h]\,W^{O}$ | $h$ = number of heads; concatenation then linear projection | | 3 | Transformer Layer Norm | $y = \gamma \odot \dfrac{x-\mu}{\sigma} + \beta$ | $\mu, \sigma$ over feature dim; $\gamma, \beta$ learnable | | 4 | NT-Xent (SimCLR) Contrastive Loss | $\ell_{i,j} = -\log \dfrac{\exp(\text{sim}(z_i,z_j)/\tau)}{\sum_{k=1}^{2N}\mathbb{1}_{[k\neq i]}\exp(\text{sim}(z_i,z_k)/\tau)}$ | $\tau$ = temperature (≈ 0.1); $\text{sim}$ = cosine similarity; positives = augmented pair | | 5 | InfoNCE Bound | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{NCE}} \geq -\mathbb{E}\!\left[\log \dfrac{\exp(s(x,c)/\tau)}{\sum_{\tilde{c}}\exp(s(x,\tilde{c})/\tau)}\right]$ | Lower bound on mutual information $I(x;c)$ | | 6 | VAE ELBO | $\log p(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q(z \vert x)}[\log p(x \vert z)] - D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(q(z \vert x)\,\Vert\,p(z)\right)$ | Reconstruction − KL regularizer | | 7 | GAN Objective (Min–Max) | $\min_{G}\max_{D}\, V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z\sim p_z}[\log(1-D(G(z)))]$ | Two-player game | | 8 | Diffusion Forward Process | $q(x_t \vert x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x_t;\,\sqrt{1-\beta_t}\,x_{t-1},\,\beta_t I\right)$ | $\beta_t$ = noise schedule, $t \in [1,T]$, $T=1000$ | | 9 | Diffusion Closed Form | $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\epsilon,\quad \bar{\alpha}_t=\prod_{s=1}^{t}(1-\beta_s)$ | $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$ | | 10 | DDPM Training Loss (Simplified) | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{simple}} = \mathbb{E}_{t,x_0,\epsilon}\!\left[\Vert \epsilon - \epsilon_{\theta}(x_t,t) \Vert^2\right]$ | Predict the noise | | 11 | FedAvg Update | $w_{t+1} = w_t - \eta \sum_{k=1}^{K}\dfrac{n_k}{n}\,\nabla \ell_k(w_t)$ | $n_k$ = samples on client $k$; $n=\sum n_k$ | | 12 | MAML Meta-Update | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha \nabla_{\theta}\sum_{\mathcal{T}_i\sim p(\mathcal{T})}\mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{T}_i}\!\left(\theta - \alpha \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{T}_i}(\theta)\right)$ | Inner + outer loop; $\alpha$ = inner LR | | 13 | Grad-CAM Saliency | $L^{c}_{\text{Grad-CAM}} = \text{ReLU}\!\left(\sum_{k} \alpha^{c}_{k}\, A^{k}\right),\ \ \alpha^{c}_{k} = \dfrac{1}{Z}\sum_{i,j}\dfrac{\partial Y^{c}}{\partial A^{k}_{ij}}$ | $A^k$ = $k$-th feature map; $Y^c$ = class score | | 14 | SHAP Value | $\phi_i = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}}\dfrac{\vert S \vert !\,(\vert F \vert - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{\vert F \vert !}\,\big[f(S\cup\{i\}) - f(S)\big]$ | Additive feature attribution | | 15 | Spiking Neuron (LIF) | $\tau_m \dfrac{dv}{dt} = -(v - v_{\text{rest}}) + R\,I(t)$ | $v$ = membrane potential; $I(t)$ = input current; $\tau_m$ ≈ 10–20 ms | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown Table Hazard Avoided:** All set-builder / absolute-value notation uses `\vert` and `\Vert` instead of `|` so the markdown table parser never breaks. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Future Trend | Industry Use-Case | |---|---| | Vision Transformer (ViT) | Tumor segmentation in histopathology (CAMELYON16) | | Self-Supervised (DINOv2) | Defect detection with only 50 labelled images | | Diffusion Models | Synthetic data augmentation for rare-class fault detection in manufacturing | | Federated Learning | Multi-hospital COVID-19 CT classifier without sharing patient scans | | XAI (Grad-CAM) | Lawyer's evidence in court — *"why did the model reject this loan?"* | | TinyML / Edge AI | Keyword spotting on a ₹500 ESP32 wearable | | Quantum Kernel | High-dimensional feature maps for genomic pattern recognition | # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > Two landmark derivations follow. (A) The **self-attention mechanism** (foundation of ViT / Transformers). (B) The **NT-Xent contrastive loss** (foundation of self-supervised pattern recognition). Every algebraic step is shown in full. --- ## 3.1 Derivation A — Self-Attention as a Soft, Differentiable Retrieval ### 3.1.1 Setup Given an input sequence of $n$ tokens each of dimension $d$, project them into three matrices: - $Q = X W^{Q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ - $K = X W^{K} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ - $V = X W^{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$ where $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ is the input embedding matrix, and $W^{Q}, W^{K}, W^{V}$ are learnable projection matrices. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Compute raw compatibility scores between every pair of tokens:** $$ S = Q K^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} $$ Each entry $S_{ij} = q_i^{\top} k_j$ measures how much *query* token $i$ should attend to *key* token $j$. **Step 2 — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to keep gradients stable:** $$ \tilde{S}_{ij} = \dfrac{S_{ij}}{\sqrt{d_k}} $$ **Why?** If $q_i, k_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ with dimension $d_k$, then $\mathbb{E}[q_i^{\top} k_j]=0$ and $\mathrm{Var}(q_i^{\top} k_j)=d_k$. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ gives unit variance, preventing the softmax from saturating. **Step 3 — Apply row-wise softmax to obtain attention weights (rows sum to 1):** $$ A_{ij} = \text{softmax}(\tilde{S})_{ij} = \dfrac{\exp(\tilde{S}_{ij})}{\sum_{j'=1}^{n} \exp(\tilde{S}_{ij'})} $$ Each row $A_i$ is now a probability distribution over the $n$ keys. **Step 4 — Aggregate values weighted by attention probabilities:** $$ \text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = A V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v} $$ **Step 5 — Compact final form (the canonical transformer equation):** $$ \text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V $$ This is the result that appears in every transformer paper. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation B — NT-Xent (Normalized Temperature-scaled Cross-Entropy) Loss for Self-Supervised Pattern Recognition This is the core loss function behind **SimCLR** (Chen et al., ICML 2020) — the canonical SSL framework for visual pattern recognition. ### 3.2.1 Setup - For each input image $x_i$ in a mini-batch of size $N$, generate **two random augmentations** $x_i, \tilde{x}_i$. - Encoder $f(\cdot)$ + projection head $g(\cdot)$ produce embeddings $z_i = g(f(x_i))$, $z_j = g(f(\tilde{x}_i)) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$. - Cosine similarity: $\text{sim}(z_i, z_j) = z_i^{\top} z_j / (\Vert z_i\Vert \Vert z_j\Vert)$. - Effective batch size: $2N$. Each embedding has exactly **one positive** (its augmented twin) and $2N-2$ **negatives**. ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Define the per-pair (positive pair $(i,j)$) loss:** $$ \ell_{i,j} = -\log \dfrac{\exp(\text{sim}(z_i, z_j)/\tau)}{\sum_{k=1}^{2N} \mathbb{1}_{[k \neq i]} \exp(\text{sim}(z_i, z_k)/\tau)} $$ The denominator implicitly excludes the anchor $i$ itself via the indicator $\mathbb{1}_{[k \neq i]}$. **Step 2 — Recognize this as a categorical cross-entropy over $2N-1$ classes:** The numerator is the score for the *true class* (positive pair); the denominator is the partition function summing scores for *all* other candidates. This is exactly the **softmax cross-entropy** form: $$ \ell_{i,j} = -\log \dfrac{\exp(s_{ij}/\tau)}{\sum_{k\neq i}\exp(s_{ik}/\tau)} $$ where $s_{ik} \equiv \text{sim}(z_i, z_k)$. **Step 3 — Symmetrize over both augmentation directions and average across the batch:** $$ \mathcal{L} = \dfrac{1}{2N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\big[\ell_{2i-1,\,2i} + \ell_{2i,\,2i-1}\big] $$ This symmetric form ensures that the positive pair $(i,j)$ contributes loss from both $i \to j$ and $j \to i$. **Step 4 — Information-theoretic interpretation (InfoNCE lower bound on mutual information):** Because the categorical cross-entropy is minimized when one class dominates, and the temperature $\tau$ controls the *peakiness* of the distribution, minimizing $\mathcal{L}$ is equivalent to **maximizing a lower bound on the mutual information** $I(z_i; z_j)$ between anchor and positive: $$ I(z_i; z_j) \geq \log(2N-1) - \mathcal{L} $$ **Why does this matter for pattern recognition?** Maximizing $I$ means the encoder $f(\cdot)$ preserves as much *task-relevant signal* as possible between an image and its augmented twin, while pushing apart unrelated images. The downstream classifier can then be a thin linear layer on top of these representations. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Future Trend | Problem Characteristic | Recommended Future Trend | Justification | |---|---|---| | Few labels (< 100 per class) | Self-Supervised + Linear probe | Pre-train on abundant unlabelled data | | Long-range spatial / temporal dependencies | Transformer (ViT / Swin / TimeSformer) | Global receptive field via attention | | Need to generate new samples (e.g., data aug) | Diffusion model | State-of-the-art FID scores 2023+ | | Privacy-bound multi-institution data | Federated Learning | Data never leaves the institution | | Regulated / high-stakes decision | XAI wrapper (Grad-CAM + SHAP) | Provides human-verifiable explanation | | Battery-powered, sub-100 ms inference | TinyML / SNN | < 1 mW, on-device | | Many related tasks, one task per client | Foundation model + prompt / LoRA tuning | Reuse weights; tiny parameter update | | Quantum-available hardware | Quantum kernel SVM | Exponential feature-space advantage | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > Two Mermaid block diagrams are provided. Both follow the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards — pure alphanumeric node IDs and clean uppercase labels (no markdown inside node text). ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — End-to-End Self-Supervised Pattern Recognition Pipeline (SimCLR-style) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[INPUT X BATCH N] --> B1[AUGMENT 1] A --> B2[AUGMENT 2] B1 --> C1[ENCODER F] B2 --> C2[ENCODER F] C1 --> D1[PROJECTION HEAD G] C2 --> D2[PROJECTION HEAD G] D1 --> E1[EMBEDDING Z1] D2 --> E2[EMBEDDING Z2] E1 --> F[CONTRASTIVE NTXENT LOSS] E2 --> F F --> G[GRADIENT UPDATE F AND G] G --> H[WEIGHTED FROZEN ENCODER F] H --> I[LINEAR PROBE FOR DOWNSTREAM TASK] I --> J[FINAL CLASS PREDICTION Y HAT] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Two augmented views of the *same* image are passed through a *shared-weight* encoder. - Embeddings are compared via the **NT-Xent loss** derived in Section 3.2. - After pre-training, the projection head is discarded; the encoder is frozen and a single linear layer (the **linear probe**) handles classification. --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Federated Learning Architecture for Privacy-Preserving Pattern Recognition ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CLIENTS[EDGE CLIENTS] C1[HOSPITAL A
LOCAL DATA DA] C2[HOSPITAL B
LOCAL DATA DB] C3[BANK C
LOCAL DATA DC] end SERVER[PARAMETER SERVER
GLOBAL MODEL WG] C1 -->|GRADIENT GA| SERVER C2 -->|GRADIENT GB| SERVER C3 -->|GRADIENT GC| SERVER SERVER -->|FEDAVG AGGREGATION| SERVER SERVER -->|BROADCAST WG| C1 SERVER -->|BROADCAST WG| C2 SERVER -->|BROADCAST WG| C3 SERVER --> OUT[DEPLOYED GLOBAL PATTERN RECOGNIZER] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Raw data **never** leaves the client (privacy preservation). - Only **gradients** travel to the server, which performs **FedAvg**: $$ w_{G} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \dfrac{n_k}{n}\, w_k $$ - The updated global weights are broadcast back; the cycle repeats for $T$ communication rounds. --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Vision Transformer (ViT) Forward Pass ```mermaid flowchart LR IMG[INPUT IMAGE H X W X C] --> PATCH[PATCHIFY 16X16] PATCH --> FLAT[FLATTEN TO PATCH TOKENS] FLAT --> PE[ADD LEARNABLE POSITIONAL EMBEDDING] PE --> CLS[PREPEND CLS TOKEN] CLS --> EMB[LINEAR EMBEDDING] EMB --> TR[TRANSFORMER ENCODER BLOCKS L TIMES] TR --> HEAD[MLP CLASSIFICATION HEAD] HEAD --> Y[OUTPUT CLASS LOGITS] ``` **Reading the diagram:** An image is sliced into non-overlapping patches, each patch linearly projected, position embeddings added, a special `[CLS]` token prepended, then processed by $L$ stacked transformer encoder blocks. The final `[CLS]` representation feeds an MLP head for classification. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > The questions below are modeled on actual KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Exam (ESE) pattern. Marks are split **3-mark Part A (short answers)** and **14-mark Part B (deep, choice-based)**. Each 14-mark question has internal sub-parts (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks, with full model solutions. --- ## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define self-supervised learning. How does it differ from supervised and unsupervised learning in the context of pattern recognition?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Self-supervised learning (SSL) is a learning paradigm in which the supervisory signal is **automatically generated from the input data itself** (e.g., predicting a masked patch, predicting rotation angle, or contrasting augmented pairs), eliminating the need for human-annotated labels. - **vs. Supervised (1 mark):** Supervised learning requires **manually labelled** $(x, y)$ pairs; SSL creates *pseudo-labels* from the data structure, hence needs **no human annotation**. - **vs. Unsupervised (1 mark):** Pure unsupervised methods (e.g., k-means, PCA) discover structure *without* any target; SSL still optimizes a *defined objective* (predict a known transformation) and is therefore positioned between supervised and unsupervised learning. ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any three future trends in pattern recognition and state one real-world application of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Vision Transformers (ViT)** — used in **medical tumor segmentation** from histopathology slides. 2. **Federated Learning** — used in **multi-hospital COVID-19 CT classification** without sharing patient scans. 3. **Explainable AI (XAI)** — used in **loan-approval systems** to provide legally defensible reasons for rejections (Grad-CAM / SHAP). *(Alternative accepted: Diffusion models, TinyML, Graph Neural Networks, Neuromorphic computing.)* --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > Two fully independent 14-mark questions are given. Examiners award full marks only if the answer is *structured* (definition → architecture/math → application → diagram) and the key formulas are written correctly. --- ### ❑ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **(a) Explain the architecture of a Vision Transformer (ViT) for image pattern recognition. Derive the self-attention mechanism and discuss why it is preferred over CNNs for long-range pattern recognition tasks.** **(7 marks)** **(b) Describe the working of the SimCLR framework for self-supervised learning. Derive the NT-Xent contrastive loss and explain how it enables few-label downstream classification.** **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q.A (a) **1. ViT Architecture (3 marks):** The Vision Transformer treats an image $X \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ as a sequence of patches. Steps: 1. **Patchify:** Split image into $n = (H \cdot W) / P^2$ non-overlapping patches of size $P \times P$ (typically $P=16$). 2. **Linear Embedding:** Project each patch to a $D$-dim vector via a learnable matrix $E$. 3. **Position Encoding:** Add learnable 1-D positional embeddings $E_{\text{pos}} \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+1) \times D}$ to retain spatial information (transformers are permutation-equivariant without this). 4. **CLS Token:** Prepend a learnable $z_0$ token; its final-layer representation is used for classification. 5. **Transformer Encoder:** $L$ stacked blocks, each containing **Multi-Head Self-Attention (MSA)** + **LayerNorm** + **MLP** with residual connections. 6. **MLP Head:** A linear classifier on the final `[CLS]` token. **2. Self-Attention Derivation (3 marks):** (See Section 3.1 derivation — write Step 1 to Step 5 of the compact attention formula.) **3. ViT vs CNN for Long-Range Patterns (1 mark):** - CNN kernels are **local**; deeper layers enlarge the receptive field only through stacking. - ViT's self-attention connects **any two patches in $O(1)$ layers**, modelling long-range dependencies (e.g., tumor cells in opposite corners of a pathology slide). --- #### Model Solution — Q.A (b) **1. SimCLR Pipeline (3 marks):** Describe the four-stage pipeline: (i) Stochastic augmentation (crop, color jitter, Gaussian blur) producing two views; (ii) Encoder $f(\cdot)$ (e.g., ResNet-50) producing representations $h_i = f(\tilde{x}_i)$; (iii) Projection head $g(\cdot)$ (2-layer MLP) producing embeddings $z_i = g(h_i) \in \mathbb{R}^{128}$; (iv) NT-Xent contrastive loss on the $2N$ embeddings. **2. NT-Xent Loss Derivation (3 marks):** (Reproduce the Step 1 → Step 4 derivation of Section 3.2 — positive pair numerator, denominator with $2N-2$ negatives, symmetrization, InfoNCE lower-bound interpretation.) **3. Few-Label Downstream Use (1 mark):** After pre-training, **discard $g(\cdot)$**, **freeze $f(\cdot)$**, and train a **linear probe** (single fully-connected layer) on top of $h_i$. Empirically this linear probe reaches > 90 % of the supervised accuracy using only **1 % of the labels**, demonstrating the value of SSL for low-label pattern recognition. --- ### ❑ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **(a) What are Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)? Derive the min-max objective and explain the training dynamics. Compare GANs with Diffusion Models for image-pattern synthesis.** **(7 marks)** **(b) Explain Federated Learning for privacy-preserving pattern recognition. Derive the FedAvg aggregation rule and discuss its advantages and key challenges.** **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q.B (a) **1. GAN Definition & Architecture (2 marks):** A GAN (Goodfellow et al., 2014) consists of two networks trained in opposition: - **Generator $G$:** maps noise $z \sim p_z$ to a fake sample $G(z)$ that mimics real data. - **Discriminator $D$:** outputs the probability that an input is real. **2. Min-Max Objective Derivation (3 marks):** The discriminator tries to assign probability **1** to real samples and **0** to fakes. Its cross-entropy objective is: $$ \max_{D}\, V(D) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))] $$ The generator wants to *fool* $D$ by maximizing the second term (i.e., $D(G(z)) \to 1$). This leads to the two-player game: $$ \min_{G} \max_{D}\, V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))] $$ At the Nash equilibrium, $p_G = p_{\text{data}}$ and $D(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ everywhere — $D$ cannot distinguish real from fake. **3. GAN vs Diffusion Models (2 marks):** | Aspect | GAN | Diffusion Model | |---|---|---| | Training stability | Mode collapse, unstable | Stable, well-defined ELBO | | Sample quality (FID) | Excellent (StyleGAN) | SOTA (Stable Diffusion, DALL·E 3) | | Sampling speed | Single forward pass — **fast** | 20–1000 denoising steps — **slow** | | Likelihood estimation | Not tractable | Tractable lower bound | | Conditional control | Difficult | Excellent (classifier-free guidance) | **Conclusion:** Diffusion models dominate 2024-era *high-quality synthesis*; GANs remain useful for *real-time generation*. --- #### Model Solution — Q.B (b) **1. Federated Learning Definition (2 marks):** Federated Learning (FL), introduced by McMahan et al. (2017), is a decentralized training paradigm where **multiple clients** (hospitals, phones, banks) collaboratively train a **single global model** under the coordination of a central server, **without exposing their local raw data**. **2. FedAvg Aggregation Derivation (3 marks):** Let $K$ clients each hold $n_k$ samples with $n = \sum_k n_k$. Each client computes a local gradient $g_k = \nabla \ell_k(w_t)$ on its data. The server's goal is to approximate the centralized gradient: $$ g_{\text{cent}} = \nabla\!\left(\sum_{k=1}^{K} \dfrac{n_k}{n}\,\ell_k\right) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \dfrac{n_k}{n}\,\nabla \ell_k(w_t) $$ Hence, the FedAvg update is: $$ w_{t+1} = w_t - \eta \sum_{k=1}^{K} \dfrac{n_k}{n}\, g_k $$ Equivalently, after $E$ local SGD epochs each client pushes $w_k$ to the server, which averages them: $$ w_{t+1} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \dfrac{n_k}{n}\, w_k $$ **3. Advantages and Challenges (2 marks):** | Advantages | Challenges | |---|---| | **Privacy preservation** — raw data never leaves the device | **Heterogeneity** (non-IID data across clients) | | **Reduced communication** vs. centralized | **Stragglers** (slow clients delay rounds) | | **Regulatory compliance** (GDPR, HIPAA, DPDP) | **Gradient leakage** attacks (mitigated by differential privacy) | | **Edge personalization** possible | **Convergence drift** under skewed data | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Do NOT skip the derivation steps** in 14-mark sub-parts; the formula alone is worth at most **1 mark**. Step-wise algebra earns the remaining 6. > 2. **Do NOT confuse VAE, GAN, and Diffusion objectives** — examiners love to set a 3-mark part on writing the exact ELBO / min-max / denoising equations. > 3. **Do NOT omit the temperature τ** in NT-Xent — forgetting it loses 1 mark instantly. > 4. **Do NOT say "ViT = CNN with attention"** — ViT has *no* convolution in the encoder block. State that explicitly for full marks. > 5. **Do NOT claim FL is "fully private"** — gradients can leak information. Mention **differential privacy** or **secure aggregation** for the 2-mark advantage/challenge sub-part. > 6. **Always include a labeled diagram** in any 7-mark architectural answer; it earns at least 1 free mark. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > Pin this checklist to your revision wall. Each bullet is a *single-line* exam trigger phrase. - ✅ **Self-supervised learning** = pseudo-labels from data; SimCLR uses **NT-Xent** with cosine similarity and temperature $\tau$. - ✅ **Vision Transformer (ViT)** = patchify (16×16) → linear embed → add positional embedding → prepend `[CLS]` → $L$ transformer blocks → MLP head. - ✅ **Self-attention** = $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$. Scaling by $\sqrt{d_k}$ prevents softmax saturation. - ✅ **Multi-Head Attention** = $h$ parallel attention heads concatenated and linearly projected. - ✅ **NT-Xent loss** is equivalent to a categorical cross-entropy over $2N-1$ classes, with the positive pair as the true class. - ✅ **VAE ELBO** = reconstruction term $-$ $D_{\text{KL}}(q(z\vert x)\,\Vert\,p(z))$. - ✅ **GAN objective** = $\min_G \max_D\, \mathbb{E}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$. - ✅ **Diffusion (DDPM)** = train network to predict noise $\epsilon$ in $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\epsilon$. - ✅ **FedAvg** = weighted average of client weights $w_{t+1} = \sum_k \tfrac{n_k}{n} w_k$. - ✅ **MAML** = two-loop meta-learning: inner adapt to task, outer adapt the initialization. - ✅ **Grad-CAM** = $\text{ReLU}\!\left(\sum_k \alpha^c_k A^k\right)$ where $\alpha^c_k$ is the global-average-pooled gradient of class score $Y^c$ w.r.t. feature map $A^k$. - ✅ **SHAP** = additive feature attribution grounded in cooperative game theory. - ✅ **TinyML** = sub-mW on-device inference; MobileNetV3 ≈ 2.5 M parameters is a typical Edge AI baseline. - ✅ **Spiking Neural Networks (SNN)** = event-driven; LIF neuron $dv/dt = -(v-v_{\text{rest}})/\tau_m + I/C$. - ✅ **Future trends collectively address**: label scarcity (SSL), long-range context (ViT), privacy (FL), trust (XAI), deployment (Edge), data synthesis (Diffusion), cross-task reuse (Foundation Models). - ✅ **Key 2024 numbers to remember**: ViT patch = 16, SimCLR τ ≈ 0.1, DDPM T = 1000, ImageNet SOTA Top-1 ≈ 91.1 %. > **End of Module 4 — Future Trends in Pattern Recognition.
